where $\eta(d)$ is a transmission efficiency function of mechanical deformation depth.

#### E. Magnetic / Inductive Sensing
A permanent magnet embedded in elastomer moves relative to a Hall-effect or magnetoresistive sensor under force, producing a measurable voltage change $\Delta V_H$ proportional to magnetic field variation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application / Engineering Use | Units |
|---------|----------------|-------------------------------|-------|
| $R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$ | $\rho$: resistivity, $L$: length, $A$: area | Baseline resistance of piezoresistive cell | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $GF$: gauge factor, $\varepsilon$: strain | Piezoresistive sensitivity metric | Dimensionless |
| $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_r$: relative permittivity, $\varepsilon_0$: $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | Capacitive touch pad design | Farads (F) |
| $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | $d_{33}$: piezo coefficient, $F$: force | Dynamic vibration/slip sensing | Coulombs (C) |
| $V = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_x + R_{ref}}$ | Voltage divider readout | Standard piezoresistive signal conditioning | Volts (V) |
| $S_d = \dfrac{\Delta C / C}{\Delta F}$ | $S_d$: differential sensitivity | Capacitive sensor force resolution | $\text{N}^{-1}$ |
| $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency | Tactile sensor bandwidth | Hertz (Hz) |
| $\tau = RC$ | Time constant | Response time of tactile cell | Seconds (s) |
| $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure definition | Sensor spatial resolution calculation | Pascals (Pa) |
| $N = \dfrac{A_{skin}}{A_{cell}}$ | Sensor count per area | Array density calculation | Count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Table (Memorize for KTU 2024):**

| Property | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Optical |
|----------|----------------|------------|---------------|---------|
| Static Force Sensing | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |
| Dynamic Sensing | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent |
| Sensitivity | Medium | High | Very High | High |
| Hysteresis | Moderate | Low | Low | Very Low |
| EMI Susceptibility | Low | Low | Very Low | Immune |
| Fabrication Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tesla Optimus & Figure 01:** Full-body capacitive tactile skins enabling safe human-robot collaboration (collision detection at < 50 ms response).
- **NASA Robonaut 2:** Piezoresistive fingertips capable of detecting 0.1 N force increments for tool manipulation in zero-gravity.
- **Shadow Robot Dexterous Hand:** Uses **magnetoresistive + optical hybrid** tactile sensors (BioTac mimicry) for object identification.
- **Surena IV (Iranian humanoid):** Distributed force sensors in feet for balance control on uneven terrain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity to Applied Force

**Problem Statement:** A humanoid fingertip uses a parallel-plate capacitive sensor with initial plate area $A_0 = 4 \,\text{mm}^2$, initial separation $d_0 = 0.5 \,\text{mm}$, dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r = 3.5$. When a normal force $F = 1 \,\text{N}$ is applied, the elastomer compresses by $\Delta d = 0.05 \,\text{mm}$. Compute the fractional change in capacitance and the differential sensitivity $S_d$.

**Step 1 — Write the baseline capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{A_0}{d_0}$$

$$C_0 = (3.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = (3.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(8 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$C_0 = 247.9 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{F}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the compressed capacitance after force application:**

The new separation becomes $d_1 = d_0 - \Delta d = 0.45 \, \text{mm}$.

$$C_1 = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{A_0}{d_1} = \frac{C_0 \cdot d_0}{d_1} = \frac{247.9 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.5}{0.45}$$

$$C_1 = 275.4 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{F}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fractional change $\Delta C / C$:**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{C_1 - C_0}{C_0} = \frac{d_0}{d_1} - 1 = \frac{0.5}{0.45} - 1 = 0.1111$$

So $\Delta C / C_0 = 11.11\%$.

**Step 4 — Compute the differential sensitivity $S_d$:**

$$S_d = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{\Delta F} = \frac{0.1111}{1 \, \text{N}} = 0.1111 \, \text{N}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A fractional change of **11.11%** for a 1 N force is exceptionally high sensitivity — this is why capacitive sensors dominate modern humanoid fingertips (e.g., SynTouch BioTac replica, MIT capacitive e-skin 2023).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoresistive Sensor Voltage Divider Readout

**Problem Statement:** A piezoresistive tactile cell has baseline resistance $R_0 = 10 \,\text{k}\Omega$. Under force $F$, its resistance drops by 20%. The cell is connected in a voltage divider with reference resistor $R_{ref} = 10 \,\text{k}\Omega$ and excitation voltage $V_s = 5\,\text{V}$. Compute the unloaded and loaded output voltages and the differential signal $\Delta V$.

**Step 1 — Unloaded output voltage $V_{out,0}$:**

$$V_{out,0} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_0}{R_0 + R_{ref}} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{10 + 10} = 2.5 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Loaded resistance:**

$$R_x = R_0 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 10 \times 0.8 = 8 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Loaded output voltage $V_{out,F}$:**

$$V_{out,F} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_x + R_{ref}} = 5 \cdot \frac{8}{8 + 10} = \frac{40}{18} = 2.222 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Differential signal:**

$$\Delta V = V_{out,0} - V_{out,F} = 2.5 - 2.222 = 0.278 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute sensitivity per Newton (assuming linear regime, $F_{max}$ = 5 N):**

$$S_V = \frac{\Delta V}{F_{max}} = \frac{0.278}{5} = 0.0556 \, \text{V/N}$$

This sensitivity is well above the noise floor of typical 12-bit ADCs ($\sim 1.22 \,\text{mV}$), confirming robust detection.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Humanoid Subsystem

| Humanoid Body Region | Recommended Sensor Type | Justification | Bandwidth Requirement |
|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Fingertip / Palm | Capacitive + Piezoelectric hybrid | Needs both static grip force & dynamic slip detection | 0–1 kHz |
| Foot sole | Piezoresistive array | Must support body weight (static) | 0–100 Hz |
| Forearm (safety skin) | Capacitive mesh | Wide-area, low-cost collision detection | 0–50 Hz |
| Knee / Joints | Strain gauge (piezoresistive) | High force, embedded measurement | 0–500 Hz |
| Face (social robot) | Optical / Quantum tunneling | Soft, fine resolution for expression | 0–200 Hz |
| Chest / Torso | Magnetic (Hall-effect) array | Mid-range sensitivity, robust to EMI | 0–100 Hz |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Tactile Array Spatial Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A humanoid's palm has area $A_{skin} = 80 \,\text{cm}^2$. Each sensor cell occupies a square area $A_{cell} = 4 \,\text{mm}^2$. Calculate the total number of sensors and compare with the human fingertip density.

**Step 1 — Convert to consistent units:**

$$A_{skin} = 80 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A_{cell} = (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute sensor count:**

$$N = \frac{A_{skin}}{A_{cell}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000 \,\text{sensors}$$

**Step 3 — Compute density per cm²:**

$$\rho_{sensor} = \frac{2000}{80} = 25 \,\text{sensors/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compare with human reference:**

The human fingertip has ~2,400 receptors/cm². Therefore, the current humanoid palm achieves approximately:

$$\frac{25}{2400} \times 100\% = 1.04\%$$

of human-equivalent spatial resolution — illustrating the massive engineering gap that motivates ongoing humanoid e-skin research.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Humanoid Tactile System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Physical Stimulus: Force, Texture, Temperature] --> B[Compliant Skin Layer: PDMS Silicone Elastomer]
    B --> C[Transduction Layer: Piezoresistive OR Capacitive OR Piezoelectric]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning: Amplifier and Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Multiplexer: 128 to 1 Analog Switch]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter: 16-bit ADC at 1 kHz]
    F --> G[Microcontroller or FPGA: Real Time Data Acquisition]
    G --> H[Perception Algorithm: CNN for Object Recognition]
    H --> I[Decision Module: Grasp Stability, Slip Prevention, Safety Stop]
    I --> J[Actuator Response: Motor Torque Adjustment]
    J --> K[Feedback to Hand Joints: Closed Loop Control]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Section of a Capacitive Tactile Skin Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Electrode: Indium Tin Oxide ITO Grid] --> B[Elastomer Dielectric: PDMS Layer thickness d]
    B --> C[Bottom Electrode: Copper or PEDOT Pattern]
    C --> D[Flexible PCB Substrate: Polyimide Kapton]
    D --> E[ASIC Readout Chip: Charge Amplifier per Cell]
    E --> F[Ground Plane and Shield Layer]
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Hierarchy: From Cell to Cognition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Taxel] --> B[Tactile Array of N Taxels]
    B --> C[Spatial Feature Map]
    C --> D[Temporal Feature Stream]
    D --> E[Convolutional Neural Network]
    E --> F[Classification Output: Object Identity, Slip Probability]
    F --> G[Behavior Layer: Adjust Grip Force]
```

### 4.4 Taxonomy of Touch Modalities in Humanoids

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Touch Sensing Taxonomy] --> B[Contact Detection]
    A --> C[Force and Pressure]
    A --> D[Slip and Shear]
    A --> E[Texture and Vibration]
    A --> F[Temperature and Thermal]
    B --> B1[Binary Threshold Switch]
    C --> C1[Static Normal Force]
    C --> C2[Static Shear Force]
    D --> D1[Micro Vibration Spectrum]
    D --> D2[Lateral Displacement Rate]
    E --> E1[High Frequency Spectral Analysis]
    F --> F1[Thermistor OR Thermocouple Based]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each `flowchart TB` and `flowchart LR` block is a Mermaid v10-compliant graph. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B1`) and labels contain only raw uppercase text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering on GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define tactile sensing. List any four transduction principles used in humanoid robotic touch sensors.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Tactile sensing is the modality by which a humanoid robot detects and quantifies physical contact with the environment through the measurement of mechanical, thermal, or vibrotactile stimuli via distributed transducers embedded in its artificial skin.

The four primary transduction principles are:
1. **Piezoresistive** — resistance change under mechanical strain.
2. **Capacitive** — capacitance change with plate separation.
3. **Piezoelectric** — charge generation under dynamic force.
4. **Optical** — light intensity modulation through deformable waveguides.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Four principles with one-line description = 2)*

---

#### **Question 2: Why cannot piezoelectric sensors reliably detect static forces? How does this limitation influence their placement on a humanoid?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Piezoelectric crystals generate an electrical charge $Q$ proportional to the *time-varying* applied force $dF/dt$, not to the absolute magnitude. When a force becomes constant, the generated charge redistributes through leakage paths, causing the output voltage to decay exponentially with time constant $\tau = RC$. Hence, **static (DC) forces produce zero sustained signal**.

**Engineering Consequence:** Piezoelectric sensors are exclusively deployed where the stimulus is dynamic — fingertip slip detection, vibration-based texture recognition, and impact detection in safety skins. They are unsuitable for the foot sole or weight-bearing joints, where piezoresistive or capacitive sensors are preferred.

*(Marks: Charge-based explanation = 2, Placement justification = 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat functional diagrams, explain the working principle of a capacitive tactile sensor. Derive an expression for its sensitivity to applied normal force. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** A humanoid fingertip integrates a $4 \times 4$ capacitive tactile array. Each cell has area $A = 2\,\text{mm}^2$, initial separation $d_0 = 0.4\,\text{mm}$, and elastomer dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r = 3.2$. Compute: (i) baseline capacitance per cell, (ii) capacitance when compressed by $\Delta d = 0.04\,\text{mm}$, and (iii) total array capacitance if all cells are compressed simultaneously.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

A capacitive tactile sensor consists of two parallel conductive plates separated by a compliant elastomeric dielectric. The elastomer acts as both the mechanical suspension and the dielectric medium. When an external normal force $F$ is applied, the elastomer compresses, reducing the plate separation from $d_0$ to $d = d_0 - \Delta d$, while the plate area $A$ remains approximately constant (negligible lateral expansion for thin elastomers).

The capacitance is:

$$C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{A}{d}$$

Under applied force $F$, the elastomer follows Hooke's law in the linear regime:

$$\Delta d = \frac{F}{k}$$

where $k$ is the effective spring constant of the elastomer column. Substituting $d = d_0 - F/k$:

$$C(F) = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{A}{d_0 - F/k}$$

The differential sensitivity (rate of change of capacitance per unit force) is:

$$S_d = \frac{dC}{dF} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{1/k}{(d_0 - F/k)^2} = \frac{C_0}{(d_0 - F/k) \cdot k}$$

In the small-signal limit where $F \ll k \cdot d_0$:

$$S_d \approx \frac{C_0}{k \cdot d_0^2} \cdot d_0 = \frac{C_0}{k \cdot d_0}$$

This shows that sensitivity is **inversely proportional** to elastomer stiffness $k$ and initial gap $d_0$. Softer elastomers and thinner initial gaps yield higher sensitivity but reduced measurement range — a fundamental design trade-off.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Baseline capacitance per cell:**

$$C_0 = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \frac{A}{d_0} = (3.2)(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = (3.2)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 141.66 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{F} \approx 141.7 \, \text{fF}$$

**(ii) Compressed capacitance per cell:**

New separation $d_1 = 0.4 - 0.04 = 0.36 \,\text{mm}$.

$$C_1 = C_0 \cdot \frac{d_0}{d_1} = 141.66 \times \frac{0.4}{0.36} = 157.4 \, \text{fF}$$

**(iii) Total array capacitance (4×4 = 16 cells in parallel):**

For simultaneous compression, assuming parallel interconnection:

$$C_{array} = 16 \cdot C_1 = 16 \times 157.4 = 2518.4 \, \text{fF} \approx 2.52 \, \text{pF}$$

*(Part a marks: Diagram + principle = 3, derivation = 4. Part b marks: i = 2, ii = 2, iii = 3)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare piezoresistive, capacitive, and piezoelectric touch sensors under the following criteria: static force sensing, dynamic force sensing, sensitivity, hysteresis, and EMI immunity. Which type is most suitable for humanoid foot sole pressure mapping? Justify. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a functional block diagram how slip detection is achieved in a humanoid fingertip using piezoelectric sensors. Discuss the role of high-frequency spectral analysis (above 100 Hz) in this process.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The comparative analysis is presented in the table below (students must reproduce a similar table in the answer script):

| Criterion | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric |
|-----------|----------------|------------|---------------|
| Static Force | Excellent (continuous $\Delta R$) | Excellent (continuous $\Delta C$) | Poor (charge decays) |
| Dynamic Force | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent (high bandwidth) |
| Sensitivity | Medium (GF ≈ 2–200) | High (sub-pF resolution) | Very High (µN-level) |
| Hysteresis | Moderate | Low | Low |
| EMI Immunity | Low (resistive measurement) | Low (capacitive coupling) | Very High (charge-based) |

**Selection for Foot Sole:** **Piezoresistive arrays are most suitable.** Justification:
- The foot must support static body weight (a humanoid of mass 60 kg produces ~600 N on each foot), requiring excellent **DC force response**.
- Piezoresistive sensors provide monotonic resistance change with sustained load.
- Array fabrication is simple using printed polymer thick films on flexible substrates.
- Cost-effective for large-area deployment across the entire foot sole (~100 cm²).

Capacitive sensors are an acceptable alternative but require complex shielding. Piezoelectric sensors are unsuitable because they cannot register the static stance phase of gait.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Slip Detection Functional Block Diagram (Textual Description for Exam Script):**

The slip detection subsystem comprises the following sequential stages:

1. **Piezoelectric Sensor (PVDF film):** Embedded in the elastomer fingertip, generates charge pulses $Q(t)$ proportional to the rate of force application.
2. **Charge Amplifier (Charge-to-Voltage Converter):** Converts $Q$ into a proportional voltage $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$.
3. **High-Pass Filter (>100 Hz):** Eliminates low-frequency grip force components and isolates slip-induced micro-vibrations.
4. **Spectral Analyzer (FFT Module):** Computes the power spectral density of the filtered signal. Slip generates characteristic spectral peaks in the **100 Hz – 1 kHz** range with high-frequency content extending to several kHz.
5. **Threshold Comparator:** If spectral energy in the slip band exceeds a calibrated threshold, the controller triggers a grip force increase of 10–30%.

**Role of High-Frequency Analysis (>100 Hz):**
During stable grip, micro-vibrations are dominated by the grip force ripple (typically <20 Hz). When slip initiates, the relative tangential motion between the fingertip and object creates high-frequency stick-slip oscillations with energy concentrated above 100 Hz. By spectrally isolating this band, the algorithm can distinguish *incipient slip* from *steady-state grip*, allowing the humanoid to react **before complete slippage occurs** — a critical capability for delicate object manipulation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Conflating "Touch" with "Force":** Touch is the umbrella modality encompassing contact, force, slip, texture, and temperature. Force is only one sub-modality. Defining "touch" as merely "force sensing" loses **2 of 3 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Omitting Units:** Any formula without SI units loses 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Skipping the Static/Dynamic Distinction for Piezoelectric:** A common pitfall is claiming piezoelectric sensors are universally superior. Always state they detect only *dynamic* stimuli.
> 4. **Missing the Justification Step:** In design-choice questions (e.g., "Which sensor for foot sole?"), simply naming the sensor without a 2–3 sentence justification loses up to **50% of design marks**.
> 5. **Diagram Omission:** KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** per Part B sub-question if the neat diagram is missing or is replaced by a verbal description alone.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Touch in humanoids** is a contact-based exteroceptive modality requiring physical interaction.
- The **four-layer architecture** is: Skin → Transducer → Readout → Perception.
- The **five transduction principles** are: piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, optical, magnetic.
- **Piezoresistive** = best for static force; **piezoelectric** = best for dynamic vibration.
- **Capacitive formula:** $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$. Sensitivity is highest for soft elastomers and thin gaps.
- **Gauge Factor:** $GF = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$ — dimensionless sensitivity metric.
- **Piezoelectric equation:** $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ — units C/N, cannot sense DC.
- **Human fingertip density:** ~2,400 receptors/cm². Current humanoids achieve ~25–500 sensors/cm².
- **Foot sole sensor selection:** Piezoresistive (static body weight support).
- **Fingertip sensor selection:** Capacitive + Piezoelectric hybrid (grip force + slip detection).
- **Slip detection band:** 100 Hz – 1 kHz spectral isolation via FFT.
- **Real-world humanoids with tactile skin:** Tesla Optimus, NASA Robonaut 2, Figure 01, Shadow Hand, Surena IV.
- **KTU 2024 must-know constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, human hand receptors ≈ 17,000.
- **Sampling rate for dynamic touch:** ≥ 1 kHz to capture slip events.
- **Hysteresis ranking (lowest to highest):** Optical < Piezoelectric < Capacitive < Piezoresistive.
- **EMI immunity ranking:** Optical > Piezoelectric > Piezoresistive ≈ Capacitive.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "Touch is **contact**, **multimodal**, and **hierarchically transduced** — remember the four-layer architecture and the static/dynamic dichotomy."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fundamentals of Humanoid Robots (PERAT744)**, *Sound* is defined as a **mechanical longitudinal pressure wave** that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by means of adiabatic compression and rarefaction of the constituent particles, carrying audible acoustic energy from a vibrating source to a receiver such as a microphone or a robotic auditory system.

From the perspective of humanoid robotics, sound encompasses two parallel engineering domains:

1. **Physical Acoustics** — the physics of pressure wave generation, propagation, reflection, absorption, and diffraction.
2. **Computational Audition** — the algorithmic extraction of semantic and spatial information from acoustic signals acquired through microphone arrays embedded in a humanoid platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Construct):**
> *Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave that requires a material medium for its propagation. In humanoid robots, sound serves as a primary modality for speech-based human–robot interaction (HRI), environmental awareness, and acoustic event detection.*

### Key Quantitative Descriptors

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C) | $c$ | $\approx 343$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Threshold of human hearing (intensity) | $I_0$ | $10^{-12}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Threshold of pain (intensity) | $I_{\text{pain}}$ | $1$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Audible frequency range (human) | $f$ | $20 - 20000$ | $\text{Hz}$ |
| Speech fundamental frequency (adult) | $F_0$ | $85 - 180$ (M), $165 - 255$ (F) | $\text{Hz}$ |
| Standard reference pressure | $p_0$ | $20$ | $\mu\text{Pa}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pond-Ripple Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)**
>
> Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. The stone pushes the water down, and that displacement travels outward as a circular ripple — a *transverse* wave. Now imagine clapping your hands in a silent room. Your hands push the air molecules together; those molecules push the molecules next to them, and so on. Unlike the water ripple (which goes up and down), the air molecules oscillate **back and forth along the direction of travel** — this is a *longitudinal* wave, and **this is sound**.
>
> For a humanoid robot, the *ears* (microphones) are simply doing what your eardrums do — sensing the tiny pressure changes in the air and turning them into electrical signals. A humanoid with two microphones (one on each side of its "head") can figure out *where* a sound is coming from, just like you can close your eyes and point toward a snapping finger.

**Why Sound Matters in Humanoid Robotics (Module-3 Context):**

- **Speech Interaction (HRI)** — Verbal communication is the most natural human interface; humanoid robots must perceive and synthesize speech.
- **Auditory Scene Analysis** — A robot must distinguish between a human voice, a slamming door, breaking glass, or a falling object.
- **Sound Localization** — Bimanual hearing (left + right ear) gives the robot 3-D spatial awareness even with eyes closed.
- **Bimodal Fusion** — Sound cues are often combined with vision (e.g., lip-reading) for robust perception.

## 1.3 Classification of Sound by Frequency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Frequency Sub-Bands in Humanoid Audio Design:**
> Engineers designing humanoid auditory systems partition the audible spectrum into operationally meaningful bands. A microphone's frequency response and the robot's DSP filters are tuned to these bands.

| Band Name | Frequency Range | Typical Source | Robotic Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infrasound | $< 20$ Hz | Earthquakes, machinery vibration | Structural monitoring |
| Speech band | $300 - 3400$ Hz | Human voice (telephony) | Speech recognition (ASR) |
| Wide-band speech | $50 - 8000$ Hz | High-fidelity voice | Modern HRI / voice assistants |
| Full audio band | $20 - 20000$ Hz | Music, environmental | Acoustic event detection |
| Ultrasound | $> 20000$ Hz | Dog whistles, sonar | Echolocation (not typical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Sound Wave

A sound wave is fully described by five mutually dependent physical quantities. The relationships among them form the **backbone of every acoustics problem** a KTU examiner can set from this module.

### 2.1.1 Wavelength ($\lambda$)

The spatial period of the wave — the distance over which the pressure pattern completes one full cycle (one compression + one rarefaction).

### 2.1.2 Frequency ($f$)

The number of complete pressure oscillations per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. One Hz equals one cycle per second.

### 2.1.3 Period ($T$)

The time taken for one complete oscillation. It is the reciprocal of frequency:

$$T = \frac{1}{f}$$

### 2.1.4 Angular Frequency ($\omega$)

The rate of change of phase in radians per second. Used heavily in DSP and impedance calculations.

$$\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

### 2.1.5 Amplitude ($A$) and Pressure Variation

The maximum displacement of particles from their equilibrium position, or equivalently, the maximum pressure deviation from ambient atmospheric pressure.

### 2.1.6 The Universal Wave Equation

The **single most important equation** in the entire module ties together speed, frequency, and wavelength:

$$v = f \lambda$$

**Why this matters in a humanoid robot:** The robot's microphone samples the pressure waveform at a fixed rate $f_s$ (sample rate). The choice of $f_s$ must obey the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

which is why humanoid audio front-ends typically operate at **$f_s = 16000$ Hz** (telephony ASR) or **$f_s = 48000$ Hz** (full-band humanoids).

## 2.2 Speed of Sound in Different Media

Sound cannot travel through a vacuum — it requires *elasticity* (a restoring force) and *inertia* (mass to be displaced). The general expression for the phase speed of a longitudinal wave in a homogeneous medium is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

where $E$ is the appropriate modulus of elasticity and $\rho$ is the medium's mass density.

| Medium | Governing Modulus | Expression for $c$ | Approximate Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal gas (adiabatic) | Bulk modulus $B = \gamma P$ | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | $\approx 343$ m/s in air @ 293 K |
| Solid (longitudinal rod) | Young's modulus $Y$ | $c = \sqrt{Y / \rho}$ | $\approx 5000$ m/s in steel |
| Liquid | Bulk modulus $B$ | $c = \sqrt{B / \rho}$ | $\approx 1480$ m/s in water |

### 2.2.1 Newton's Formula and Laplace's Correction

Newton originally proposed $c = \sqrt{P / \rho}$ for air, which gave a value of $\approx 280$ m/s — too low. Laplace corrected this by recognizing that sound compressions/rarefactions occur *adiabatically* (not isothermally) because the rapid oscillations do not allow time for heat exchange. The ratio of specific heats $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ was introduced, yielding the modern formula.

## 2.3 Sound Intensity and the Decibel Scale

Human hearing spans an enormous dynamic range — from the softest audible whisper to the threshold of pain — covering **12 orders of magnitude** in intensity. A linear scale is impractical, so the **logarithmic decibel (dB)** scale is universally adopted.

### 2.3.1 Sound Intensity ($I$)

The acoustic power transmitted per unit area, perpendicular to the direction of propagation:

$$I = \frac{P}{A} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

For a spherical wave radiating uniformly from a point source of power $P$ at distance $r$:

$$I(r) = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}$$

This is the **inverse-square law** for sound intensity.

### 2.3.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL) in Decibels

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$

where $I_0 = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^2$ is the **standard reference intensity** (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz).

### 2.3.3 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

Since microphones measure pressure, not intensity directly, the equivalent pressure-based form is:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$

with $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $20$ instead of $10$ in the pressure form?**
> Because intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I \propto p^2$), the factor of 2 comes out of the logarithm: $\log(p^2) = 2 \log(p)$.

## 2.4 Doppler Effect in Humanoid Motion

When either the sound source or the humanoid (receiver) is in motion relative to the medium, the observed frequency shifts. For a humanoid robot's microphone receiving a stationary source's sound while the robot moves with velocity $v_r$:

$$f' = f \left( \frac{c \pm v_r}{c} \right)$$

The upper sign is used when the robot moves *toward* the source (frequency increases), the lower sign when moving *away* (frequency decreases).

## 2.5 Sound Localization — The Robotic Auditory Cortex

A humanoid with two microphones mounted on its "head" can estimate the **azimuth angle** of a sound source using the **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** and **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)**.

### 2.5.1 ITD Model (for distant sources)

$$\Delta t = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c}$$

where $d$ is the inter-microphone distance, $\theta$ is the angle from the forward axis, and $c$ is the speed of sound.

### 2.5.2 ILD Model (for high-frequency sources)

The head casts an "acoustic shadow"; the far-side microphone receives a lower-intensity signal. The ILD in dB is:

$$\Delta L \approx 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot f \cdot d \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** The ITD model is only valid when $d < \lambda$ (i.e., low frequencies). For $d > \lambda$, the **spatial aliasing** ambiguity appears, which is why humanoids use *phased arrays* with $d \approx \lambda / 2$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. KTU examiners expect candidates to reproduce, identify, and apply these formulas accurately. The vertical bars are typeset with `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Universal wave relation | $v = f \lambda$ | $v$: speed, $f$: frequency, $\lambda$: wavelength |
| 2 | Period | $T = 1 \div f$ | Reciprocal of frequency |
| 3 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Radians per second |
| 4 | Speed in ideal gas | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | $\gamma$: ratio of specific heats, $R$: gas const, $T$: temp, $M$: molar mass |
| 5 | Speed in solid | $c = \sqrt{Y / \rho}$ | $Y$: Young's modulus, $\rho$: density |
| 6 | Speed in liquid | $c = \sqrt{B / \rho}$ | $B$: bulk modulus |
| 7 | Intensity (point source) | $I = P / (4\pi r^2)$ | Inverse-square law |
| 8 | Intensity level | $\beta = 10 \log_{10}\vert I / I_0 \vert$ | In decibels; $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 9 | Pressure level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\vert p / p_0 \vert$ | $p_0 = 20 \ \mu$Pa |
| 10 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Sampling theorem |
| 11 | ITD localization | $\Delta t = d \sin\theta / c$ | $\theta$: azimuth angle |
| 12 | Doppler (moving receiver) | $f' = f (c \pm v_r) / c$ | Approaching/receding sign |
| 13 | Acoustic power of a wave | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A^2 \omega^2 c S$ | $A$: amplitude, $S$: cross-section area |
| 14 | Wavenumber | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | Spatial frequency |
| 15 | Speed in air vs temperature | $c \approx 331 + 0.6 T$ | $T$ in °C, $c$ in m/s (linear approx.) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Humanoid ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition)** pipelines (e.g., Whisper, Kaldi, DeepSpeech) are designed for the speech band $f \in [300, 3400]$ Hz for telephony, or full-band for ambient humanoids.
- **Robot audition libraries** like **HARK (Honda)** and **ODAS** implement microphone-array beamforming using the ITD/ILD equations above.
- **Active noise control** in humanoids (e.g., NAO, Pepper) uses the inverse-square law to estimate and cancel ambient noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas (Laplace's Formula)

**Goal:** Starting from first principles of adiabatic gas dynamics, derive $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$.

**Step 0 — Physical Setup.**
Consider a column of gas inside a long tube. A piston at one end executes a tiny oscillation, producing a longitudinal pressure wave. The wave is a succession of infinitesimal adiabatic compressions and rarefactions.

**Step 1 — Adiabatic Equation of State.**
For an adiabatic (no heat exchange) process in an ideal gas:

$$P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating implicitly:

$$dP \cdot V^{\gamma} + P \cdot \gamma V^{\gamma - 1} dV = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = -\gamma \frac{P}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Adiabatic Bulk Modulus.**
The bulk modulus $B$ is defined as:

$$B = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

Substituting the derivative just obtained:

$$B = -V \cdot \left( -\gamma \frac{P}{V} \right) = \gamma P$$

**Step 3 — Apply the General Wave-Speed Formula.**
The speed of a longitudinal pressure wave in a fluid is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$$

Replacing $B = \gamma P$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\rho$ via the Ideal Gas Law.**
The ideal gas law in molar form is $P V = n R T$, and mass density is $\rho = n M / V$, so:

$$P = \frac{\rho R T}{M}$$

Substituting into the speed expression:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot \rho R T / M}{\rho}}$$

The $\rho$ cancels cleanly:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification in Air.**
For dry air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 293$ K, $M = 0.02897$ kg/mol.

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.02897}} \approx 343 \ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the standard tabulated value and the simplified linear approximation $c \approx 331 + 0.6 T$ (in °C) used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sound Pressure Level and Intensity Level Equivalence

**Goal:** Show that the pressure-based decibel form $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ is equivalent to $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$.

**Step 1 — Intensity–Pressure Proportionality.**
For a plane progressive sound wave, the time-averaged intensity is:

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$$

**Step 2 — Insert into the Intensity-Level Definition.**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)}{p_0^2 / (\rho c)}\right)$$

The $\rho c$ cancels because it is the **same medium** for both reference and measured waves:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Logarithm Power Rule.**

$$L_I = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{L_p = L_I = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
A 6-dB increase in SPL corresponds to a **doubling** of the RMS sound pressure, while a 20-dB increase corresponds to a **tenfold** pressure rise and a **hundredfold** intensity rise. KTU numerical problems often pivot on this logarithmic scaling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Interaural Time Difference (ITD) for Sound Localization

**Goal:** Derive the geometric relation $\Delta t = (d \sin\theta) / c$ for a two-microphone humanoid head.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup.**
Consider two microphones $M_L$ and $M_R$ separated by distance $d$, both lying on a horizontal line. A sound source $S$ is located in the far field at azimuth angle $\theta$ measured from the forward axis (the perpendicular bisector of $M_L M_R$).

**Step 2 — Path-Length Difference.**
Because the source is in the far field, the incoming wavefronts are effectively planar. The extra path length to the farther microphone (say $M_R$) is the projection of the inter-microphone vector onto the direction of arrival:

$$\Delta L = d \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Convert Path Difference to Time Difference.**
Since the wave propagates at speed $c$:

$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta L}{c} = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to Obtain the Azimuth Estimate.**

$$\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{d}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Practical Limits (KTU Frequently Asked).**

- The maximum $\Delta t$ occurs at $\theta = 90°$ (sound from the side): $\Delta t_{\max} = d / c$.
- For a humanoid with $d = 0.18$ m: $\Delta t_{\max} \approx 0.18 / 343 \approx 525 \ \mu\text{s}$.
- The robot's sampling clock must resolve this — a $48$ kHz audio card gives a $20.8 \ \mu\text{s}$ quantization, which is sufficient.
- The model breaks down when $d > \lambda/2$ (spatial aliasing at high frequencies), so the ILD cue takes over.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem.** A humanoid robot's microphone records a sound of frequency $f = 440$ Hz (concert A) in air at $T = 27$ °C. Find (a) the wavelength, (b) the period, and (c) the wavelength inside water if the robot's foot is submerged (use $c_{\text{water}} = 1480$ m/s).

**Solution.**

**(a) Speed of sound in air at 27 °C** using $c \approx 331 + 0.6 T$:

$$c_{\text{air}} = 331 + 0.6 \times 27 = 347.2 \ \text{m/s}$$

Wavelength:

$$\lambda_{\text{air}} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{347.2}{440} \approx 0.789 \ \text{m}$$

**(b) Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{440} \approx 2.273 \ \text{ms}$$

**(c) Wavelength in water:**

$$\lambda_{\text{water}} = \frac{1480}{440} \approx 3.364 \ \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The wavelength in water is over 4× longer than in air, illustrating why acoustic localization underwater requires larger microphone spacings.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sound vs. Other Waves

| Property | Sound (in air) | Light (in vacuum) | Water Surface Ripple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wave type | Longitudinal | Transverse EM | Transverse mechanical |
| Medium required? | Yes | No | Yes (water) |
| Typical speed | $343$ m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s | $\sim 0.3$ m/s (gravity waves) |
| Frequency range | $20 - 20000$ Hz | $4 \times 10^{14} - 7.5 \times 10^{14}$ Hz | $0.1 - 5$ Hz |
| Audible/visible? | Audible | Visible | Visible |
| Used in humanoid sensing? | **Yes (audition)** | **Yes (vision)** | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Humanoid Audio Processing Pipeline

The following flow chart maps the complete signal chain from a physical sound event in the environment to a high-level action decision inside a humanoid robot's cognitive layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Sound Source in Environment] --> nodeB[Acoustic Wave Propagation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Left Microphone Capsule]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Right Microphone Capsule]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Pre-amplifier Stage]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Pre-amplifier Stage]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Analog to Digital Converter]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Analog to Digital Converter]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Digital Signal Processor]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Noise Reduction Filter]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Feature Extraction Module]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Sound Localization Engine]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Speech Recognition Engine]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Acoustic Event Classifier]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Robot Decision Module]
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP[Robot Actuator Response]
    nodeO --> nodeQ[Speech Synthesis Output]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The two parallel microphone paths implement the **binaural hearing** model used by humanoid auditory systems.
- The DSP block (nodeI) is the heart of the system, where sampling, filtering, and feature extraction (e.g., MFCC) occur.
- Three parallel perceptual streams — localization, speech, and event classification — feed a central decision module that drives actuators and speech output.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sound Wave Physical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Sound Source] -->|Vibration| nodeP[Pressure Variation in Medium]
    nodeP -->|Compression| nodeC[High Pressure Region]
    nodeP -->|Rarefaction| nodeR[Low Pressure Region]
    nodeC --> nodeW[Wavelength lambda]
    nodeR --> nodeW
    nodeW -->|Propagation| nodeM[Microphone Receiver]
    nodeM -->|Electrical Signal| nodeDSP[Robot Audio Front End]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The chain emphasizes the physical cause-effect sequence: mechanical vibration → alternating compression/rarefaction → wavelength-scale spatial pattern → transduction to electricity at the microphone → entry into the robot's digital processing chain.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sound Localization Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Acquired Stereo Audio] --> nodeB[Frame Segmentation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Cross-Correlation]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Detect Peak Lag tau]
    nodeD --> nodeE{tau within bounds}
    nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Compute Azimuth theta]
    nodeE -->|No| nodeG[Use ILD Fallback]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Emit Angle Estimate]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Update Robot World Map]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The cross-correlation peak in nodeC directly yields the **Interaural Time Difference**; the ILD fallback path is engaged when the signal is dominated by high-frequency components where ITD is ambiguous.

---

## 4.4 Acoustic Propagation Medium Comparison (Visual Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Sound Propagation] --> nodeY[Gas e.g. Air]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Liquid e.g. Water]
    nodeX --> nodeW2[Solid e.g. Steel]
    nodeY --> nodeY1[c about 343 m per s]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1[c about 1480 m per s]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW3[c about 5000 m per s]
```

This concept map reinforces the Section 2.2 table, illustrating the monotonic relationship between medium density/elasticity and the resulting acoustic speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define sound intensity and intensity level. State the threshold of hearing in $\text{W/m}^2$ and explain why the decibel scale is used in humanoid audio engineering.**

**Model Answer (3-mark schema):**

- **Sound intensity ($I$):** Acoustic power transmitted per unit perpendicular area, $I = P/A$, measured in $\text{W/m}^2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Intensity level ($\beta$):** Logarithmic measure defined as $\beta = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ in decibels (dB), with reference intensity $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ \text{W/m}^2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Why decibel:** Human hearing spans 12 orders of magnitude in intensity; a linear scale is impractical. The dB scale compresses this range, mirrors the ear's logarithmic response, and matches the digitised dynamic range (e.g., 16-bit ADC) of robot microphone front-ends. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**What is meant by sound localization? Mention the two principal cues used by humanoid robots for binaural sound localization.**

**Model Answer (3-mark schema):**

- **Sound localization:** The process by which a humanoid robot estimates the spatial direction (azimuth and elevation) of a sound-emitting object using its microphone array. **(1 mark)**
- **Cue 1 — Interaural Time Difference (ITD):** Difference in arrival time of the wave at the two microphones; dominant at low frequencies ($f < 1500$ Hz) and given by $\Delta t = d \sin\theta / c$. **(1 mark)**
- **Cue 2 — Interaural Level Difference (ILD):** Difference in intensity at the two microphones due to the head's acoustic shadow; dominant at high frequencies. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions on a specific module offer an internal choice (e.g., "Answer **any one** of the following"). Two fully-worked alternative question options are provided below so you can practise both.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

#### (a) Derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas. State the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The gas is **ideal** and obeys the equation of state $P V = n R T$.
2. The compressions and rarefactions are **adiabatic** (no heat transfer between adjacent gas slabs) because the oscillations are too rapid for thermal equilibration.
3. The wave is a small-amplitude, longitudinal, plane-progressive disturbance.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the adiabatic condition $P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$, differentiate:

$$V^{\gamma} dP + \gamma P V^{\gamma - 1} dV = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = -\gamma \frac{P}{V}$$

The adiabatic bulk modulus is $B = -V \, dP/dV = \gamma P$.

The longitudinal wave speed in a fluid is $c = \sqrt{B / \rho}$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$

Using the ideal gas law $P = \rho R T / M$:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}}$$

**Verification:** For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, $M = 0.029$ kg/mol, $T = 300$ K gives $c \approx 347$ m/s. ✓

#### (b) Explain the binaural sound localization technique used in humanoid robots. Derive the ITD equation and discuss its physical limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Setup (2 marks):** A humanoid with two microphones separated by distance $d$ can mimic biological binaural hearing. A sound from azimuth $\theta$ reaches the near microphone first; the time lag is the ITD cue.

**Derivation of ITD (3 marks):** For a far-field source, the wavefront is planar. The path-length difference is $\Delta L = d \sin\theta$. Dividing by wave speed $c$:

$$\Delta t = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{c \, \Delta t}{d}\right)$$

**Limitations and Practical Mitigations (2 marks):**
- Valid only when $d < \lambda/2$ (spatial aliasing otherwise).
- ITD is ambiguous for high frequencies; ILD takes over.
- Requires precise clock synchronisation between microphone channels.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

#### (a) Explain the decibel scale. Derive the relationship between sound intensity level and sound pressure level, highlighting the factor-of-2 difference in the log expressions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Sound intensity level (SIL) in dB is $\beta = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$, with $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ \text{W/m}^2$. Sound pressure level (SPL) is $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20 \ \mu$Pa.

**Derivation of the factor-of-2 (5 marks):** For a plane progressive wave, $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)$. Substituting into SIL:

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)}{p_0^2 / (\rho c)}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$

The factor 2 emerges because intensity is proportional to the square of pressure, and $\log(x^2) = 2 \log(x)$.

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the audio signal processing pipeline in a humanoid robot, starting from sound acquisition to action decision. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):** The pipeline consists of the following stages (see Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Acoustic Stage:** Sound propagates as a longitudinal pressure wave.
2. **Transduction Stage:** Two microphones convert pressure to voltage; pre-amplifiers boost the signal.
3. **Digitisation Stage:** ADCs sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist), typically 16 kHz or 48 kHz.
4. **DSP Stage:** Digital filters remove noise; feature extraction computes MFCCs or spectrograms.
5. **Perception Stage:** Three parallel engines — sound localization, ASR, and acoustic event classification — produce semantic outputs.
6. **Decision Stage:** A fusion module (often probabilistic, e.g., Bayesian or neural) integrates the auditory cues with vision and proprioception.
7. **Action Stage:** The robot issues motor commands (turn head, navigate) and/or speech synthesis outputs.

**Key Justification (3 marks):** The dual-microphone design provides spatial hearing; the parallel perception engines mirror the human auditory cortex's parallel processing streams; the fusion step exemplifies multimodal integration essential for robust HRI.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in 'Sound' Module Questions (per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f$ with $\omega$** — many students write $\omega = 1/f$ instead of $T = 1/f$. Examiner deducts **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** in $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ when numerical substitution is required. A *plug-and-chug* with $T$ in °C yields a wrong answer and an automatic **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Using $10 \log$ for pressure and $20 \log$ for intensity** — reversed by rote memorization. Always derive from $I \propto p^2$.
> 4. **Omitting the "adiabatic" justification** in the derivation of the speed of sound in a gas. The Laplace correction is **2 marks** by itself.
> 5. **Writing $\theta = \sin^{-1}(d \Delta t / c)$ without the arcsin inverse** — the standard answer is $\theta = \arcsin(c \Delta t / d)$.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Nyquist criterion** when discussing the choice of $f_s$ in humanoid ASR systems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the 'Sound' Module (Last-Night Revision)**

- **Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave** that requires a material medium; it cannot travel through vacuum.
- The **universal wave equation** $v = f \lambda$ is the single most-tested relation; commit it to memory with units.
- **Speed of sound in air** at 20 °C is **343 m/s**; it scales as $\sqrt{T}$ (in Kelvin) and is **independent of pressure and frequency** (for audible ranges).
- The **adiabatic assumption** (Laplace correction) distinguishes the correct formula $c = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$ from Newton's incorrect $c = \sqrt{P / \rho}$.
- **Sound intensity** $I = P / A$ follows an **inverse-square law** for a point source: $I \propto 1/r^2$.
- The **decibel scale** is logarithmic: $10 \text{ dB} = $ tenfold intensity; $20 \text{ dB} = $ tenfold pressure.
- Use $10 \log(I / I_0)$ for intensity-level; use $20 \log(p / p_0)$ for pressure-level — the factor of 2 reflects $I \propto p^2$.
- Reference values: $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ \text{W/m}^2$, $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$, audible range $20 \text{ Hz} - 20 \text{ kHz}$.
- The **Doppler effect** $f' = f (c \pm v_r) / c$ applies when a humanoid receiver moves relative to a stationary source.
- **Binaural localization** uses **ITD** (low-freq) and **ILD** (high-freq) cues: $\Delta t = d \sin\theta / c$.
- **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ dictates the ADC clock rate of every humanoid audio front-end.
- For humanoids, **ASR**, **sound localization**, and **acoustic event detection** are the three principal applications of sound perception.
- Modern humanoid platforms (NAO, Pepper, ASIMO, Optimus, Sophia) all carry **multi-microphone arrays** to enable spatial hearing and robust HRI.
- Always annotate numerical answers with units; a numerically correct answer without units forfeits marks under KTU valuation norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tactile Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMID HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tactile Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tactile Sensing** in humanoid robotics is defined as the continuous, real-time acquisition, transduction, and interpretation of mechanical contact information—including normal force, shear force, vibration, texture, temperature, and contact geometry—at the physical boundary (skin) between the robot and its environment. It is the engineering equivalent of the human sense of touch, achieved through a distributed array of transducers that convert mechanical stimuli into quantifiable electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Tactile sensing refers to the detection and measurement of physical interaction parameters (force, pressure, vibration, slip, temperature) through contact-based transducers embedded in the robot's outer surface, enabling the robot to perceive and react to its immediate physical surroundings.

Mathematically, a generalized tactile response can be expressed as the mapping:

$$ S(t) = f(F_n, F_t, T, \nu, A) $$

where $F_n$ is the normal force, $F_t$ is the tangential (shear) force, $T$ is temperature, $\nu$ is the vibration/slip frequency, and $A$ is the contact area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person reaching into a dark bag to identify a key. They cannot see the object, but their fingertips can:
- Feel the **shape** (geometry)
- Sense the **sharpness** of edges (force gradients)
- Detect the **material** (texture/friction)
- Notice **slippage** (dynamic feedback)

This entire experience is replicated in a humanoid robot by **tactile sensors** embedded in its skin, fingertips, palms, and feet. Just as human skin contains four types of mechanoreceptors (Meissner, Pacinian, Merkel, Ruffini), a robotic tactile system contains a heterogeneous array of transducers each specialized for a particular stimulus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Tactile sensing is *not* the same as force/torque sensing at the joint. Joint force sensors measure internal loads on the actuator, whereas tactile sensors measure *external* contact at the robot's surface. This distinction is a common exam trap.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following quantitative parameters define the quality of a tactile sensor and are **high-yield KTU exam terms**:

- **Spatial Resolution (mm):** Minimum distance between two distinguishable contact points. Human fingertip ≈ **1 mm**; advanced e-skin ≈ **0.5 mm**.
- **Sensitivity (V/N or pF/N):** Ratio of electrical output to applied mechanical input.
- **Dynamic Range (N):** Ratio between the minimum detectable force and the maximum measurable force, often expressed in decibels.
- **Hysteresis (%):** Difference in output between the loading and unloading cycle at the same input force.
- **Response Time (ms):** Time delay between stimulus application and stable sensor output.
- **Force Range (N):** Operational interval, typically **0.01 N** (light touch) to **100 N** (manipulation).
- **Power Consumption (mW):** Critical for battery-powered humanoid platforms.

> [!TIP]
> **Human Reference Baseline (for comparison in exams):** Human skin has ~**241 receptors/cm²** in the fingertip, sensitivity down to **~10 mg** of force, and a frequency response from near-DC up to **~1000 Hz** (Pacinian corpuscles).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Tactile Sensors

Tactile sensors can be broadly classified along three independent axes. KTU examiners frequently ask the student to draw or tabulate this hierarchy.

### A. Classification by Transduction Principle

1. **Resistive (Piezoresistive):** Resistance changes with applied force. Uses conductive elastomers, FSRs (Force-Sensitive Resistors), or carbon nanotube composites.
2. **Capacitive:** Capacitance changes due to gap ($d$) or overlap area ($A$) variation.
3. **Piezoelectric:** Generates charge proportional to applied stress (dynamic only).
4. **Optical:** Measures light intensity modulation through deformable waveguides.
5. **Magnetic / Hall Effect:** Detects displacement of a magnet embedded in elastomer.
6. **MEMS-Based:** Silicon microfabricated diaphragms with piezoresistive or capacitive readouts.
7. **Electrochemical / Iontronic:** Uses electric double layers (EDL) for ultra-high sensitivity.

### B. Classification by Structural Form

- **Single-Cell (Discrete) Sensors:** One transducer per taxel.
- **Array Sensors:** $m \times n$ matrix of taxels (tactile elements), e.g., $16 \times 16$ fingertip arrays.
- **E-Skin / Large-Area Flexible Sensors:** Distributed sensor sheets covering the entire robot body.
- **Barometric / Suction Sensors:** Used in specialized grippers for delicate objects.

### C. Classification by Measured Quantity

- **Force / Pressure:** Static contact force.
- **Shear / Slip:** Tangential motion detection.
- **Vibration / Texture:** High-frequency micro-vibrations during sliding.
- **Temperature:** Thermal coupling with the environment.
- **Proximity / Pre-Contact:** Capacitive or infrared pre-touch sensing.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step

### Principle 1: Piezoresistive Sensor

When a conductive elastomer is compressed, its volume resistivity $\rho$ decreases because conductive pathways multiply. The equivalent resistance is:

$$ R = \frac{\rho L}{A} $$

A voltage divider converts this resistance change into a measurable voltage:

$$ V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_{taxel}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Piezoresistive sensors are simple, low-cost, and easily fabricated as arrays, but they suffer from hysteresis and temperature drift—common viva questions.

### Principle 2: Capacitive Sensor

A parallel-plate capacitor with a deformable dielectric:

$$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} $$

When force compresses the dielectric, $d \rightarrow d - \Delta d$, producing a fractional capacitance change:

$$ \frac{\Delta C}{C} = \frac{\Delta d}{d - \Delta d} $$

Capacitive sensors offer **low power consumption, high sensitivity, and good dynamic range**, making them the preferred choice for humanoid fingertips.

### Principle 3: Piezoelectric Sensor

The direct piezoelectric effect produces a surface charge density:

$$ D = d_{33} \cdot \sigma $$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (e.g., PVDF: $\approx 33$ pC/N) and $\sigma$ is the applied stress. The open-circuit voltage is:

$$ V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure static forces** because the generated charge leaks away through the finite input impedance of the readout circuit. They are inherently AC-coupled and ideal only for vibration/slip detection.

### Principle 4: Optical Sensor

Light from an LED travels through a compliant elastomeric waveguide. Internal deformation causes **frustrated total internal reflection (FTIR)** or intensity attenuation, captured by a photodiode or CMOS camera. Used in the GelSight family of sensors.

### Principle 5: MEMS Tactile Sensor

A micromachined silicon diaphragm with embedded piezoresistors forms a Wheatstone bridge. Applied pressure causes diaphragm deflection, generating an output voltage:

$$ V_{out} = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$

These are batch-fabricated, miniaturized, and offer excellent linearity and bandwidth.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy

- **Why multiple principles coexist:** No single transduction method simultaneously satisfies sensitivity, bandwidth, flexibility, power, and cost. Humanoid platforms use **heterogeneous** sensor suites.
- **Why e-skin is hard:** A robot has ~**1.8 m²** of effective skin surface; covering this with millions of taxels demands *flexible*, *stretchable*, and *multimodal* electronics.
- **Why redundancy is built in:** Critical contact points (fingertips, palms, soles) have overlapping sensor modalities to survive single-point failures.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Standard Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|------------------------|--------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \,/\, d$ | F (Farad) | Capacitive taxel design |
| 2 | Capacitive sensitivity | $S_c = \dfrac{1}{C}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\dfrac{1}{d}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Predicts dynamic range |
| 3 | Fractional capacitance change | $\Delta C / C = \Delta d / (d - \Delta d)$ | dimensionless | Linearization design |
| 4 | Piezoresistive voltage divider | $V_{out} = V_{cc} R_s / (R_s + R_{taxel})$ | V (Volt) | FSR readout circuits |
| 5 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{33} F$ | C (Coulomb) | Dynamic force sensing |
| 6 | Piezoelectric open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = d_{33} F / C_p$ | V (Volt) | Charge-amplifier sizing |
| 7 | Wheatstone bridge output | $V_{out} = V_{exc} (\Delta R / R)$ | V (Volt) | MEMS pressure readouts |
| 8 | Optical intensity (Beer-Lambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | W/m² | Optical tactile sensors |
| 9 | Sensor SNR | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(S_{\text{signal}} / S_{\text{noise}})$ | dB | Threshold-of-touch design |
| 10 | Spatial frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz | Sampling rate selection |
| 11 | Hysteresis error | $e_H = \vert V_{up} - V_{down} \vert \,/\, V_{FS}$ | % | Calibration standard |
| 12 | Slip detection frequency | $f_{\text{slip}} = \mu v / \pi r$ | Hz | Incipient slip estimation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize equations (1), (2), (5), and (11) — these appear in nearly every Part B question on tactile sensing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetic Limbs:** i-Limb, bebionic hands use tactile arrays to modulate grip force on fragile objects (an egg, a paper cup).
- **Humanoid Manipulation:** Tesla Optimus, Figure 02, and 1X Neo use tactile feedback to grasp unstructured objects.
- **Surgical Robotics:** da Vinci instruments incorporate tactile feedback to compensate for the loss of haptic sensation in minimally invasive surgery.
- **Service Robotics:** Pepper, NAO, and ASIMO use foot tactile sensors to maintain balance on uneven terrain.
- **Industrial Cobots:** Universal Robots' e-Series uses joint torque + fingertip tactile fusion for safe human–robot collaboration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **high-weightage analytical core**. KTU board examiners consistently test derivations on capacitive sensitivity, piezoelectric voltage, and the slip-frequency relation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity and Linearization

**Problem Statement:** Derive the sensitivity of a parallel-plate capacitive tactile sensor and show under what conditions the response becomes linear.

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal parallel-plate capacitance:**

$$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} $$

where $d$ is the plate separation (initial value $d_0$), $A$ is the overlap area, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric.

**Step 2 — Assume the area $A$ is constant and the dielectric is elastomeric (Poisson ratio $\nu$ treated as constant).** Then $C$ is a function of $d$ alone. Apply an applied normal force that compresses the gap by an amount $\Delta d$, so the new gap is $d = d_0 - \Delta d$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the governing equation:**

$$ C(d) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the capacitance change $\Delta C = C(d) - C(d_0)$:**

$$ \Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right) $$

**Step 5 — Bring the terms over a common denominator:**

$$ \Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{d_0 - (d_0 - \Delta d)}{d_0(d_0 - \Delta d)} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_0(d_0 - \Delta d)} $$

**Step 6 — Form the fractional change $\Delta C / C$:**

$$ \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d} $$

**Step 7 — Define sensitivity $S_c$ as the derivative of normalized capacitance with respect to normalized displacement:**

$$ S_c = \frac{d(\Delta C / C_0)}{d(\Delta d / d_0)} = \frac{1}{(1 - \Delta d / d_0)^2} $$

**Step 8 — Linearization by small-displacement approximation $\Delta d \ll d_0$:**

For small compressions, $(1 - \Delta d / d_0)^2 \approx 1$, giving:

$$ \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} $$

The output is now **linear in displacement**, and hence approximately linear in applied force (since for an elastomer $F \approx k \Delta d$ via Hooke's law).

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d} \;\;\xrightarrow{\Delta d \ll d_0}\;\; \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoelectric Open-Circuit Voltage and Frequency Limitation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the open-circuit voltage of a piezoelectric tactile element and explain why it cannot measure DC/static force.

**Step 1 — Piezoelectric constitutive relation (direct effect, 33-mode):**

$$ D_3 = d_{33} \, T_3 + \varepsilon_{33}^{T} E_3 $$

where $D_3$ is the electric displacement, $T_3$ is the applied stress, $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric coefficient, and $E_3$ is the electric field.

**Step 2 — Under open-circuit boundary condition, $D_3 = Q / A_{elec}$ where $A_{elec}$ is the electrode area:**

$$ Q = d_{33} \, T_3 \, A_{elec} = d_{33} F $$

**Step 3 — The generated charge sits on the inherent self-capacitance $C_p$ of the piezo element:**

$$ C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{33}^{T} A_{elec}}{t_p} $$

where $t_p$ is the piezo film thickness.

**Step 4 — Solve for open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = Q / C_p$:**

$$ V_{oc} = \frac{d_{33} F t_p}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{33}^{T} A_{elec}} $$

**Step 5 — Identify the proportionality constant as the *piezoelectric voltage coefficient* $g_{33} = d_{33} / \varepsilon_{33}^{T}$:**

$$ \boxed{V_{oc} = g_{33} \, t_p \, \sigma = g_{33} \, t_p \, \frac{F}{A_{elec}}} $$

**Step 6 — Why static sensing fails:** Any practical readout circuit has a finite input resistance $R_{\text{in}}$ and inherent capacitance. The piezo + input form an RC high-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi R_{\text{in}} C_p)$. For force variations slower than $f_c$, the charge leaks away faster than it accumulates, so $V_{oc} \rightarrow 0$. Therefore, **only AC/dynamic stimuli are measurable** by direct piezoelectric effect.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Incipient Slip Detection Frequency

**Problem Statement:** Derive the characteristic vibration frequency generated when a tactile sensor first detects slip on a gripped object.

**Step 1 — Consider a spherical fingertip of radius $r$ in contact with a flat surface, under a normal load $N$.** The contact patch is a circle of radius $a$ given by Hertz contact mechanics:

$$ a = \left( \frac{3 N r}{4 E^*} \right)^{1/3} $$

where $E^*$ is the combined elastic modulus.

**Step 2 — The tangential traction (shear stress) required to initiate sliding is governed by Coulomb's law:**

$$ F_t = \mu N $$

where $\mu$ is the static coefficient of friction.

**Step 3 — During incipient slip, micro-vibrations occur at the asperity contact. The wavelength of these vibrations is of order $\lambda \approx 2 \pi r$ (stick–slip oscillation around the contact patch).**

**Step 4 — The slip frequency is therefore:**

$$ f_{\text{slip}} = \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{v}{2 \pi r} $$

**Step 5 — Substituting $v = \mu g t$ (gravity-driven slip with $g$ acceleration over time $t$):**

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{slip}}(t) = \frac{\mu g t}{2 \pi r}} $$

**Step 6 — Engineering implication:** By sampling tactile data at $f_s \geq 4 f_{\text{slip}}$ and using a high-frequency piezoelectric taxel, the robot can detect slip **~50–200 ms** before it becomes catastrophic, allowing grip force to be increased.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Optical | MEMS |
|----------|----------------|------------|---------------|---------|------|
| Static force | Excellent | Excellent | **Poor (AC only)** | Excellent | Excellent |
| Dynamic range | Medium | High | Very high | High | High |
| Hysteresis | High | Low | Low | Low | Very low |
| Spatial resolution | 1–2 mm | 0.5–1 mm | <0.5 mm | <0.1 mm (camera-based) | ~50 µm |
| Power consumption | Medium | Low | Zero (passive) | High (LED+CMOS) | Low |
| Flexibility | High (polymer) | High | High | Limited | **Poor (rigid Si)** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Medium |
| Multimodal sensing | Limited | Easy | Limited | Excellent | Limited |
| Typical humanoid use | Palm, sole | Fingertip | Vibration/Slip | Fingertip (high-res) | Joint, finger pad |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Rule of Thumb:** If a question says "design a fingertip for fine manipulation," the answer is **capacitive + piezoelectric hybrid**. If it says "design a foot sole for balance," the answer is **piezoresistive array**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Tactile Sensing System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full signal chain from physical contact through to a closed-loop control decision in a humanoid robot.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Contact: Force, Slip, Temp, Texture] --> B[Tactile Transducer Array: taxels on skin]
    B --> C[Analog Front End: charge amplifier or bridge amp]
    C --> D[Multiplexer and ADC: digitize each taxel]
    D --> E[Signal Processing: filtering, feature extraction]
    E --> F[Feature Bank: pressure map, slip flag, vibration spectrum]
    F --> G[Tactile Fusion Module: combines modalities]
    G --> H{Decision Layer}
    H -->|slip detected| I[Increase Grip Force Command]
    H -->|object stable| J[Maintain Grasp]
    H -->|thermal alert| K[Withdraw or Replan]
    I --> L[Actuator and Motor Controller]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Hand Reacts in Real Time]
    M -.feedback.-> A
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Tactile Sensor Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Tactile Sensors] --> S1[By Transduction Principle]
    R --> S2[By Physical Form]
    R --> S3[By Stimulus Modality]

    S1 --> P1[Piezoresistive: FSR, conductive elastomer]
    S1 --> P2[Capacitive: parallel plate, EDL]
    S1 --> P3[Piezoelectric: PVDF, PZT]
    S1 --> P4[Optical: FTIR, GelSight]
    S1 --> P5[Magnetic: Hall, magnetorheological]
    S1 --> P6[MEMS: silicon diaphragm]

    S2 --> F1[Single Taxel]
    S2 --> F2[Array: 16x16, 32x32]
    S2 --> F3[E-Skin: large area flexible sheet]
    S2 --> F4[Barometric: suction based]

    S3 --> M1[Normal Force and Pressure]
    S3 --> M2[Shear and Slip]
    S3 --> M3[Vibration and Texture]
    S3 --> M4[Temperature]
    S3 --> M5[Pre-Contact Proximity]
```

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning Pipeline for a Capacitive Fingertip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Deformable Dielectric] --> X2[Capacitance Change dC]
    X2 --> X3[Charge Amplifier: C to V conversion]
    X3 --> X4[Band-Pass Filter: 0.1 Hz to 1 kHz]
    X4 --> X5[16-bit ADC: 1 MSps]
    X5 --> X6[Digital Decimation and Feature Extraction]
    X6 --> X7[Pressure Map 16x16 to Host Processor]
    X7 --> X8[Grasp Stability Classifier]
    X8 --> X9[Grip Force Update Command]
```

## 4.4 Bio-Inspiration Mapping: Human Skin to Robotic Skin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Human Mechanoreceptors] --> H2[Meissner: light touch, 30 to 50 Hz]
    H1 --> H3[Merkel: pressure, static, low freq]
    H1 --> H4[Ruffini: stretch, DC to 15 Hz]
    H1 --> H5[Pacinian: vibration, 100 to 1000 Hz]

    H2 --> R1[Robotic Piezoelectric Sensor]
    H3 --> R2[Robotic Capacitive Sensor]
    H4 --> R3[Robotic Resistive Stretch Sensor]
    H5 --> R4[Robotic MEMS Accelerometer on Skin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The bio-inspired mapping above is one of the most frequently-asked 7-mark questions in KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations. Memorize the receptor name, the stimulus type, and the frequency band, then map each to a corresponding robotic sensor principle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Define tactile sensing in humanoid robots. List any four key performance parameters used to characterize a tactile sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

Tactile sensing is the process of detecting and measuring mechanical contact parameters such as normal force, shear force, vibration, slip, and temperature at the outer surface of a humanoid robot, emulating the human sense of touch.

The four key performance parameters are:
1. **Spatial Resolution** — minimum distance between two distinguishable contact points (typically **1 mm** for fingertips).
2. **Sensitivity** — output signal per unit applied input force (e.g., V/N).
3. **Dynamic Range** — ratio of maximum to minimum measurable force, expressed in **dB**.
4. **Response Time** — delay between stimulus application and stable electrical output (typically **1 to 10 ms**).

*(Examiner's Note: Full 3 marks require definition + four labeled parameters with units. Naming them without units loses 1 mark.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Compare piezoelectric and piezoresistive tactile sensors on the basis of (i) static force measurement capability, (ii) power consumption, and (iii) hysteresis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| (i) Static force | **Cannot measure** static force; AC-coupled only | **Excellent** static capability |
| (ii) Power consumption | **Zero passive** sensing power | Continuous excitation current required |
| (iii) Hysteresis | Very low (typically <2%) | High (5%–15%) |

*(Examiner's Note: Tabular format fetches full marks. Long paragraphs lose 1 mark for lack of clarity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of a capacitive tactile sensor with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for its sensitivity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A capacitive tactile sensor consists of two conductive plates separated by a soft elastomeric dielectric. When an external normal force is applied to the upper plate, the dielectric compresses, reducing the gap $d$ between the plates. Since capacitance is inversely proportional to gap, this produces a measurable increase in capacitance.

**Diagram (described in text):** Two parallel horizontal plates with the upper one connected to a force input, a soft elastomer layer in between, and output leads connected to a charge amplifier.

**Derivation:** (as already shown in Section 3.1 of these notes)

Starting from $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, applying $\Delta d$ compression, the fractional capacitance change is:

$$ \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d} $$

and the normalized sensitivity is:

$$ S_c = \frac{1}{(1 - \Delta d / d_0)^2} $$

For small $\Delta d \ll d_0$, $S_c \approx 1$ and the response is linear: $\Delta C / C_0 \approx \Delta d / d_0$.

**Engineering Justification:** Capacitive sensors are favored in humanoid fingertips because they offer low power consumption, low hysteresis, and good high-frequency response, while being readily fabricable as flexible arrays using PDMS or Ecoflex dielectrics.

**(b) [7 Marks] With a block diagram, describe the architecture of a humanoid robot tactile sensing system. Discuss how slip is detected and prevented.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — the controller chain is: Transducer Array → Analog Front-End → Multiplexer/ADC → Digital Signal Processing → Tactile Fusion → Decision Layer → Actuator Command.)*

**Slip Detection Mechanism:**
1. The piezoelectric taxel continuously monitors high-frequency micro-vibrations at the contact interface.
2. A sliding Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is computed over a sliding window of ~10 ms.
3. The dominant frequency is compared against the incipient-slip frequency $f_{\text{slip}} = \mu g t / (2 \pi r)$.
4. When spectral energy in the 50–500 Hz band exceeds an empirically-set threshold, an *incipient-slip flag* is raised.
5. The decision layer commands the gripper to increase the normal force by $\Delta F = k_p \cdot (\text{slip magnitude})$ within **20–50 ms**, well before the object fully slides.
6. A complementary *texture signature* is extracted from the same FFT to identify the material being manipulated (e.g., metal vs. paper vs. fabric).

**Prevention Strategy:** A Proportional-Integral (PI) controller adjusts grip force to keep the slip spectral energy just below the threshold, balancing grasp security against excessive compressive force that could damage fragile objects.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare the various transduction principles used in tactile sensors. State one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Principle | Working Summary | Advantage | Limitation |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Piezoresistive** | Resistance changes when conductive elastomer is compressed | Simple, low cost, easy array fabrication | High hysteresis, temperature drift |
| **Capacitive** | Gap/area change alters capacitance | Low power, high sensitivity, low hysteresis | Susceptible to EMI, needs shielding |
| **Piezoelectric** | Stress generates charge | Zero passive power, wide bandwidth | Cannot sense static force |
| **Optical (FTIR/GelSight)** | Deformation modulates light | Ultra-high spatial resolution, multimodal | Bulky, high power, expensive |
| **MEMS** | Piezoresistors on silicon diaphragm | Batch-fabricated, precise, linear | Rigid, brittle, small area |
