| **Magnetic/Hall** | Magnet displacement detected by Hall sensor | Robust, immune to dust/oil | Susceptible to external magnetic fields |

**Conclusion:** No single principle dominates. Modern humanoid e-skin uses a **hybrid stack** of capacitive (static force) + piezoelectric (vibration) + temperature sensors for multimodal coverage.

**(b) [7 Marks] Draw the bio-inspired mapping between human skin receptors and robotic tactile sensors. Justify the choice of sensor type for humanoid fingertips.**

**Model Solution:**

**Bio-Inspired Mapping:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes.)*

- **Meissner corpuscles** (light touch, 30–50 Hz) → **Piezoelectric taxels** for vibration/slip detection.
- **Merkel discs** (sustained pressure, DC–15 Hz) → **Capacitive arrays** for static pressure mapping.
- **Ruffini endings** (skin stretch, DC–15 Hz) → **Resistive stretch sensors** for shear force and curvature estimation.
- **Pacinian corpuscles** (high-frequency vibration, 100–1000 Hz) → **MEMS accelerometers or high-bandwidth piezo films** for texture recognition.

**Justification for Fingertip Sensor Choice:**
Human fingertips require both **fine static resolution** (to grasp a pen without crushing it) and **fast dynamic response** (to detect incipient slip within tens of milliseconds). Therefore, the optimal humanoid fingertip design is a **capacitive + piezoelectric hybrid array**, typically $16 \times 16$ or $32 \times 32$ taxels, integrated on a flexible PCB and covered by a silicone rubber skin. This matches human performance across the full ~0.01 N–50 N force range and 0–1000 Hz frequency band.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Tactile Sensing Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "force sensor" with "tactile sensor"** — Joint torque sensors measure internal loads; tactile sensors measure external contact. Examiner deducts **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Stating "piezoelectric sensors can measure static force"** — This is factually wrong. The charge leaks through finite input impedance. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting units in formulas** — A derivation of capacitive sensitivity without specifying units loses **1 mark** under KTU 2024 OBE rubrics.
> 4. **Skipping the linearization step** — Writing $\Delta C / C = \Delta d / d$ without justifying $\Delta d \ll d_0$ is a **1.5-mark penalty** because it misses the non-linearity discussion.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — KTU 2024 Scheme mandates at least one schematic. A textual description alone loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Memorizing only the equation, not the engineering interpretation** — A derivation without concluding "this is why capacitive sensors are preferred for humanoid fingertips" loses **1 mark** under the application-level Bloom's rubric.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Tactile sensing = external contact measurement** (distinct from joint force sensing).
- [x] **Human skin has four receptor types** — Meissner, Merkel, Ruffini, Pacinian — mapped to piezo, capacitive, stretch, and high-frequency piezo/MEMS sensors respectively.
- [x] **Five major transduction principles** — Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Optical, MEMS.
- [x] **Master equation: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$** with sensitivity $S_c = 1/(1 - \Delta d/d_0)^2$.
- [x] **Piezoelectric sensors are AC-only** — They cannot measure static forces due to charge leakage.
- [x] **Slip detection frequency** is given by $f_{\text{slip}} = \mu g t / (2 \pi r)$, allowing **50–200 ms early warning** before catastrophic slip.
- [x] **Hybrid sensor design is mandatory** in modern humanoids — capacitive for static + piezo for dynamic.
- [x] **Key metrics** — spatial resolution, sensitivity, dynamic range, hysteresis, response time, force range.
- [x] **Hysteresis error formula** — $e_H = \vert V_{up} - V_{down} \vert / V_{FS}$.
- [x] **Spatial sampling rule** — $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist) for tactile data acquisition.
- [x] **E-skin must be flexible, stretchable, multimodal, low-power, and dense** to cover the ~1.8 m² humanoid surface.
- [x] **Industry relevance** — Tesla Optimus, Figure 02, 1X Neo, ASIMO, and modern prosthetic hands all rely on tactile sensing for dexterous manipulation.
- [x] **Memorize the four-class taxonomy** — by principle, by form, by stimulus, and by application region (fingertip, palm, sole, body).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Audition # Audition in Humanoid Robots ## Core Technical Definition > [!NOTE] > **Audition** in humanoid robotics is the computational emulation of the biological hearing process, enabling a robot to **capture, decode, localize, and interpret acoustic signals** from its environment. It encompasses the entire signal-processing pipeline from raw pressure-wave acquisition (via microphones) to high-level semantic understanding (such as speech recognition and speaker identification). In the context of KTU's PERAT744 syllabus, audition is treated as the **sensory counterpart to vision**, forming one of the two principal cognitive perception channels that allow a humanoid robot to engage meaningfully with humans. The discipline merges three engineering pillars: 1. **Acoustics** (physics of sound propagation) 2. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** (filtering, transformation, feature extraction) 3. **Machine Intelligence** (pattern recognition, speech-to-text, NLP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight):** > - Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **v = 343 m/s** > - Human audible frequency range: **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** > - Speech-relevant bandwidth: **300 Hz – 3,400 Hz** > - Reference acoustic pressure (threshold of hearing): **p₀ = 20 μPa** --- ## Intuitive Overview — The "Analogy" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Robot as a Listener at a Noisy Party"** > > Imagine you are standing in a crowded auditorium trying to hear your friend call your name. Your ears capture sound waves from every direction; your brain instantly filters the crowd noise, locates the direction your friend's voice is coming from, recognizes the words, and produces a meaning — all within a fraction of a second. > > A humanoid robot's audition system performs the *exact same feat*, but with mathematical precision: > - **Ears → Microphone Array** (captures sound) > - **Eardrum → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** (transduces & samples) > - **Cochlea → DSP Filterbank** (frequency decomposition) > - **Auditory Nerve → Feature Vectors (MFCCs)** (neural encoding) > - **Auditory Cortex → Speech Recognition Engine** (interpretation) > - **Brain's Spatial Maps → Beamforming / DoA Estimation** (localization) This is why **audition is non-negotiable** for social humanoids like **Pepper, NAO, ASIMO, and Sophia** — without it, natural verbal communication becomes impossible. --- ## Why Audition Matters for Humanoids | Human Need | Robotic Equivalent | Engineering Outcome | |---|---|---| | Conversational dialogue | Speech Recognition (ASR) | Voice commands, social interaction | | Detecting danger (horn, glass breaking) | Acoustic Event Detection | Safety reflexes | | Identifying who spoke | Speaker Identification | Personalization, security | | Knowing where sound came from | Sound Source Localization (SSL) | Gaze/head orientation, attention | | Enjoying music / recognizing emotion | Audio Scene Analysis | Affective computing | --- ## Biological Inspiration vs. Robotic Implementation The human auditory system is **bilaterally symmetric** (two ears), and the brain computes spatial cues via: - **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** — microsecond timing differences between ears. - **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** — intensity differences due to head shadowing. Humanoid robots replicate this using **microphone arrays** (typically 2, 4, or 8 microphones) arranged geometrically to compute the same cues via algorithms like **GCC-PHAT** (Generalized Cross-Correlation with Phase Transform). # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Sound — Foundation Layer Sound is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** propagating through a compressible medium via alternating compressions and rarefactions of molecules. ### Core Wave Equation for Sound Pressure For a one-dimensional plane wave traveling along the x-axis through a homogeneous medium: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial^{2} p(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}} &= \frac{1}{c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} p(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $p(x,t)$ = instantaneous acoustic pressure (Pa) at position $x$ and time $t$ - $c$ = speed of sound in the medium (m/s) - For air at 20°C: $c \approx \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$ ### Why This Matters for Robots The wave equation is the **governing law** that determines how a robot's emitted vocalization (for vocal cords / speaker feedback) propagates, and inversely, how incoming sound is structured when captured. Any DSP applied to microphone data assumes this harmonic structure. --- ## 2.2 The Acoustic Signal Processing Pipeline (KTU High-Priority) A humanoid robot's audition subsystem follows this **5-stage functional pipeline**: ### **Stage 1: Acoustic Acquisition** Microphones (MEMS, electret, or condenser) convert pressure variations into analog voltage signals. Typical humanoid robots use **2 to 8 microphones** mounted on the head. ### **Stage 2: Analog Conditioning & Sampling** Anti-aliasing low-pass filter → ADC sampling at $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion). For speech: $f_s = \mathbf{16 \; kHz}$ (covers 0–8 kHz). For full audio: $f_s = \mathbf{44.1 \; kHz}$ or **48 kHz**. ### **Stage 3: Pre-processing** - Noise suppression (spectral subtraction, Wiener filter) - Echo cancellation (AEC) - Automatic Gain Control (AGC) ### **Stage 4: Feature Extraction** - **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** → spectrogram - **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs)** → 12–13 coefficients per frame - **Filter banks** mimicking cochlear frequency response ### **Stage 5: Recognition & Interpretation** - Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) — classical ASR - Deep Neural Networks (DNNs, RNNs, Transformers) — modern ASR - Gaussian Mixture Models — speaker ID - DOA estimators — GCC-PHAT, MUSIC, ESPRIT --- ## 2.3 Sound Source Localization (SSL) — Mathematical Core ### **A. Interaural Time Difference (ITD) Model** For two microphones separated by distance $d$ (head width ≈ 18 cm for adult-sized humanoid): $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta t &= \frac{d \cdot \sin(\theta)}{c} \end{aligned} $$ Where $\theta$ = angle of incidence relative to the broadside (perpendicular) direction. ### **B. Direction of Arrival (DoA) Estimation** Inverting the above for angle: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{d} \right) \end{aligned} $$ ### **C. Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ILD}_{\text{dB}} &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_{\text{near}}}{P_{\text{far}}} \right) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **ITD dominates below 1.5 kHz** (wavelength > head size), while **ILD dominates above 1.5 kHz** (head creates acoustic shadow). This is the **Duplex Theory of Sound Localization** — a guaranteed KTU exam topic. --- ## 2.4 Acoustic Feature Extraction — MFCC Derivation Outline MFCCs are computed via these sequential operations: 1. **Pre-emphasis:** Boost high frequencies to balance spectrum. 2. **Framing:** Split signal into 20–40 ms windows with 50% overlap. 3. **Hamming Windowing:** Reduce spectral leakage. 4. **FFT:** Convert each frame to frequency domain. 5. **Mel Filterbank:** Apply triangular filters spaced on the Mel scale. 6. **Logarithm:** Mimic human loudness perception. 7. **DCT:** Decorrelate filterbank energies. The Mel scale conversion is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} m &= 1127 \cdot \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{f}{700} \right) \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Audition Module** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wave relation | $v = f \lambda$ | v in m/s, f in Hz, λ in m | | 2 | Speed of sound in air | $c \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | T in °C, c in m/s | | 3 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | $p_0 = 20 \; \mu Pa$, L_p in dB SPL | | 4 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \; W/m^2$ | | 5 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Prevents aliasing | | 6 | Interaural Time Diff. | $\Delta t = (d \sin\theta)/c$ | d = mic separation | | 7 | Direction of Arrival | $\theta = \arcsin(c \Delta t / d)$ | Valid for $-90° \leq \theta \leq 90°$ | | 8 | Interaural Level Diff. | $\text{ILD} = 20 \log_{10}(P_{\text{near}}/P_{\text{far}})$ | In dB | | 9 | Mel scale | $m = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$ | f in Hz | | 10 | Inverse Mel | $f = 700 \left( e^{m/1127} - 1 \right)$ | m in Mels | | 11 | STFT window width | $\tau \approx 20 - 40 \; \text{ms}$ | Speech frames | | 12 | Reverberation time (Sabine) | $RT_{60} = 0.161 \cdot V/A$ | V=volume, A=absorption | > All `vert`-style absolute values have been replaced with explicit formulas above to keep the table parser-safe. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Audition in humanoid robots is foundational for: - **Healthcare Robotics**: Detecting distress calls, coughing, fall sounds. - **Service Robotics**: Voice-controlled assistants in hotels/hospitals. - **Industrial Cobots**: Responding to verbal safety commands ("Stop!"). - **Educational Robots**: Speech therapy assistance, language tutoring. - **Search & Rescue**: Locating survivors by voice/groaning in rubble. - **Social Robotics**: Empathy-based interaction via emotion detection in voice. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Microphone Signal to MFCC Feature Vector This is the **gold-standard derivation** for KTU Part B questions on auditory feature extraction. ### **Given:** A discrete-time audio signal $x[n]$ sampled at $f_s = 16 \; kHz$, representing a 1-second utterance. We want to extract a sequence of MFCC feature vectors. ### **Step 1 — Pre-emphasis Filter** Compensate for the natural roll-off of high frequencies in speech: $$ \begin{aligned} y[n] &= x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1], \quad \alpha \in [0.95, 0.97] \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* Subtracting a fraction of the previous sample emphasizes the high-frequency components, flattening the spectrum — analogous to the human cochlea's active amplification. ### **Step 2 — Framing** Divide the signal into overlapping frames of length $N$ samples with hop size $M$: $$ \begin{aligned} x_k[n] &= y[n] \cdot \mathbf{1}[M \cdot k \leq n < M \cdot k + N] \end{aligned} $$ Typical values: $N = 400$ samples (25 ms at 16 kHz), $M = 160$ samples (10 ms hop). ### **Step 3 — Windowing (Hamming Window)** Multiply each frame by the Hamming window to reduce spectral leakage: $$ \begin{aligned} w[n] &= 0.54 - 0.46 \cdot \cos\!\left( \frac{2\pi n}{N-1} \right), \quad 0 \leq n \leq N-1 \end{aligned} $$ Windowed frame: $$ \begin{aligned} x_w[n] &= x_k[n] \cdot w[n] \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 4 — FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** Convert each windowed frame to frequency domain: $$ \begin{aligned} X_k[m] &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_w[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi m n / N}, \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1 \end{aligned} $$ Compute magnitude (power) spectrum: $$ \begin{aligned} P_k[m] &= \frac{1}{N} \vert X_k[m] \vert^{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 5 — Mel Filterbank** Apply $L$ triangular filters spaced on the Mel scale. The output of filter $\ell$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} S_k[\ell] &= \sum_{m=0}^{N/2} P_k[m] \cdot H_\ell[m], \quad \ell = 1, 2, \ldots, L \end{aligned} $$ Where $H_\ell[m]$ is the triangular gain of filter $\ell$ at frequency bin $m$. ### **Step 6 — Log Compression** Apply logarithm to mimic human loudness perception (which is logarithmic): $$ \begin{aligned} \tilde{S}_k[\ell] &= \ln\!\left( S_k[\ell] + \epsilon \right) \end{aligned} $$ ($\epsilon$ is a small constant to avoid $\ln(0)$.) ### **Step 7 — Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** Produce decorrelated cepstral coefficients: $$ \begin{aligned} c_k[i] &= \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} \tilde{S}_k[\ell] \cdot \cos\!\left( \frac{\pi i (\ell - 0.5)}{L} \right), \quad i = 1, \ldots, D \end{aligned} $$ Typically $D = 12$ or **13** MFCCs per frame. ### **Final Output** A 1-second utterance at 16 kHz with 25 ms frames and 10 ms hop produces: - Number of frames = $\lfloor (16000 - 400) / 160 \rfloor + 1 = \mathbf{99 \; frames}$ - Each frame yields a 13-dimensional MFCC vector. - Total feature tensor: $99 \times 13 = \mathbf{1287 \; coefficients}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: GCC-PHAT for Time Delay Estimation GCC-PHAT (Generalized Cross-Correlation with Phase Transform) is the **industry-standard algorithm** for computing TDoA (Time Difference of Arrival) between two microphones. ### **Given:** Two microphone signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, with sampling rate $f_s$, microphone separation $d$. ### **Step 1 — Cross-Correlation in Time Domain** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{12}[\tau] &= \sum_{n} x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n+\tau] \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 2 — Transform to Frequency Domain** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{12}[\tau] &= \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega) \right\} \end{aligned} $$ Where $X_2^{*}(\omega)$ is the complex conjugate of the FFT of $x_2$. ### **Step 3 — Apply PHAT Whitening** Normalize the cross-power spectrum by its magnitude: $$ \begin{aligned} \Psi_{12}(\omega) &= \frac{X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega)}{\vert X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega) \vert} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 4 — Inverse FFT to Get GCC-PHAT** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{PHAT}}[\tau] &= \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ \Psi_{12}(\omega) \right\} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 5 — Locate Peak** The time delay $\hat{\tau}$ is the lag at which $R_{\text{PHAT}}[\tau]$ peaks: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\tau} &= \arg\max_{\tau} \; R_{\text{PHAT}}[\tau] \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 6 — Compute Direction of Arrival** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} \right) \end{aligned} $$ > *Logic:* PHAT whitening flattens the spectrum, suppressing reverberation effects and emphasizing the direct-path timing peak. This makes the algorithm robust in noisy indoor environments — critical for humanoid robots operating in homes and offices. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — DoA Calculation ### **Problem:** A humanoid robot has two microphones on its head separated by $d = 0.20 \; m$. The speed of sound is $c = 343 \; m/s$. GCC-PHAT estimates a time delay of $\hat{\tau} = 250 \; \mu s$. Find the direction of arrival. ### **Solution:** **Step 1:** Substitute into the DoA formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{343 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-6}}{0.20} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} c \cdot \hat{\tau} &= 343 \cdot 0.000250 = 0.08575 \; m \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Divide by d: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} &= \frac{0.08575}{0.20} = 0.42875 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Apply arcsine: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin(0.42875) \approx 25.4^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The sound source is located approximately **25.4° to the right** of the robot's broadside (forward-facing perpendicular axis). The robot's head should rotate by this angle to align with the speaker. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!TIP] > **Comparison of Sound Localization Techniques (KTU High-Yield Table)** | Technique | Principle | Spatial Resolution | Computational Cost | Robustness | |---|---|---|---|---| | ITD-based | Time difference between ears | High at low freq. | Low | Moderate | | ILD-based | Intensity difference | High at high freq. | Low | Moderate | | GCC-PHAT | Whitened cross-correlation | High | Moderate | High (reverb-tolerant) | | MUSIC | Subspace eigenvalue decomp. | Very high (super-resolution) | Very high | Moderate | | ESPRIT | Rotational invariance | Very high | High | Moderate | | Beamforming (MVDR) | Adaptive filter steering | High | Moderate | High | | Deep Learning (CNN/RNN) | Learned spectral cues | High | Inference only | Very high (with training data) | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Auditory Processing Pipeline — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Acoustic Source
Human Voice / Event] --> B[Microphone Array
2 to 8 MEMS Mics] B --> C[Analog Preamp
and Anti Alias Filter] C --> D[ADC
Sample at 16 to 48 kHz] D --> E[Noise Suppression
AEC and AGC] E --> F[Feature Extraction
MFCC or Spectrogram] F --> G{Switch on Task} G --> H[ASR Engine
Speech to Text] G --> I[SSL Module
GCC PHAT or MUSIC] G --> J[Speaker ID
GMM or DNN] H --> K[NLU Layer
Intent and Entity] K --> L[Robot Response
Action or Speech] I --> M[Head Orientation
Motor Control] J --> N[User Profile
Personalization] L --> O[Humanoid Behavior
Social Interaction] M --> O N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Microphone Array Geometry for Sound Localization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Head[Humanoid Robot Head] M1[Mic 1 - Left Ear] M2[Mic 2 - Right Ear] M3[Mic 3 - Forehead] M4[Mic 4 - Rear] end M1 --- M2 M2 --- M3 M3 --- M4 M4 --- M1 S[Sound Source
at angle theta] -.Delay tau1.-> M1 S -.Delay tau2.-> M2 S -.Delay tau3.-> M3 S -.Delay tau4.-> M4 ``` ## 4.3 MFCC Feature Extraction — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Audio Signal x of n] --> B[Pre emphasis
y n = x n - alpha x n minus 1] B --> C[Framing
25 ms windows
10 ms hop] C --> D[Hamming Window
Spectral Leakage Reduction] D --> E[FFT
N point DFT] E --> F[Power Spectrum
P of m] F --> G[Mel Filterbank
20 to 40 Filters] G --> H[Log Compression
Log S of L] H --> I[DCT
Discrete Cosine Transform] I --> J[MFCC Vector
12 to 13 Coefficients] J --> K[Delta and Delta Delta
Dynamic Features] K --> L[Feature Tensor
Frame x Coefficient] ``` ## 4.4 Duplex Theory — Frequency vs. Localization Cue ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incoming Sound Wave] --> B{Frequency Range} B -->|Below 1.5 kHz| C[ITD Dominates
Wavelength greater than head size
Time cues reliable] B -->|Above 1.5 kHz| D[ILD Dominates
Head shadowing
Level cues reliable] C --> E[Brain / Processor Combines Both Cues] D --> E E --> F[Perceived Sound Direction] ``` ## 4.5 Human vs. Robot Auditory System — Architectural Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Bio[Human Biological System] H1[Outer Ear Pinna] H2[Eardrum] H3[Cochlea] H4[Auditory Nerve] H5[Brainstem ITD ILD] H6[Auditory Cortex] end subgraph Bot[Robot Engineering System] R1[MEMS Microphone Array] R2[ADC and Preamp] R3[DSP Filterbank] R4[Feature Pipeline MFCC] R5[GCC PHAT MUSIC] R6[ASR DNN Engine] end H1 -.Functional equivalent.-> R1 H2 -.Functional equivalent.-> R2 H3 -.Functional equivalent.-> R3 H4 -.Functional equivalent.-> R4 H5 -.Functional equivalent.-> R5 H6 -.Functional equivalent.-> R6 ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define audition in the context of humanoid robotics. List any two functional applications of audition in social humanoid robots.** **Model Answer:** Audition is the engineering discipline that enables a humanoid robot to **capture, process, and interpret acoustic signals** from its environment, replicating the human sense of hearing. **Two functional applications:** 1. **Speech Recognition (ASR):** Allows verbal commands and natural dialogue interaction with humans. 2. **Sound Source Localization (SSL):** Enables the robot to identify the spatial origin of sounds (e.g., a calling human) and orient its head and gaze accordingly. > *Additional valid applications:* acoustic event detection (horn, glass break), speaker identification, emotion recognition from prosody, music recognition. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the Duplex Theory of sound localization? State the frequency boundary that separates its two regimes.** **Model Answer:** The Duplex Theory states that the human auditory system (and bio-inspired robotic systems) uses **two complementary cues** to localize sound in the horizontal plane: 1. **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** — dominant at low frequencies. 2. **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** — dominant at high frequencies. **Boundary frequency: ≈ 1.5 kHz** (Below this, wavelength > head diameter, so the head does not cast an acoustic shadow. Above this, head shadowing creates measurable level differences.) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer ONE question from a Module Internal Choice (either Q.A or Q.B). Each carries 14 marks split across two 7-mark sub-parts.** --- ### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 14 Marks #### **(a) Explain with diagrams the GCC-PHAT algorithm for time delay estimation in a humanoid robot's microphone array. Derive the relevant equations. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** The Generalized Cross-Correlation with Phase Transform (GCC-PHAT) is a robust algorithm used by humanoid robots to estimate the **Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)** between two microphones. It is highly robust against reverberation and ambient noise, making it ideal for indoor social robots. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. **Capture** signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ from the two microphones. 2. **Compute FFT** of both signals to obtain $X_1(\omega)$ and $X_2(\omega)$. 3. **Cross-power spectrum:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{12}(\omega) &= X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega) \end{aligned} $$ 4. **PHAT whitening** — divide by magnitude: $$ \begin{aligned} \Psi_{12}(\omega) &= \frac{X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega)}{\vert X_1(\omega) \cdot X_2^{*}(\omega) \vert} \end{aligned} $$ 5. **Inverse FFT** to obtain the GCC-PHAT function: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{PHAT}}(\tau) &= \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{ \Psi_{12}(\omega) \right\} \end{aligned} $$ 6. **Peak detection:** $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\tau} &= \arg\max_{\tau} \; R_{\text{PHAT}}(\tau) \end{aligned} $$ 7. **Direction of Arrival:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Why PHAT:** The whitening operation normalizes the spectrum magnitude to unity across all frequencies, leaving only the phase information. This suppresses the influence of strong spectral peaks (which often correspond to reverberant components) and emphasizes the direct-path timing peak. **Block Diagram:** ``` Mic1 ──► FFT ──► Cross-multiply ──► |·|⁻¹ ──► IFFT ──► Peak ──► τ ↑ Mic2 ──► FFT ──► Conjugate ───────────┘ ``` #### **(b) A humanoid robot has two microphones separated by 17 cm. The speed of sound is 340 m/s. If GCC-PHAT estimates a peak at τ = 380 μs, compute the angle of arrival of the sound source. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $d = 0.17 \; m$ - $c = 340 \; m/s$ - $\hat{\tau} = 380 \times 10^{-6} \; s$ **Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin\!\left( \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Compute numerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} c \cdot \hat{\tau} &= 340 \cdot 380 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1292 \; m \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Divide by d:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{c \cdot \hat{\tau}}{d} &= \frac{0.1292}{0.17} \approx 0.760 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply arcsine:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \arcsin(0.760) \approx 49.5^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret sign:** Positive $\hat{\tau}$ conventionally indicates the sound arrived **later** at the second microphone. Depending on microphone labeling, the source is approximately **49.5°** to one side of the robot's forward axis. **Answer: $\theta \approx 49.5^{\circ}$** --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 14 Marks #### **(a) Explain the Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) feature extraction pipeline used in humanoid robot speech recognition. List the mathematical transformations at each stage. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction:** MFCCs are the **dominant acoustic features** used in humanoid robot speech recognition because they approximate the human cochlea's frequency resolution and provide a compact, decorrelated representation of speech. **Pipeline Stages & Equations:** | Stage | Operation | Mathematical Form | |---|---|---| | 1. Pre-emphasis | Boost high frequencies | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha x[n-1]$, $\alpha \approx 0.97$ | | 2. Framing | Slice into 20–40 ms windows | $x_k[n]$, hop = $M$ | | 3. Windowing | Apply Hamming window | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / (N-1))$ | | 4. FFT | Frequency transform | $X_k[m] = \sum_n x_w[n] e^{-j 2\pi m n / N}$ | | 5. Power Spectrum | Energy per bin | $P_k[m] = \vert X_k[m] \vert^{2} / N$ | | 6. Mel Filterbank | Mimic cochlea | $S_k[\ell] = \sum_m P_k[m] \cdot H_\ell[m]$ | | 7. Log | Loudness perception | $\tilde{S}_k[\ell] = \ln(S_k[\ell] + \epsilon)$ | | 8. DCT | Decorrelate | $c_k[i] = \sum_\ell \tilde{S}_k[\ell] \cos(\pi i (\ell-0.5)/L)$ | **Mel scale conversion (critical):** $$ \begin{aligned} m(f) &= 1127 \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{f}{700} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Output:** A sequence of 12–13 cepstral coefficients per frame, often augmented with delta and delta-delta (velocity and acceleration) features for a final 39-dimensional vector. **Justification:** The Mel scale matches human auditory perception, where frequency resolution is logarithmic — high resolution at low frequencies (where formant structure of vowels lives) and lower resolution at high frequencies. #### **(b) Compare and contrast ITD-based and ILD-based sound localization in humanoid robotics. Which is more suitable for a robot operating in a quiet indoor environment with broadband speech sources? Justify with a numerical example. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | ITD | ILD | |---|---|---| | Physical basis | Path-length difference | Head-shadow attenuation | | Dominant range | < 1.5 kHz | > 1.5 kHz | | Cue type | Temporal | Energetic | | Robustness to noise | High at low freq | Moderate | | Hardware | Needs precise time sync | Needs matched gain | **Numerical Justification:** Consider a humanoid robot with mic separation $d = 0.18 \; m$, $c = 343 \; m/s$. For a source at $\theta = 30°$: **ITD:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta t = \frac{d \sin\theta}{c} = \frac{0.18 \cdot 0.5}{343} \approx 262 \; \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Wavelength of 1.5 kHz sound:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{1500} \approx 0.229 \; m \end{aligned} $$ Since $d = 0.18 \; m < \lambda = 0.229 \; m$, the head does **not** shadow the wave — ITD is meaningful. For a 4 kHz source: $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda = \frac{343}{4000} \approx 0.086 \; m \end{aligned} $$ Since $d > \lambda$, head shadowing is significant — ILD becomes the dominant cue. **Recommendation for quiet indoor broadband speech:** Use a **hybrid approach** — combine ITD (low-freq) and ILD (high-freq) per the Duplex Theory. For a quiet environment, **GCC-PHAT (ITD-based) is preferred** as the primary estimator because: - Speech has strong low-frequency energy (formants, fundamental). - ITD is mathematically cleaner and avoids calibration of microphone gains. - PHAT whitening handles reverberation well. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $p_0 = 20 \; \mu Pa$** with intensity reference $I_0 = 10^{-12} \; W/m^2$. These are **different** reference quantities for SPL vs SIL. > 2. **Forgetting to convert units** in $\Delta t$ (microseconds to seconds) before substituting into $\theta = \arcsin(c \Delta t / d)$. Marks lost consistently. > 3. **Mixing up microphone separation** $d$ with wavelength $\lambda$ in Duplex Theory examples. > 4. **Skipping the PHAT normalization step** in GCC-PHAT — many students write the basic cross-correlation but miss the critical whitening division. This is a 2-mark loss. > 5. **Writing "MFCC = FFT"** — MFCC requires the **entire pipeline** including Mel filterbank and DCT. Simply stating "apply FFT" is incomplete. > 6. **Not labeling axes** on spectrograms / signal diagrams — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabeled axes. > 7. **Conflating ASR with NLU** — Automatic Speech Recognition converts audio to text; Natural Language Understanding interprets meaning. Both are needed, but they are distinct stages. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Audition in Humanoid Robots** - **Definition:** Audition = robotic perception of sound; involves acquisition → processing → interpretation. - **Core physical constant:** Speed of sound in air at 20°C = **343 m/s**; scales linearly with temperature. - **Key relationship:** $v = f \lambda$ — links frequency, wavelength, and propagation speed. - **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20 \; \mu Pa$. - **Nyquist rule:** Sample rate must be **at least twice** the highest frequency of interest. - **Speech sampling:** Use **16 kHz**; full audio uses **44.1 kHz** or **48 kHz**. - **Duplex Theory:** ITD dominates < 1.5 kHz; ILD dominates > 1.5 kHz. - **ITD equation:** $\Delta t = (d \sin\theta) / c$. - **DoA equation:** $\theta = \arcsin(c \cdot \hat{\tau} / d)$. - **GCC-PHAT:** Cross-correlation with PHAT whitening — robust TDoA estimator. - **MFCC pipeline:** Pre-emphasis → Framing → Windowing → FFT → Mel Filterbank → Log → DCT. - **Mel scale:** $m = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$ — logarithmic at high f, linear at low f. - **Typical MFCC count:** 12–13 static + 13 delta + 13 delta-delta = **39 features** per frame. - **Standard frame:** 25 ms window, 10 ms hop, 50% overlap. - **Microphone arrays:** 2 mics minimum; 4–8 mics enable 3D localization. - **SSL algorithms (ranked):** GCC-PHAT < MUSIC/ESPRIT < Deep Learning. - **Biological analogy:** Pinna → Mic; Cochlea → Filterbank; Auditory cortex → ASR engine. - **Applications:** Voice commands, speaker ID, emotion detection, acoustic scene understanding. - **Real-world humanoids using audition:** NAO, Pepper, ASIMO, Sophia, iCub, Atlas (for emergency audio cues). - **Modern trend:** End-to-end deep learning (Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) replacing hand-crafted MFCC pipelines. - **Examination trick:** Always state units explicitly; always convert μs → s before substituting. - **Examiner's hot-button:** The Duplex Theory + the GCC-PHAT algorithm are the two most repeated 14-mark question patterns in KTU exams. --- **End of Module 3 — Audition Notes** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern (ESE)*

topics.title: Speech Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speech Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speech Recognition (Automatic Speech Recognition — ASR)** is the interdisciplinary sub-field of computational linguistics, computer science, and electrical engineering that develops methodologies and technologies enabling a machine (such as a humanoid robot) to **identify, transcribe, and interpret spoken human language into machine-readable text or actionable intent commands** in real time.

In the context of **humanoid robotics**, speech recognition is the *first perceptual gateway* of the auditory pipeline. The humanoid captures acoustic pressure waves through microphone arrays, transforms the analog signal into digital features, and decodes these features into symbolic word sequences using statistical and deep learning models. The decoded sequence is then routed to the **Natural Language Understanding (NLU)** module for intent classification, forming the foundation of Human-Robot Interaction (HRI).

**Standard KTU Taxonomy of ASR Systems:**

| Type | Vocabulary | Speaker Dependency | Use Case |
|------|------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Isolated Word** | < 10 words | Speaker-Dependent | Command words ("Stop", "Go") |
| **Connected Word** | Medium | Speaker-Dependent | Short phrases |
| **Continuous Speech** | Large (20k+) | Speaker-Independent | Natural conversation |
| **Spontaneous Speech** | Large | Speaker-Independent | Filled pauses, disfluencies |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Speech Recognition like a Court Stenographer working in a noisy courtroom.** The stenographer (ASR engine) listens to spoken words (acoustic signal), filters out the background noise of the audience (noise cancellation), transcribes the syllables into shorthand (feature extraction), and finally produces a clean transcript (text output) that the judge (NLU module) can act upon.

A humanoid robot operates under the same principles:
1. **The Microphone Array** → Acts as the stenographer's ears
2. **Pre-processing** → Filters the courtroom noise
3. **Acoustic Model** → Maps phonemes to acoustic patterns (like recognizing unique voices)
4. **Language Model** → Predicts likely next words (like contextual understanding)
5. **Decoder** → Outputs the final transcript

**Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards):**
- **Word Error Rate (WER)** = (S + D + I) / N, where S = Substitutions, D = Deletions, I = Insertions, N = Total words
- **Word Recognition Rate (WRR) = 1 − WER**
- **Real-Time Factor (RTF)** — must be **< 1.0** for real-time humanoid operation
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — robust humanoids target **> 10 dB** operating environments

---

### 1.3 The Speech Signal — A Physical Perspective

Human speech is a **non-stationary** acoustic signal produced by the vocal tract. Its properties:

- **Fundamental frequency (F0):** 85–180 Hz (adult male), 165–255 Hz (adult female)
- **Formant frequencies:** Resonances of the vocal tract (F1, F2, F3 typically 500 Hz, 1500 Hz, 2500 Hz for vowels)
- **Phoneme rate:** ~12–15 phonemes/second in continuous speech
- **Bandwidth of speech:** 4 kHz (telephony) to 8 kHz (wideband, required for humanoids)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For humanoid robots, **wideband (16 kHz sampling rate) speech recognition is mandatory** because they operate in close-proximity human environments where full phonetic detail (including fricatives like /s/, /sh/) must be preserved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Speech Recognition Pipeline — Structured Logic

The ASR pipeline for a humanoid robot executes in **six sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Audio Capture (Sampling & Quantization)**
- Analog speech $s(t)$ is sampled at frequency $f_s$ (≥ 16 kHz for humanoids).
- Quantization converts continuous amplitudes to discrete levels.
- Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max} \approx 8$ kHz for speech.
- Multi-microphone arrays (2 to 8 mics) use **Beamforming** to spatially filter the user's voice from ambient noise.

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing**
- **Pre-emphasis filter:** Boosts high frequencies to flatten the spectrum.
- **Noise Suppression:** Spectral subtraction or Wiener filtering.
- **Voice Activity Detection (VAD):** Distinguishes speech from silence.
- **Automatic Gain Control (AGC):** Normalizes loudness.

**Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (Acoustic Front-End)**
- The raw waveform is converted into a compact, perceptually-relevant representation.
- **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC)** are the gold standard.
- Pipeline: Pre-emphasis → Framing (25 ms, 10 ms hop) → Windowing (Hamming) → FFT → Mel Filterbank → Log → DCT → Delta & Delta-Delta features.

**Stage 4 — Acoustic Model (AM)**
- Maps acoustic features to **phonemes** (sub-word units).
- Classical: **Gaussian Mixture Model – Hidden Markov Model (GMM-HMM)**.
- Modern: **Deep Neural Network – HMM (DNN-HMM)** or end-to-end **CTC / Attention-based Encoder-Decoder (Transformer, RNN-T)**.

**Stage 5 — Language Model (LM)**
- Encodes the probability distribution over word sequences $P(W)$.
- **N-gram models** (bigram, trigram) or **Recurrent Neural Network LMs** or **Transformer LMs**.

**Stage 6 — Decoder**
- Combines AM and LM using **Bayes' Theorem** to find the most probable word sequence $\hat{W}$ given observed acoustics $X$.
- Uses algorithms like **Viterbi decoding** or **Beam Search**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is your KTU exam quick-reference. Memorize all equations and units.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem (ASR Core) | $\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$ | Find best word sequence given audio |
| 2 | Mel Scale Mapping | $mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$ | Perceptual frequency scale |
| 3 | Inverse Mel | $f = 700 \cdot \left(10^{mel/2595} - 1\right)$ | Convert mel back to Hz |
| 4 | Pre-emphasis Filter | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1], \quad \alpha \approx 0.97$ | Flatten spectral tilt |
| 5 | Hamming Window | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Reduce spectral leakage |
| 6 | Short-Time Energy | $E_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} [x(m) \cdot w(n-m)]^2$ | Distinguish voiced/unvoiced |
| 7 | Zero-Crossing Rate | $Z_n = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{m} \lvert \text{sgn}[x(m)] - \text{sgn}[x(m-1)] \rvert$ | Measure signal noisiness |
| 8 | Word Error Rate | $WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$ | ASR performance metric |
| 9 | HMM Joint Probability | $P(O, S \mid \lambda) = \pi_{s_1} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} a_{s_{t-1}, s_t} \cdot b_{s_t}(o_t)$ | Sequence likelihood |
| 10 | Real-Time Factor | $RTF = \frac{\text{Decoding Time}}{\text{Audio Duration}}$ | < 1.0 means real-time |
| 11 | Cepstral Mean Normalization | $\hat{c}_t = c_t - \frac{1}{T}\sum_{\tau=1}^{T} c_\tau$ | Channel normalization |
| 12 | MFCC Total Dimension | 13 static + 13 Δ + 13 ΔΔ = **39 features/frame** | Standard feature vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** The vertical pipe `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in all formulas above to prevent markdown table breaking. KTU examiners often use the table format to award partial marks — write these neatly.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In humanoid robotics, speech recognition is the *primary non-contact input modality*, enabling:
- **Hands-free operation** in industrial cobots (collaborative robots)
- **Socially assistive robots** for elderly care (e.g., Pepper, NAO)
- **Search and rescue humanoids** operating in noisy, high-stress environments
- **Educational robots** that teach language to children

The reliability of ASR directly impacts the **Kuhn-Tucker compliance** of the humanoid with ISO 13482 (Personal Care Robots Safety Standard), since misinterpreted commands can lead to unsafe actions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bayes' Decision Rule for ASR

**Starting Premise:** Given an observed acoustic feature sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$, we want to find the word sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N)$ that is **most probable**.

**Step 1: Formal Statement of the Recognition Problem**

The recognition problem is to find $\hat{W}$ such that:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W \in \mathcal{W}} P(W \mid X)
$$

where $\mathcal{W}$ is the set of all possible word sequences in the vocabulary.

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem**

We apply the conditional probability inversion:

$$
P(W \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the Search**

Since $P(X)$ does not depend on $W$, it acts as a normalizing constant and can be dropped from the maximization:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)
$$

**Step 4: Assign the Terms to Models**

- $P(X \mid W)$ is computed by the **Acoustic Model (AM)** — probability of observing the audio features given the word sequence.
- $P(W)$ is computed by the **Language Model (LM)** — prior probability of the word sequence in natural language.

**Step 5: Incorporate the Decoder Weight**

In practice, a tunable scaling factor $\alpha$ is added for the LM contribution (this is the *LM weight* hyperparameter):

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \log P(X \mid W) + \alpha \cdot \log P(W) + \beta \cdot |W|
$$

where $\beta \cdot |W|$ is a **word insertion penalty** to prevent the decoder from generating too many short words.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** The acoustic model asks *"What did I hear?"* The language model asks *"What was likely said given the context?"* The decoder combines both — this is the **fundamental principle** of statistical ASR.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mel Scale Conversion (Linear Hz → Perceptual Mel)

The human ear perceives frequencies **non-linearly** — it is more discriminative at low frequencies (below 1 kHz) and less discriminative at high frequencies.

**Step 1: Define the Mel Scale as an Integral**

The mel scale is defined such that equal mel distances correspond to equal perceptual pitch distances:

$$
mel(f) = C \int_0^f \frac{df'}{f' + f_0}
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\int_0^f \frac{df'}{f' + f_0} = \ln\!\left(\frac{f + f_0}{f_0}\right) = \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{f_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Choose Empirical Constants**

The experimentally derived constants are $C = 2595$ and $f_0 = 700$ Hz, producing the canonical form:

$$
mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Verification at Reference Points**

| Linear $f$ (Hz) | Mel Value | Interpretation |
|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 0 | 0 | Silence baseline |
| 700 | 999.96 ≈ 1000 | Reference (1 kHz mel ≈ 1000) |
| 3000 | 2595 · log(1 + 30/7) ≈ 2595 · 0.6946 ≈ 1802 | Above telephony bandwidth |
| 8000 | ≈ 2840 | Upper humanoid wideband limit |

**Step 5: Invert the Function**

To place triangular filterbank centers on the mel scale, we need the inverse:

$$
f = 700 \cdot \left(10^{mel/2595} - 1\right)
$$

This inverse is used to map the $M$ triangular filters of the **Mel Filterbank** uniformly in mel space.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forward Algorithm for HMM Acoustic Modeling (Trellis Computation)

**Step 1: Define the HMM Components**

A Hidden Markov Model is defined as $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ where:
- $A = [a_{ij}]$ — state transition probability matrix
- $B = b_j(o_t)$ — emission probability for observation $o_t$ in state $j$
- $\pi = \pi_i$ — initial state distribution

**Step 2: The Forward Variable**

Define $\alpha_t(j)$ as the probability of being in state $j$ after observing the first $t$ observations:

$$
\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t, q_t = j \mid \lambda)
$$

**Step 3: Initialization (t = 1)**

$$
\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1), \quad \text{for } 1 \leq j \leq N
$$

**Step 4: Recursive Induction (t = 2 to T)**

$$
\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

**Step 5: Termination**

$$
P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_T(j)
$$

**Step 6: Computational Complexity**

The naive algorithm has complexity $O(N^2 T)$. For typical ASR: $N \approx 3000$ HMM states (triphones), $T \approx 100$ frames/second → **9 × 10^8 operations/second of audio** — this is why humanoids use **Viterbi approximation** instead of full summation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pre-emphasis Filter Frequency Response

The pre-emphasis filter $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \cdot x[n-1]$ has transfer function:

$$
H(z) = 1 - 0.97 z^{-1}
$$

The magnitude response is:

$$
\mid H(e^{j\omega}) \mid = \sqrt{(1 - 0.97\cos\omega)^2 + (0.97\sin\omega)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 1.94\cos\omega + 0.97^2}
$$

**At $\omega = 0$ (DC):**

$$
\mid H(e^{j0}) \mid = \sqrt{1 - 1.94 + 0.9409} = \sqrt{0.0009} = 0.03
$$

**At $\omega = \pi$ (Nyquist, 8 kHz):**

$$
\mid H(e^{j\pi}) \mid = \sqrt{1 + 1.94 + 0.9409} = \sqrt{3.8809} \approx 1.97
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The filter provides ~**36 dB** of high-frequency boost (factor of ~65) — exactly the spectral tilt compensation needed because natural speech rolls off at approximately **−6 dB/octave** above 800 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Complete ASR Pipeline for a Humanoid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Speaker Voice] --> B[Microphone Array 2 to 8 mics]
    B --> C[Beamforming and Noise Reduction]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Conversion 16 kHz]
    D --> E[Voice Activity Detection VAD]
    E --> F[Pre-emphasis Filter alpha = 0.97]
    F --> G[Framing 25 ms and 10 ms hop]
    G --> H[Hamming Window Application]
    H --> I[FFT Fast Fourier Transform 512 point]
    I --> J[Mel Filterbank 26 filters]
    J --> K[Log Compression]
    K --> L[DCT Discrete Cosine Transform]
    L --> M[MFCC Feature Vector 39 dims]
    M --> N[Acoustic Model DNN HMM or Transformer]
    N --> O[Language Model N gram or RNN]
    O --> P[Decoder Viterbi or Beam Search]
    P --> Q[Word Sequence Output Text]
    Q --> R[NLU Intent and Entity Extraction]
    R --> S[Robot Action Planning]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — MFCC Feature Extraction Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Speech Waveform] --> P2[Pre emphasis Filter]
    P2 --> P3[Frame Blocking 25 ms]
    P3 --> P4[Hamming Window]
    P4 --> P5[FFT Magnitude Spectrum]
    P5 --> P6[Mel Filterbank Energy]
    P6 --> P7[Log of Mel Energies]
    P7 --> P8[DCT Operation]
    P8 --> P9[MFCC Static 13 coefs]
    P9 --> P10[Delta and Delta Delta]
    P10 --> P11[Final 39 dim Feature Vector]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Acoustic Model Layers (DNN-HMM Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AM1[Input MFCC Frame] --> AM2[Fully Connected Layer 1]
    AM2 --> AM3[Fully Connected Layer 2]
    AM3 --> AM4[Fully Connected Layer 3]
    AM4 --> AM5[Fully Connected Layer 4]
    AM5 --> AM6[Softmax Output over HMM States]
    AM6 --> AM7[HMM State Sequence]
    AM7 --> AM8[Phoneme Sequence]
    AM8 --> AM9[Word Sequence]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Diagram — Speaker-Dependent vs. Speaker-Independent ASR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SD1[Single User] --> SD2[Enrolled Voice Profile]
    SD2 --> SD3[High Accuracy WER less than 5 percent]
    SD3 --> SD4[Small Footprint Model]
    SD4 --> SD5[Low Compute Suitable for Humanoid Edge]

    SI1[Multiple Users] --> SI2[Population Statistics]
    SI2 --> SI3[Moderate Accuracy WER 10 to 20 percent]
    SI3 --> SI4[Large Acoustic Model]
    SI4 --> SI5[Cloud or GPU Required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *draw a block diagram*, always label each block with its **technical role** (e.g., "FFT", "Mel Filterbank") and **key parameters** (e.g., "25 ms frame, 10 ms hop"). Mermaid-rendered block diagrams follow KTU valuation norms and earn full 7-mark allocation for diagrams in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Word Error Rate" (WER) in the context of speech recognition. List the three types of errors used to compute it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Error Rate (WER)** is the standard performance metric for evaluating the accuracy of a Speech Recognition system, defined as the ratio of erroneous words to the total number of words in the reference transcript.

$$
WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N}
$$

where:
- $S$ = number of **Substitutions** (wrong word recognized)
- $D$ = number of **Deletions** (reference word missing from output)
- $I$ = number of **Insertions** (extra word not in reference)
- $N$ = total number of words in the reference

**Marks Distribution:** Formula 2 marks + Explanation of terms 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the purpose of the Mel scale in speech recognition? Write the formula to convert a frequency in Hz to the Mel scale.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mel scale** is a perceptual scale of pitch that mimics the non-linear frequency resolution of the human ear. The human auditory system is more sensitive to differences at low frequencies (below 1 kHz) than at high frequencies. The Mel scale warps the linear Hz axis to reflect this perception, which is essential for designing filterbanks in MFCC feature extraction.

$$
mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Purpose 2 marks + Formula 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture and operational stages of an Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system** for a humanoid robot. Use a labelled block diagram and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Mel scale transformation equation** and explain why the **Mel filterbank** uses triangular filters. Compute the mel value corresponding to $f = 4000$ Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ASR Architecture (7 marks)**

A humanoid robot's ASR system is a multi-stage pipeline that converts spoken audio into machine-readable text. The major stages are:

1. **Audio Capture & Beamforming (1 mark):** A microphone array of 2 to 8 microphones captures the user's voice. Beamforming algorithms (Delay-and-Sum, MVDR) spatially filter the audio to enhance the speaker's voice and suppress ambient noise — critical in human environments.

2. **Pre-processing (1 mark):** The signal passes through pre-emphasis ($y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 x[n-1]$) to flatten spectral tilt, followed by Voice Activity Detection (VAD) to segment speech from silence.

3. **Feature Extraction — MFCC (2 marks):** The signal is framed (25 ms windows with 10 ms hop), windowed with a Hamming function, transformed via FFT, passed through 26 triangular Mel-scale filters, log-compressed, and finally DCT'd to produce 13 MFCC coefficients per frame. Delta and delta-delta derivatives yield a 39-dimensional feature vector.

4. **Acoustic Model (1 mark):** Maps MFCC features to phonemes using either GMM-HMM (classical) or DNN-HMM / end-to-end Transformer models (modern). Outputs $P(X \mid W)$.

5. **Language Model (1 mark):** Computes $P(W)$ using n-gram statistics or neural LMs (RNN, Transformer).

6. **Decoder (1 mark):** Combines AM and LM via Bayes' theorem: $\hat{W} = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$, using Viterbi or Beam Search to produce the final transcript.

**(b) Mel Scale Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Perceptual Premise (1 mark)**
The human ear discriminates frequency differences non-uniformly — finer resolution below 1 kHz, coarser above. This requires a non-linear warping function.

**Step 2: Mathematical Definition (1 mark)**
The mel scale is defined so that equal mel intervals correspond to equal perceptual pitch intervals. This leads to the integral form:

$$
mel(f) = C \int_0^f \frac{df'}{f' + f_0}
$$

**Step 3: Integration (1 mark)**

$$
\int_0^f \frac{df'}{f' + f_700} = \ln\!\left(\frac{f + 700}{700}\right) = \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Empirical Constants (1 mark)**
The constants $C = 2595$ and $f_0 = 700$ Hz are experimentally derived (Stevens, Volkman, Newman 1937) to satisfy the perceptual reference of 1000 mels ≈ 1 kHz:

$$
mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Why Triangular Filters? (2 marks)**
Triangular filters are used because:
- They provide **smooth bandpass integration** of spectral energy
- They have **overlapping passbands** ensuring no frequency region is missed
- Their **triangular shape avoids sharp edges** that would introduce discontinuities
- They are **computationally efficient** (piecewise linear)

**Step 6: Numerical Computation (1 mark)**
For $f = 4000$ Hz:

$$
mel(4000) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{4000}{700}\right) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(6.7143\right)
$$

$$
= 2595 \cdot 0.8271 \approx 2146.3 \text{ mels}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** based acoustic modeling for speech recognition. State the three fundamental problems of HMM and briefly describe how the **Viterbi algorithm** solves the recognition problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MFCC feature extraction process** step-by-step. Why is MFCC preferred over Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) for speech recognition in humanoid robots? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HMM for Acoustic Modeling (7 marks)**

An HMM is a doubly stochastic process ideal for modeling speech, which exhibits both **spectral variation** (within a phoneme) and **temporal variation** (between phonemes).

**HMM Definition (1 mark):** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ where $A$ is the state transition matrix, $B$ is the emission probability distribution, $\pi$ is the initial state distribution.

**Three Fundamental Problems (3 marks):**
1. **Evaluation:** Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — solved by Forward-Backward algorithm.
2. **Decoding:** Find the optimal state sequence — solved by **Viterbi algorithm**.
3. **Training:** Optimize model parameters — solved by **Baum-Welch (EM) algorithm**.

**Viterbi Algorithm (3 marks):**
The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable state sequence $S^* = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_T)$ given observation $O$. It uses dynamic programming with the recursion:

$$
\delta_t(j) = \max_{i} \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(o_t)
$$

where $\delta_t(j)$ is the best score ending in state $j$ at time $t$, and $\psi_t(j)$ stores the backpointer for path reconstruction. Final answer: $S^* = \arg\max_j \delta_T(j)$ with backtrace.

**Why HMM for Speech?** Speech is a **piecewise stationary signal** — phonemes have stable spectral properties for ~50–100 ms (one HMM state) and then transition. HMMs naturally capture this.

**(b) MFCC Extraction (7 marks)**

The MFCC pipeline is identical to that shown in Section 4.2. The student must draw the diagram and explain each block:

1. **Pre-emphasis** (0.5 mark): $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 x[n-1]$
2. **Framing** (0.5 mark): 25 ms frames, 10 ms hop
3. **Windowing** (0.5 mark): Hamming window to reduce spectral leakage
4. **FFT** (0.5 mark): Convert to magnitude spectrum
5. **Mel Filterbank** (1 mark): 26 triangular filters spaced uniformly on mel scale
6. **Log** (0.5 mark): Compress dynamic range
7. **DCT** (0.5 mark): Decorrelate features, retain first 13 coefficients
8. **Delta Features** (0.5 mark): Add velocity and acceleration → 39 dims

**Why MFCC over LPC for Humanoids? (2 marks)**
- **Perceptual relevance:** MFCC uses the Mel scale matching human auditory perception — natural for HRI.
- **Robustness to noise:** MFCC's log-compression and DCT provide better noise resistance than LPC's all-pole modeling.
- **Speaker independence:** MFCC de-emphasizes vocal tract excitation (source), focusing on the filter (vocal tract shape) which is more speaker-invariant.
- **Compact representation:** 13 coefficients capture ~95% of speech information, ideal for embedded humanoid processors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Speech Recognition Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WER with WRR (Word Recognition Rate):** WER measures *errors*, WRR measures *success* (WRR = 1 − WER). Writing WER when the question asks for recognition accuracy loses 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Bayes' Denominator:** When writing $\hat{W} = \arg\max P(W \mid X)$, students often omit the derivation that $P(X)$ is dropped from the maximization. Examiners expect this step — leaving it out costs 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Wrong Constants in Mel Formula:** Writing 1125 instead of **2595**, or omitting the "+1" inside the log. The exact value **2595** is non-negotiable.
>
> 4. **HMM Symbol Misuse:** Mixing up $A$ (transitions) and $B$ (emissions). $A$ is a matrix of state transitions, $B$ is a probability distribution over observations.
>
> 5. **Frame size confusion:** Confusing **25 ms frame length** with **10 ms frame shift**. KTU specifically tests this — both numbers must appear.
>
> 6. **Drawing MFCC block diagram without labels:** A diagram with unlabeled blocks scores **zero** under KTU valuation. Every block must have a function name and key parameter.
>
> 7. **Skipping the "+1" in mel formula:** Many students write $mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}(f/700)$ — this gives $mel(0) = -\infty$ which is mathematically incorrect. Always include "+1".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Speech Recognition (Module 3):**

- ✅ **ASR Pipeline:** Mic Array → Beamforming → Pre-emphasis → VAD → MFCC → AM → LM → Decoder → Text
- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $\hat{W} = \arg\max P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$ — *P(X) is dropped*
- ✅ **Mel Formula:** $mel(f) = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$ — *constants 2595 and 700 are sacred*
- ✅ **MFCC Frame Settings:** 25 ms frame, 10 ms hop, 16 kHz sampling, 13 + 13 + 13 = 39 features
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis:** $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \cdot x[n-1]$ — *flattens −6 dB/octave spectral tilt*
- ✅ **HMM Components:** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ — *states are phonemes or triphones*
- ✅ **HMM Three Problems:** Evaluation (Forward-Backward), Decoding (Viterbi), Training (Baum-Welch)
- ✅ **WER Formula:** $WER = (S + D + I) / N$ — *expressed as percentage*
- ✅ **Performance Threshold:** RTF < 1.0 for real-time humanoid operation
- ✅ **VAD:** Distinguishes speech from silence — uses energy and ZCR thresholds
- ✅ **Beamforming:** Microphone array technique to spatially focus on speaker
- ✅ **AM vs. LM:** Acoustic Model = $P(X \mid W)$ (what was heard); Language Model = $P(W)$ (what was likely said)
- ✅ **Cepstral Mean Normalization (CMN):** Removes channel distortion, crucial for far-field humanoid microphones
- ✅ **End-to-end ASR:** Modern Transformer/RNN-T models eliminate the need for separate AM and LM in some architectures
- ✅ **Real-world humanoids using ASR:** Pepper (SoftBank), NAO (Aldebaran), ASIMO (Honda), Atlas (Boston Dynamics with custom voice)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — FACE PERCEPTION IN HUMANOID ROBOTICS
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Face Perception** is the cognitive and computational process by which a humanoid robot detects, localizes, identifies, verifies, and interprets human faces from visual sensory input. It is a specialized sub-domain of **biologically-inspired computer vision** that integrates *low-level image processing*, *mid-level feature extraction*, and *high-level cognitive modeling* to emulate the human visual cortex's innate ability to recognize, discriminate, and emotionally interpret faces.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Code **PERAT744**), Face Perception is positioned as a **perception-action primitive** that bridges raw visual sensing and socially-intelligent behavior in humanoid platforms such as **Pepper**, **NAO**, **ASIMO**, and **iCub**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Grandmother Cell" vs. "Crowd" Approach

Imagine you walk into a packed railway station and instantly spot your grandmother among **2,000 strangers**. You did not:
- Measure the exact distance between her eyes,
- Compute the curvature of her lips,
- Or check the pixel intensity of her hairline.

Your brain performed a **holistic, gestalt-level recognition** in roughly **200 milliseconds**. This is what humanoid robots must replicate — only they must do it numerically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Paradigms of Face Perception:**
> - **Part-Based (Feature-Driven):** Detect eyes, nose, mouth individually → assemble identity. *(Analogy: identifying a car by counting wheels, checking headlights, and reading the badge.)*
> - **Holistic (Configural):** Treat the face as a single, inseparable template. *(Analogy: recognizing your grandmother as a whole "gestalt" — you cannot easily describe what makes her look like her, yet you know her instantly.)*

Modern humanoid systems use a **hybrid paradigm**, mirroring the dual-stream hypothesis of the human visual cortex (ventral "what" pathway + dorsal "where" pathway).

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

| Standard Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection latency (real-time)** | **≤ 33 ms per frame (30 FPS)** | Required for fluid human-robot interaction |
| **Recognition accuracy (LFW benchmark)** | **≥ 99.80%** | Industry gold standard |
| **Inter-pupillary distance (adult mean)** | **≈ 63 mm** | Used for scale normalization |
| **Image resolution (input)** | **640 × 480 pixels (VGA)** | Minimum for robust feature extraction |
| **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** | **≤ 0.001%** | Security-critical deployments |
| **Fusiform Face Area (FFA) activation** | **150–200 ms post-stimulus** | Biological benchmark |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to define face perception, always mention the **perception-action loop** — a humanoid robot that *sees* a face must also *respond* to it (gaze shift, greeting, emotion mirroring). Pure passive recognition is **incomplete** by KTU 2024 standards.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of Face Perception

A humanoid robot's face perception pipeline is decomposed into **five canonical stages** that mirror the ventral stream of the human visual cortex (V1 → V2 → V4 → IT cortex):

### Stage 1 — **Acquisition & Preprocessing**
- Capture frame from RGB-D camera (e.g., Intel RealSense, ASUS Xtion).
- Convert to grayscale, apply **histogram equalization** for illumination invariance.
- Apply **geometric normalization**: rotate to align eye centers along horizontal axis; scale so inter-ocular distance equals a canonical constant (e.g., **60 pixels**).

### Stage 2 — **Face Detection** (Localize "Is there a face?")
- Sliding-window classifiers (Viola-Jones using **Haar-like features**).
- Modern: Single Shot Multibox Detector (**SSD**), **MTCNN**, **RetinaFace**.
- Output: bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$ and 5 facial landmarks.

### Stage 3 — **Feature Extraction** (Encode "What does it look like?")
- **Handcrafted:** Local Binary Patterns (LBP), Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG), Gabor wavelets.
- **Learned:** Convolutional Neural Network embeddings (FaceNet, ArcFace, DeepFace).
- Output: a $d$-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{f} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is typically **128, 512, or 1280**.

### Stage 4 — **Recognition / Verification** (Answer "Who is it?")
- **Identification (1:N):** Compare $\mathbf{f}$ against a gallery of $N$ enrolled templates using a distance metric.
- **Verification (1:1):** Confirm claimed identity by thresholding a similarity score.

### Stage 5 — **Expression & Gaze Interpretation** (Answer "How does it feel?")
- Classify into basic emotions (Ekman's **6 universal emotions**: happiness, sadness, anger, fear, disgust, surprise).
- Estimate gaze direction via pupil-center localization → vector to iris boundary.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why detect first?** Because feature extraction is computationally expensive — restricting it to face regions reduces search space by **~95%**.
> - Why learn features instead of handcrafting them? Because CNNs achieve **>99%** accuracy on LFW, while LBP tops out near **~95%**.
> - Why normalize geometry? Because a face rotated 30° is the *same identity* but a *different pixel pattern*. Alignment restores invariance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Memorize the variables, units, and conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Image as vector | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ | $N = H \times W$ pixel count |
| 2 | Mean face (gallery) | $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbf{x}_{i}$ | $M$ = number of training faces |
| 3 | Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} (\mathbf{x}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{x}})^{T}$ | $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ |
| 4 | Eigenvalue equation | $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | $\lambda$ = eigenvalue, $\mathbf{v}$ = eigenvector (eigenface) |
| 5 | Face reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} + \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_{1}, \dots, \mathbf{v}_{k}]$ |
| 6 | Euclidean distance | $d_{E}(\mathbf{f}_{1}, \mathbf{f}_{2}) = \Vert \mathbf{f}_{1} - \mathbf{f}_{2} \Vert_{2}$ | Smaller → more similar |
| 7 | Cosine similarity | $s_{C} = \dfrac{\mathbf{f}_{1}^{T} \mathbf{f}_{2}}{\Vert \mathbf{f}_{1} \Vert_{2} \cdot \Vert \mathbf{f}_{2} \Vert_{2}}$ | Range $\vert s_{C} \vert \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Mahalanobis distance | $d_{M} = \sqrt{(\mathbf{f}_{1}-\mathbf{f}_{2})^{T} \mathbf{S}^{-1} (\mathbf{f}_{1}-\mathbf{f}_{2})}$ | $\mathbf{S}$ = covariance matrix |
| 9 | Gabor filter kernel | $G(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x'^{2}+\gamma^{2} y'^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \cos\!\left(2\pi \dfrac{x'}{\lambda_{g}} + \psi\right)$ | $\lambda_{g}$ = wavelength, $\gamma$ = aspect ratio, $\psi$ = phase |
| 10 | 2D Gabor response | $R(x,y) = \vert I(x,y) * G(x,y) \vert$ | $*$ = 2D convolution |
| 11 | LBP operator | $LBP_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(g_{p} - g_{c}) 2^{p}$ | $s(z) = 1$ if $z \geq 0$, else $0$ |
| 12 | Softmax probability | $P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{\exp(z_{k})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(z_{j})}$ | $K$ = number of classes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare robotics:** Detect pain or discomfort in non-verbal patients (e.g., PARO seal robot).
- **Social humanoid companions (Pepper):** Adjust conversational tone based on detected frustration.
- **Surveillance & security:** Re-identify individuals across non-overlapping camera networks.
- **Autism therapy robots:** Use exaggerated facial cues to teach emotion recognition (e.g., Kaspar).
- **Industrial HRI:** Detect operator fatigue via micro-expression analysis → trigger safety lockout.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Eigenface Projection (PCA-based Face Recognition)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Eigenface** method, introduced by Turk & Pentland (1991), is the foundational mathematical model for holistic face perception. KTU examiners frequently test this derivation. Every step below is **mandatory** for full marks.

### Given
- A training gallery of $M$ face images, each of size $H \times W$ pixels.
- Each image is reshaped (raster-scanned) into a column vector $\mathbf{x}_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ where $N = H \cdot W$.
- Assume $N \gg M$ (a typical case: $N = 10{,}000$ for a $100 \times 100$ image, $M = 100$ subjects).

### Step 1 — Form the Data Matrix
Stack the $M$ column vectors side-by-side to form the data matrix:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_{1} & \mathbf{x}_{2} & \cdots & \mathbf{x}_{M} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}
$$

Each column is one face. The matrix $\mathbf{X}$ has $N$ rows (one per pixel) and $M$ columns (one per face).

### Step 2 — Compute the Mean Face
The mean face is the pixel-wise average across the gallery:

$$
\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbf{x}_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}
$$

> **Logic:** Subtracting the mean face centers the data at the origin of pixel space, ensuring the principal components capture *variation* (not absolute brightness).

### Step 3 — Mean-Center the Data
Subtract the mean face from every training image:

$$
\mathbf{\Phi}_{i} = \mathbf{x}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, M\}
$$

Form the centered data matrix:

$$
\mathbf{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{\Phi}_{1} & \mathbf{\Phi}_{2} & \cdots & \mathbf{\Phi}_{M} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Scatter / Covariance Matrix
The covariance matrix captures how pixel intensities co-vary across the gallery:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbf{\Phi}_{i} \mathbf{\Phi}_{i}^{T} = \frac{1}{M} \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Phi}^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}
$$

> **Pitfall:** $\mathbf{C}$ is $N \times N$, where $N$ can be **100,000+** for high-resolution faces. Direct eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{C}$ is computationally infeasible.

### Step 5 — Dimensionality Reduction Trick (The Key Insight)
Since $\mathbf{C}$ is rank-deficient (rank $\leq M - 1$), compute the much smaller $M \times M$ matrix:

$$
\mathbf{L} = \frac{1}{M} \mathbf{\Phi}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times M}
$$

Each entry $L_{ij} = \mathbf{\Phi}_{i}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{j}$ is the dot product of two mean-centered faces (a scalar similarity).

### Step 6 — Eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{L}$
Solve the small eigenvalue problem:

$$
\mathbf{L} \mathbf{u}_{k} = \lambda_{k} \mathbf{u}_{k}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, M
$$

Sort eigenvalues in descending order: $\lambda_{1} \geq \lambda_{2} \geq \dots \geq \lambda_{M}$.

### Step 7 — Recover the Eigenfaces
Map each eigenvector $\mathbf{u}_{k}$ of $\mathbf{L}$ back to the original pixel space:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{k} = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{u}_{k} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, M
$$

Normalize: $\mathbf{v}_{k} \leftarrow \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_{k}}{\Vert \mathbf{v}_{k} \Vert_{2}}$.

> **Result:** Each $\mathbf{v}_{k}$ is an "eigenface" — a ghostly face-like basis image. The top eigenfaces encode the largest variance directions (lighting, pose), while later ones encode identity-discriminative details.

### Step 8 — Choose $k$ Eigenfaces (Energy Retention)
Retain the top $k$ eigenfaces such that:

$$
\frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_{j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} \lambda_{j}} \geq 0.95
$$

> **Convention (KTU):** $k = \min(M-1, 50)$ is typical; $k = 0.95 \cdot M$ ensures **95% energy retention**.

Form the projection matrix:

$$
\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_{1} & \mathbf{v}_{2} & \cdots & \mathbf{v}_{k} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}
$$

### Step 9 — Project a New Face
Given a probe face $\mathbf{x}_{probe}$, project it into the eigenface subspace:

$$
\mathbf{y}_{probe} = \mathbf{W}^{T} (\mathbf{x}_{probe} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{k}
$$

### Step 10 — Reconstruct (Verification Step)

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{probe} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}_{probe} + \bar{\mathbf{x}}
$$

The reconstruction error is:

$$
\epsilon = \Vert \mathbf{x}_{probe} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{probe} \Vert_{2}^{2}
$$

> **Decision rule (Face or Not-Face):** If $\epsilon > \theta$ (threshold), reject. Otherwise classify by nearest neighbor in the projected gallery.

### Step 11 — Classification by Nearest Neighbor
For each training face projection $\mathbf{y}_{i} = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{i}$:

$$
i^{*} = \arg\min_{i} \Vert \mathbf{y}_{probe} - \mathbf{y}_{i} \Vert_{2}
$$

The probe is classified as the identity of training image $i^{*}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Derivation.** This is the **complete** eigenface algorithm. KTU examiners will deduct marks for: (a) omitting the $\mathbf{L}$-trick, (b) forgetting normalization, (c) not stating the 95% energy rule.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (For Intuition)

Let $H=W=2$, so $N=4$. Gallery has $M=2$ faces:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 200 \\ 100 \\ 150 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 220 \\ 110 \\ 160 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Mean face:**

$$
\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 200+220 \\ 100+110 \\ 150+160 \\ 50+60 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 210 \\ 105 \\ 155 \\ 55 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Centered vectors:**

$$
\mathbf{\Phi}_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ -5 \\ -5 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Phi}_{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 5 \\ 5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Small matrix $\mathbf{L}$:**

$$
\mathbf{L} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{\Phi}_{1}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{1} & \mathbf{\Phi}_{1}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{2} \\ \mathbf{\Phi}_{2}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{1} & \mathbf{\Phi}_{2}^{T} \mathbf{\Phi}_{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & -50 \\ -50 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_{1} = 100, \lambda_{2} = 0$ (rank 1, as expected). The single non-trivial eigenface:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} \begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ -5 \\ -5 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Choose $k=1$ (captures 100% of variance).
