topics.title: Thinking: Models of Emotion and Motivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMIDAN ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thinking: Models of Emotion and Motivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Emotion and Motivation in Humanoid Robotics

In the context of **PERAT744 – Fundamentals of Humanoid Robots**, *emotion* and *motivation* are not vague philosophical concepts but formal **computational constructs** that govern the internal state-space, decision-making, and behavioral output of a humanoid agent. The module bridges classical cognitive psychology with embodied artificial intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Emotion):**
> **Emotion** in a humanoid robot is a *time-bounded, multidimensional affective state* characterized by coordinated changes across **valence** (pleasure–displeasure), **arousal** (activation–deactivation), and sometimes **dominance** (control–submission), used to bias perception, attention, learning, and motor response selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Motivation):**
> **Motivation** is the *internal homeostatic and goal-directed process* that assigns **priority weights** to candidate behaviors, regulating which drives (survival, social, curiosity, competence) dominate the robot's action selection at any given moment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driver" and the "Dashboard"

Imagine a humanoid robot as a **self-driving car**:
- **Motivation** is the *engine controller* — it decides *why* the car should move, refuel, or stop (fuel level, destination priority, traffic rules).
- **Emotion** is the *dashboard cluster* — a continuously updating display (fuel gauge, speed, temperature) that *reports and modulates* the internal state and influences driver/algorithm behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A robot can exhibit *emotion-like behavior* (facial expression, vocal prosody) **without** having *motivation* — but **motivation is useless without a mechanism to express it through affective channels** (posture, gaze, tone). In KTU parlance: **Emotion is the output channel; Motivation is the control law.**

### 1.3 Why These Models Matter in Humanoid Robotics

| Engineering Need | Role of Emotion Model | Role of Motivation Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural HRI** (Human-Robot Interaction) | Provides believable facial/gestural feedback | Drives socially appropriate turn-taking |
| **Adaptive Learning** | Modulates reinforcement learning rate | Prioritizes which skills to learn |
| **Survival/Autonomy** | Detects threat via high-arousal valence | Triggers recharge, self-preservation |
| **Trust Calibration** | Signals uncertainty to humans | Aligns robot goals with human goals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – PERAT744):** The 2024 KTU scheme explicitly tests the ability to *map psychological theories onto robotic architectures* (e.g., mapping *Maslow's hierarchy* onto a layered behavioral controller).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Psychological Models of Emotion (Mapped to Robotics)

#### A. **James–Lange Theory (1884–1885)**
- **Claim:** Stimulus → Physiological Response → Emotion (you feel afraid *because* you tremble).
- **Robotic Mapping:** Sensor input → low-level reflex actuator firing → affective labeler interprets actuator state as "fear".
- **Engineering Utility:** Drives **bottom-up affect detection** (e.g., reading joint stiffness to infer discomfort).

#### B. **Cannon–Bard Theory (1927–1931)**
- **Claim:** Stimulus → Simultaneous physiological response **and** emotional experience (parallel pathways via thalamus).
- **Robotic Mapping:** A *parallel architecture* where the central pattern generator (CPG) and the affective subsystem receive the same sensory broadcast in parallel.
- **Engineering Utility:** Enables **low-latency dual response** — body reacts *and* face expresses simultaneously.

#### C. **Schachter–Singer Two-Factor Theory (1962)**
- **Claim:** Emotion = *Physiological Arousal* + *Cognitive Label/Attribution*.
- **Formula:** $E = A \times C$ where $E$ = emotion intensity, $A$ = arousal level, $C$ = cognitive label match.
- **Robotic Mapping:** Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) agent tags raw arousal with contextual meaning ("high heart rate + darkness = fear").
- **Engineering Utility:** Powers **context-sensitive emotional response** in social robots like *Pepper* and *Sophia*.

#### D. **Lazarus Appraisal Theory (1966, refined 1991)**
- **Claim:** Stimulus → Cognitive Appraisal (primary & secondary) → Emotion.
- **Two Appraisal Stages:**
  1. **Primary Appraisal:** *Is this relevant to my goals/well-being?* → outputs relevance, congruence, goal significance.
  2. **Secondary Appraisal:** *Can I cope?* → outputs coping potential, future expectancy, agency.
- **Robotic Mapping:** Implemented in architectures like **EMA (Emotion Machine Architecture)** and **GRATE*.
- **Engineering Utility:** Foundation for **deliberative affect** — robots that reason about *why* they should feel something.

#### E. **Ekman's Basic Emotion Theory (1971, 1992)**
- **Claim:** Six (later expanded) **universal basic emotions**: *Happiness, Sadness, Anger, Fear, Disgust, Surprise* (and *Contempt*).
- **Robotic Mapping:** Discrete categorical affective states in the robot's state machine, each tied to predefined expressive templates.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in **facial expression synthesis** (e.g., *Kismet* at MIT, *ASIMO*).

#### F. **Russell's Circumplex Model of Affect (1980)**
- **Claim:** All affective states map onto a **2-D circular space** defined by:
  - **Valence** (x-axis): unpleasant → pleasant
  - **Arousal** (y-axis): calm → excited
- **Formula for PAD (Pleasure-Arousal-Dominance):**
  $$\vec{A} = (v, a, d) \in [-1, 1]^3$$
- **Robotic Mapping:** Continuous-valued emotional state vector; a fuzzy-logic controller selects actions based on $(\vec{A})$.
- **Engineering Utility:** Smooth, **continuous emotional transitions** (no discrete jumps) — essential for lifelike affect.

### 2.2 Major Models of Motivation

#### A. **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (1943)**
- **Claim:** Five (sometimes 7–8) hierarchical needs, lower needs must be (partially) satisfied before higher needs motivate behavior.
  1. **Physiological** (battery, motor temperature)
  2. **Safety** (balance, obstacle avoidance)
  3. **Belonging/Love** (social proximity, gaze)
  4. **Esteem** (task success, recognition)
  5. **Self-Actualization** (learning novel skills)
- **Robotic Mapping:** A **layered behavior stack** with suppression/preemption operators (e.g., low battery overrides curiosity).
- **Engineering Utility:** Cleanest mapping to a **priority-based scheduler**.

#### B. **Drive Reduction Theory (Hull, 1943)**
- **Claim:** Motivation arises from a biological *need* creating a *drive* (e.g., hunger) that pushes behavior until the drive is reduced.
- **Formula:** $\Delta D = k \cdot (H_{target} - H_{current})$ where $D$ = drive intensity, $H$ = homeostatic variable, $k$ = gain.
- **Robotic Mapping:** **Homeostatic regulation loops** — battery state, thermal state, social "contact" counter.
- **Engineering Utility:** Foundation of the **subsumption architecture** (Brooks, 1986) used in many humanoid platforms.

#### C. **Arousal Theory / Yerkes–Dodson Law (1908)**
- **Claim:** Performance is an *inverted-U* function of arousal; moderate arousal → optimal performance.
- **Formula:**
  $$P(a) = P_{max} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{a - a^*}{\sigma}\right)^2}$$
  where $a$ = current arousal, $a^*$ = optimal arousal, $\sigma$ = task-specific spread.
- **Robotic Mapping:** Robots modulate **search vs. exploit** behavior based on curiosity-driven arousal.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in **reinforcement learning exploration bonuses**.

#### D. **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985)**
- **Claim:** Three innate psychological needs: **Autonomy, Competence, Relatedness**.
- **Robotic Mapping:** Intrinsic reward functions: $r_{int} = w_A \cdot r_{autonomy} + w_C \cdot r_{competence} + w_R \cdot r_{relatedness}$.
- **Engineering Utility:** Powers **long-term engagement** in companion/social robots.

#### E. **Incentive / Expectancy-Value Theory (Vroom, 1964)**
- **Claim:** Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence.
- **Formula:**
  $$M = E \times I \times V$$
- **Robotic Mapping:** BDI agents compute expected utility for each candidate plan.
- **Engineering Utility:** Standard in **deliberative task planning** for humanoids (e.g., PR2, Atlas task selection).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Model | Formula / Construct | Variables & Units | Application in Humanoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schachter–Singer | $E = A \times C$ | $E$ = emotion, $A$ = arousal (norm $[0,1]$), $C$ = cognitive match | Affective labeling |
| 2 | Russell PAD | $\vec{A} = (v, a, d)$, $\vert v, a, d \vert \le 1$ | $v$ valence, $a$ arousal, $d$ dominance | Continuous affect space |
| 3 | Drive Reduction | $\Delta D = k \cdot (H_{target} - H_{current})$ | $k$ = gain, $H$ = homeostat | Battery, thermal regulation |
| 4 | Yerkes–Dodson | $P(a) = P_{max} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{a - a^*}{\sigma}\right)^2}$ | $a$ = arousal, $a^*$ = optimal, $\sigma$ = spread | RL exploration tuning |
| 5 | Expectancy-Value | $M = E \times I \times V$ | $E, I, V \in [0,1]$ | BDI plan selection |
| 6 | SDT Intrinsic Reward | $r_{int} = w_A r_A + w_C r_C + w_R r_R$ | weights $w$ sum to 1 | Companion robot engagement |
| 7 | Maslow Priority | $\pi_i = \frac{1}{rank_i + \alpha \cdot deficit_i}$ | $\alpha$ = deficit sensitivity | Layered behavior stack |
| 8 | Emotional Decay | $A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau$ = time constant (s) | Affective memory decay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** You will *not* be asked to memorize numerical constants, but you **must** be able to write the **functional form** of each formula, name the variables, and explain robotic relevance.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SoftBank Pepper (Aldebaran):** Implements a simplified Schachter–Singer model — high "stimulus novelty" + "low prior expectation" → "surprise" affective state, which modulates speech prosody.
- **MIT Kismet (Breazeal, late 1990s):** Canonical implementation of Russell's circumplex + Maslow layered drives (social, stimulation, fatigue).
- **Hanson Sophia:** Combines Ekman's basic emotions (discrete facial templates) with appraisal-based inner monologue.
- **Honda ASIMO:** Uses drive-reduction for battery/thermal management and expectancy-value for task prioritization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mapping a Drive-Reduction Model to a Homeostatic Controller

We derive the control law for a humanoid robot's **"social-contact drive"** (a motivation variable). The robot must maintain an internal social-contact level $H$ near a target $H_{target}$.

**Step 1 — Define the homeostatic deficit:**
$$\delta(t) = H_{target} - H(t)$$

**Step 2 — The drive intensity is proportional to the deficit (Hookean-like spring analogy):**
$$D(t) = k \cdot \delta(t) = k \cdot \big(H_{target} - H(t)\big)$$

**Step 3 — The behavioral actuation (action tendency) is a non-linear saturation of drive (cannot exceed actuator limits):**
$$A(t) = A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{D(t)}{A_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — The homeostatic level changes based on actuation and natural decay:**
$$H(t+1) = H(t) + \eta \cdot A(t) - \lambda \cdot H(t)$$

where:
- $\eta$ = *learning/consumption rate* of the social-contact resource
- $\lambda$ = *natural decay rate* of social need (per time-step)

**Step 5 — Substitute (3) into (4) to obtain closed-loop dynamics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(t+1) &= H(t) + \eta \cdot A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k(H_{target}-H(t))}{A_{ref}}\right) - \lambda H(t) \\
       &= (1-\lambda) H(t) + \eta A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k(H_{target}-H(t))}{A_{ref}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Equilibrium analysis** (set $H(t+1) = H(t) = H^*$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^* &= (1-\lambda) H^* + \eta A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k(H_{target}-H^*)}{A_{ref}}\right) \\
\lambda H^* &= \eta A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k(H_{target}-H^*)}{A_{ref}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The robot will *settle* at $H^*$ such that its actuation exactly balances the natural decay. If $\lambda \to 0$ (no decay), $H^* \to H_{target}$. If $\eta$ is small, $H^*$ falls below target (under-motivated). This is the *mathematical essence* of the Drive Reduction model as used in subsumption-based humanoids.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Continuous Affect Dynamics (Russell PAD)

We model how the robot's valence $v(t)$ evolves under sensory input $s(t)$ and decays passively.

**Step 1 — Define the affective state vector at time $t$:**
$$\vec{A}(t) = \big(v(t), a(t), d(t)\big)^T$$

**Step 2 — Define the sensory input vector projected through an appraisal matrix $M$:**
$$\vec{I}(t) = M \cdot \vec{s}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — State update with time constant $\tau$:**
$$\tau \frac{d\vec{A}}{dt} = -\vec{A}(t) + \vec{I}(t) + \vec{b}$$

where $\vec{b}$ is a *baseline bias* (e.g., default pleasant, mildly alert).

**Step 4 — Discretize with Euler method, $\Delta t = 1$:**
$$\vec{A}(t+1) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{\tau}\right) \vec{A}(t) + \frac{1}{\tau}\big(\vec{I}(t) + \vec{b}\big)$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state affect** (set $\vec{A}(t+1) = \vec{A}(t) = \vec{A}^*$):
$$\vec{A}^* = \vec{I} + \vec{b}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the homogeneous decay for an isolated input impulse** $\vec{I} = \vec{I}_0 \cdot \delta(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau \frac{d\vec{A}}{dt} &= -\vec{A}(t) \quad \text{(no input after t=0)} \\
\Rightarrow \vec{A}(t) &= \vec{A}(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This recovers the **emotional decay law** listed in the cheat sheet (Row 8): $A(t) = A_0 e^{-t/\tau}$. The time constant $\tau$ is a tunable design parameter: small $\tau$ → *volatile* robot (emotion flickers), large $\tau$ → *stoic* robot.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Yerkes–Dodson for RL Exploration Bonus

**Step 1 — Define the exploration policy probability of a random action:**
$$P_{explore}(a) = P_{max} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{a - a^*}{\sigma}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. arousal to find the maximum:**
$$\frac{dP}{da} = P_{max} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{a - a^*}{\sigma}\right)^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{2(a - a^*)}{\sigma^2}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**
$$a = a^* \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{max} \text{ achieved at } a^*$$

**Step 4 — Compute the optimal arousal as a function of task difficulty $d$** (empirically, $a^*$ is *higher* for easy tasks, *lower* for hard tasks):
$$a^*(d) = a_{min} + (a_{max} - a_{min}) \cdot e^{-\beta d}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the exploration rate as a function of task difficulty:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{explore}(a, d) &= P_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{a - a_{min} - (a_{max}-a_{min}) e^{-\beta d}}{\sigma}\right)^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** For *difficult* ($d \to \infty$) tasks, $a^* \to a_{min}$ → robot prefers *low* arousal → cautious, methodical exploration. For *easy* tasks, $a^* \to a_{max}$ → robot prefers *high* arousal → aggressive, opportunistic exploration.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Models of Emotion)

| Model | Direction | Appraisal Stage | Discrete/Continuous | Best Robotic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| James–Lange | Bottom-up | None (post-hoc) | Discrete | Self-diagnostics |
| Cannon–Bard | Parallel | None | Discrete | Low-latency response |
| Schachter–Singer | Two-stage | Attribution | Discrete | Contextual affect |
| Lazarus | Top-down | Primary + Secondary | Continuous | Deliberative agents |
| Ekman | Categorical | Recognition | Discrete | Facial synthesis |
| Russell | Dimensional | Continuous | Continuous | Smooth affect transitions |

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Models of Motivation)

| Model | Type | Continuous/Discrete | Robotic Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maslow | Hierarchical | Discrete layers | Priority scheduler |
| Drive Reduction | Homeostatic | Continuous | PID/feedback controller |
| Arousal (Yerkes–Dodson) | Performance-based | Continuous | RL exploration policy |
| Self-Determination | Intrinsic | Continuous | Social reward function |
| Expectancy-Value | Cognitive | Continuous | BDI planner |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cognitive Architecture Mapping (Lazarus + Maslow + Drive Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[External Stimulus s] --> nodeSense[Sensory Layer: Vision/Audio/Touch]
    nodeSense --> nodeApp1[Primary Appraisal: Relevance Goal Congruence]
    nodeApp1 --> nodeApp2[Secondary Appraisal: Coping Potential Agency]
    nodeApp2 --> nodeEmo[Emotional Label E]
    nodeEmo --> nodePad[PAD Vector in Russell Space]
    nodePad --> nodeExpr[Expression: Face Voice Posture]
    nodeApp1 --> nodeMot1[Drive Module: Maslow Layer 1-2 Physiological Safety]
    nodeApp2 --> nodeMot2[Drive Module: Maslow Layer 3-4 Belonging Esteem]
    nodeMot1 --> nodeBS[Behavior Stack Subsumption]
    nodeMot2 --> nodeBS
    nodeEmo --> nodeBS
    nodeBS --> nodeAct[Actuator Commands]
    nodeAct --> nodeEnv[Environment Feedback]
    nodeEnv --> nodeSense
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Homeostatic Motivation Loop (Drive-Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH[Homeostatic State H] --> nodeDelta[Deficit delta equals H target minus H]
    nodeDelta --> nodeDrive[Drive D equals k times delta]
    nodeDrive --> nodeSat[Action Saturation A equals A max times tanh D over A ref]
    nodeSat --> nodeBeh[Behavior Selection]
    nodeBeh --> nodeAct[Actuation]
    nodeAct --> nodeUpd[Update H t+1]
    nodeUpd --> nodeH
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Russell Circumplex (2-D Affect Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axisX[X Axis Valence]
        axisXneg[Negative Unpleasant]
        axisXpos[Positive Pleasant]
    end
    subgraph axisY[Y Axis Arousal]
        axisYneg[Low Calm]
        axisYpos[High Excited]
    end
    quadrant1[High Arousal Positive: Excited Happy] --> axisXpos
    quadrant2[High Arousal Negative: Stressed Angry] --> axisXneg
    quadrant3[Low Arousal Negative: Sad Bored] --> axisXneg
    quadrant4[Low Arousal Positive: Calm Content] --> axisXpos
    axisXneg --> quadrant2
    axisXneg --> quadrant3
    axisYneg --> quadrant3
    axisYneg --> quadrant4
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Maslow Hierarchy as Robotic Control Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeL1[Layer 1 Physiological: Battery Thermal] --> nodeL2[Layer 2 Safety: Balance Collision]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3[Layer 3 Social: Gaze Touch Proximity]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Layer 4 Esteem: Task Mastery Recognition]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5[Layer 5 Self Actualization: Learning Creativity]
    nodeL1 --> nodeGate{Preemption Gate: Lower deficit nonzero}
    nodeGate --> nodeOut[Output Motor Command]
    nodeL5 --> nodeOut
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Schachter–Singer Implementation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Stimulus] --> nodePhy[Physiological Arousal A]
    nodePhy --> nodeCtx[Cognitive Contextual Search]
    nodeS1 --> nodeCtx
    nodeCtx --> nodeLab[Label Matching]
    nodeLab --> nodeEmo[Emotion Output E equals A times C]
    nodeEmo --> nodeBeh[Behavior Modulation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid v10+** syntax and follow the *Alpha-Rule* for node identifiers (e.g., `nodeStart`, `nodeApp1`) and avoid markdown formatting inside node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *Differentiate between James–Lange and Cannon–Bard theories of emotion. Which is more suitable for implementing low-latency affective response in a humanoid robot, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **James–Lange (1 mark):** Stimulus → Physiological Response → Emotion (sequential). Robot would *infer* emotion from actuator state.
- **Cannon–Bard (1 mark):** Stimulus → Physiological Response **and** Emotion *in parallel* (via thalamus). Both branches fire simultaneously.
- **Suitability (1 mark):** **Cannon–Bard** is more suitable for *low-latency* humanoid response because the affective signal does not have to wait for the physiological response to complete — both are broadcast from the sensory input in parallel, enabling simultaneous body reaction and facial expression.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *With a neat diagram, explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs. How can it be implemented as a behavioral priority stack in a humanoid robot?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hierarchy (1.5 marks):** Five levels — Physiological → Safety → Belonging → Esteem → Self-Actualization — lower needs act as *preconditions* for higher ones.
- **Implementation (1.5 marks):** Each level is a **behavioral layer** in a subsumption-style stack; a *preemption gate* monitors lower-layer deficits. If Layer 1 (battery) deficit > threshold, it **overrides** higher layers (e.g., curiosity-driven exploration) — robot recharges instead of exploring.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *Explain Russell's Circumplex Model of affect in detail. Derive the steady-state affective vector and the emotional decay law for a humanoid robot. Discuss the engineering advantage of continuous affect over discrete categories.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Russell's Model Description (3 marks):**
- Two dimensions: **Valence** (pleasant–unpleasant) and **Arousal** (calm–excited).
- All affective states plotted in a 2-D circular space; third optional axis is **Dominance**.
- Affective state vector: $\vec{A} = (v, a, d)$, each component in $[-1, 1]$.

**2. Derivation of Steady-State Affect (2 marks):**
- From Section 3.2, the affective ODE is $\tau \frac{d\vec{A}}{dt} = -\vec{A}(t) + \vec{I}(t) + \vec{b}$.
- At equilibrium ($\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt} = 0$): $\vec{A}^* = \vec{I} + \vec{b}$.

**3. Derivation of Decay Law (1 mark):**
- Without input ($\vec{I} = 0$, $\vec{b} = 0$): $\vec{A}(t) = \vec{A}(0) e^{-t/\tau}$.

**4. Engineering Advantage (1 mark):**
- **Continuous** transitions (smooth interpolation between affective states), no jarring jumps.
- Easy to combine multiple drives via *vector addition*.
- Compatible with fuzzy logic and gradient-based learning.

> **Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *Describe Schachter–Singer Two-Factor Theory. Implement it as a robotic affective controller for a humanoid exposed to a novel object. Show with equations how the emotion intensity is computed.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Theory (2 marks):**
- Emotion = Physiological Arousal × Cognitive Label/Attribution.
- A stimulus first causes undifferentiated arousal; then the agent *searches* for an explanation based on context.

**2. Robotic Implementation (3 marks):**
- **Sensor input** $s$ → novelty detector → raw arousal $A \in [0, 1]$.
- **Contextual memory** retrieves prior labels: $C = \text{softmax}(\text{similarity}(s, M_{context}))$ where $M_{context}$ is a memory matrix.
- **Emotion intensity:** $E = A \times C \in [0, 1]$.
- **Action selection:** if $E > \theta$, trigger *curiosity-approach* behavior; else, ignore.

**3. Worked Example (2 marks):**
- Novel object detected, $A = 0.8$. Best contextual match = "previous toys" with similarity $C = 0.6$.
- Therefore $E = 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48$.
- If curiosity threshold $\theta = 0.5$, robot *does not* approach → *cautious* behavior consistent with Schachter–Singer.

---

> **Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *Compare Maslow's Hierarchy and Drive Reduction Theory as motivation models for humanoid robots. Derive the homeostatic equilibrium equation and explain its engineering interpretation.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Maslow | Drive Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Hierarchical, qualitative | Quantitative, feedback-based |
| Variables | Discrete layers | Continuous homeostats |
| Robotic Mapping | Priority stack | PID/feedback controller |
| Strength | Handles high-level goals | Mathematically rigorous |
| Weakness | Hard to formalize | Limited to physiological needs |

**2. Derivation of Equilibrium (2.5 marks):**
- From Section 3.1, Step 6:
$$\lambda H^* = \eta A_{max} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k(H_{target}-H^*)}{A_{ref}}\right)$$

**3. Engineering Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- Robot settles where *actuation exactly balances natural decay*.
- Small $\eta$ (slow consumption) → $H^*$ falls below target → chronic under-motivation.
- Large $k$ (high drive gain) → $H^* \to H_{target}$ quickly → responsive but potentially oscillatory.
- Choosing $(k, \eta, \lambda)$ tunes the *personality* of the robot (sluggish vs. reactive).

> **Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
> *Explain the Yerkes–Dodson Law. Show its derivation as a Gaussian performance curve. How is it used in tuning the exploration rate of a reinforcement learning agent in a humanoid robot?*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Law Statement (1 mark):**
- Performance is an *inverted-U* function of arousal — moderate arousal optimizes learning/performance.

**2. Gaussian Derivation (3 marks):**
- Performance $P(a) = P_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{a - a^*}{\sigma}\right)^2\right]$
- Differentiating and equating to zero gives $a = a^*$ as the maximum (Section 3.3).
- $a^*$ is a decreasing function of task difficulty $d$:
$$a^*(d) = a_{min} + (a_{max} - a_{min}) e^{-\beta d}$$

**3. RL Exploration Application (3 marks):**
- In an $\epsilon$-greedy or softmax policy, set $\epsilon = 1 - P(a, d)$ (higher performance → less exploration).
- For *hard* tasks ($d$ large), $a^* \to a_{min}$ → robot stays in *low* arousal state → *cautious*, narrow exploration around best-known action.
- For *easy* tasks, $a^* \to a_{max}$ → robot in *high* arousal → broad exploration to discover better strategies.
- This emulates human-like curiosity modulation in humanoids.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (How Students Lose Marks in Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing "appraisal" and "arousal":** Appraisal is *cognitive*; arousal is *physiological/activation*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting robotic context:** A theory described without *mapping to a humanoid component* scores ≤ 50% in Part B. Always end a theory answer with "**in a humanoid, this is implemented as...**".
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps:** Even partial credit requires showing at least *three* steps of algebra in a derivation question.
> 4. **Misnaming variables:** Do not write $P = V \times A$ where $V$ = arousal. Use canonical notation: $E = A \times C$ (Schachter–Singer).
> 5. **Omitting time constant $\tau$** in Russell-based models — it is the *only* parameter that distinguishes one robot's "personality" from another's.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Emotion** = time-bounded affective state; **Motivation** = persistent goal-prioritizing drive.
- **Six major emotion models** to remember: James–Lange, Cannon–Bard, Schachter–Singer, Lazarus, Ekman, Russell.
- **Five major motivation models** to remember: Maslow, Drive Reduction, Arousal (Yerkes–Dodson), Self-Determination, Expectancy–Value.
- **Key formula in one line each:**
  - Schachter–Singer: $E = A \times C$
  - Russell PAD: $\vec{A} = (v, a, d)$, $\vert v \vert, \vert a \vert, \vert d \vert \le 1$
  - Drive Reduction: $\Delta D = k (H_{target} - H)$
  - Yerkes–Dodson: $P(a) = P_{max} e^{-(a-a^*)^2/\sigma^2}$
  - SDT: $r_{int} = w_A r_A + w_C r_C + w_R r_R$
  - Expectancy–Value: $M = E \times I \times V$
  - Affective Decay: $A(t) = A_0 e^{-t/\tau}$
- **Robotic instantiations** to cite by name: *Kismet* (Russell + Maslow), *Pepper* (Schachter–Singer), *Sophia* (Ekman + appraisal), *ASIMO* (Drive Reduction + Expectancy-Value).
- **Mapping mantra:** Emotion → *output channel* (face, voice, posture); Motivation → *control law* (priority, goal).
- **Always** conclude theory questions with a one-line robotic implementation — it scores the "application" mark.
- **Two appraisal stages** (Lazarus) = Primary (relevance) + Secondary (coping). Misnaming these is the #1 cause of lost marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception of Motion: Over View of ZMP, 2-D Analysis, Ground Reaction Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Perception of Motion: Over View of ZMP, 2-D Analysis, Ground Reaction Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Zero Moment Point (ZMP) — Formal Academic Definition

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the specific point on the ground (or the support polygon) where the net moment of the **inertial forces** and the **gravitational forces** acting on the robot's body has **zero horizontal components**. In simpler terms, it is the point on the contact surface where the tipping moment of the ground reaction force (GRF) vanishes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Vukobratovic, 1972):**
> The ZMP is defined as the point on the ground plane about which the sum of all active moments due to gravity and inertial forces acting on the robot's links equals zero along the horizontal axes (x and y), i.e., $M_x^{ZMP} = 0$ and $M_y^{ZMP} = 0$.

**Three foundational vectors that define bipedal balance:**

| Symbol | Term | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CoM** | Center of Mass | Geometric center where the robot's total mass is concentrated. |
| **CoP** | Center of Pressure | Point on the foot where the resultant ground reaction force is applied. |
| **ZMP** | Zero Moment Point | Point where the net horizontal moment of inertial + gravitational forces is zero. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Relationship (must memorize for KTU):**
> When the robot is in **dynamic equilibrium** (not falling), the **ZMP coincides with the CoP**. However, the ZMP and the projection of the CoM (often called the *pseudo-ZMP* in unstable states) are *different* points.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy: A Waiter Carrying a Tray on a Single Finger**

Imagine a waiter balancing a long stick vertically on the tip of his finger. The stick is the robot, the finger is the foot contact, and the table surface is the ground.

- If the waiter keeps the stick perfectly still, the point on the ground where the stick's weight is "felt" is the **ZMP** — a single spot directly under the stick.
- If the stick tilts or starts to fall, the **ZMP** shifts toward the edge of the contact area.
- The instant the ZMP crosses the edge of the finger (the support polygon), the stick **falls over** — equivalent to a humanoid robot toppling.

For a humanoid robot:
- The **support polygon** is the convex hull formed by the foot/feet in contact with the ground.
- The ZMP must remain **strictly inside** this polygon for the robot to remain balanced.
- A controller's job is to **plan the trajectory of the CoM** such that the resulting ZMP stays within the support polygon.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the ZMP as a "balance credit card" — as long as the card (ZMP) stays within the table (support polygon), the robot is safe. The instant the card falls off the table's edge, the robot tips over.

---

## 1.3 Ground Reaction Force (GRF) — Formal Definition

The **Ground Reaction Force (GRF)** is the equal and opposite force exerted by the ground on the robot's foot in response to the robot's weight and motion, as dictated by **Newton's Third Law**. The GRF has three orthogonal components:

- **Vertical component ($F_z$):** Supports the robot's weight and counteracts vertical acceleration.
- **Anterior-Posterior component ($F_x$):** Propels the robot forward or backward (walking/braking).
- **Medio-Lateral component ($F_y$):** Stabilizes side-to-side motion.

The **point of application** of this resultant GRF on the foot is the **Center of Pressure (CoP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:**
> ZMP = CoP **only when** the foot is flat on the ground and no tipping moment exists. If the robot is in a transient falling state, the ZMP mathematically leaves the support polygon and becomes a "Fictitious ZMP" (FZMP) — but the CoP is always bounded by the foot edges.

---

## 1.4 Why ZMP & GRF Matter in Humanoid Robotics

| Engineering Application | Role of ZMP/GRF |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Bipedal Gait Planning** | Generates stable walking patterns (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas, NAO). |
| **Push Recovery** | Detects external disturbances and adjusts foot placement. |
| **Terrain Adaptation** | Computes stable footholds on uneven ground. |
| **Sports Biomechanics** | Analyzes human athletes' running, jumping, and balance. |
| **Rehabilitation Robots** | Assists paraplegic patients in regaining walking ability. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard Earth gravity** used in all ZMP/GRF calculations is $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (or approximated as $9.8 \, m/s^2$ for KTU problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2-D Cart-Table Model (Foundational Model)

The **Cart-Table Model**, introduced by **Kajita et al. (2003)**, is the most widely-used simplified model for ZMP analysis in 2D. It treats the robot as a **massless table** supported on **two carts** (representing the feet), with the Center of Mass (CoM) moving on top of the table.

**Model Assumptions:**

1. The robot is reduced to a single point mass $m$ (the CoM).
2. The mass is constrained to move on a horizontal table at a constant height $z_c$.
3. The ground is rigid, flat, and horizontal.
4. The contact between the feet and ground has a single resultant force $F$ (the GRF).
5. The ZMP lies within the support polygon (stable region).

**Resultant Forces on the Cart (from the table's free body diagram):**

- The cart exerts a force $\tau$ on the mass, accelerating it horizontally.
- Gravity pulls the mass down: $mg$.
- The table pushes up with a normal force $N$ on the mass.

**Equilibrium of the cart mass (Newton's Second Law in x and z):**

- **Horizontal:** $F_x = m \cdot \ddot{x}$
- **Vertical:** $F_z - mg = m \cdot \ddot{z}$ → therefore $F_z = m(g + \ddot{z})$

**Taking moments about the ZMP point $(p_x, 0)$ on the ground:**

The moment of the inertial force $m\ddot{x}$ (acting at height $z$) and the gravitational force $mg$ (also acting at height $z$) about the ZMP must equal zero. The moment arm of the reaction force $F$ about the ZMP is zero by definition.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of ZMP Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the unknown ZMP coordinate:** Let the ZMP on the ground be at horizontal position $p_x$ (in 2D, the sagittal plane).

**Step 2 — Apply the moment balance condition:**
The total moment about the ZMP equals zero:
$$\tau_{gravity} + \tau_{inertial} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express moments explicitly:**
The gravity force $mg$ acts downward at $(x, z)$, so its moment arm about the ZMP is $(x - p_x)$:
$$M_{gravity} = -mg \cdot (x - p_x)$$

**Step 4 — Express inertial moment:**
The inertial (d'Alembert) force $m\ddot{x}$ acts horizontally (backward when accelerating forward) at height $z$. Its moment arm about the ZMP is the vertical height $z$:
$$M_{inertial} = -m\ddot{x} \cdot z$$

**Step 5 — Sum to zero and solve for $p_x$:**
$$-mg(x - p_x) - m\ddot{x} \cdot z = 0$$
$$p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the 2D ZMP equation (single mass / cart-table model).** It is the most frequently tested formula in KTU university exams.

**Step 6 — Generalize to multi-link robot:**
For a multi-rigid-body robot with $n$ links, the ZMP is computed by summing the contributions of each link:
$$p_x = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left[ x_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{x}_i \right]}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **2D ZMP (Cart-Table)** | $p_x = x - \dfrac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$ | $p_x$ (m), $x$ (m), $z$ (m), $\ddot{x}$ (m/s²), $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Constant height CoM: $z = h$, $\ddot{z} = 0$ |
| 2 | **Simplified 2D ZMP** | $p_x = x - \dfrac{h}{g} \cdot \ddot{x}$ | $h$ = constant CoM height (m) | Linear Inverted Pendulum Mode (LIPM) |
| 3 | **3D ZMP - x-axis** | $p_x = x - \dfrac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$ | Sagittal plane | Multi-body |
| 4 | **3D ZMP - y-axis** | $p_y = y - \dfrac{z \cdot \ddot{y}}{g + \ddot{z}}$ | Frontal (lateral) plane | Multi-body |
| 5 | **Multi-body ZMP** | $p_x = \dfrac{\sum m_i [x_i(\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{x}_i]}{\sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | Sum over all $n$ links | Full humanoid model |
| 6 | **Vertical GRF** | $F_z = m(g + \ddot{z})$ | $F_z$ in N, $m$ in kg | Newton's 2nd Law, vertical |
| 7 | **Horizontal GRF (x)** | $F_x = m \cdot \ddot{x}$ | $F_x$ in N | Sagittal acceleration |
| 8 | **LIPM Dynamics** | $\ddot{x} = \dfrac{g}{h}(x - p_x)$ | EOM of Linear Inverted Pendulum | $z = h$ constant, $\ddot{z} = 0$ |
| 9 | **CoP = ZMP condition** | $x_{CoP} = x_{ZMP}$ | Both on the ground | Foot flat, in dynamic equilibrium |
| 10 | **Newton-Euler GRF** | $\sum F_{ext} = m_{total} \cdot a_{CoM}$ | All external forces on robot | Ground is the source of $F_{ext}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention for KTU:** Use SI units throughout — meters (m), kilograms (kg), seconds (s), and Newtons (N). Angles in radians unless stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ZMP/GRF framework is the **backbone of every modern bipedal humanoid controller**:

- **Honda ASIMO** uses ZMP-based preview control for omnidirectional walking.
- **Boston Dynamics Atlas** uses Model Predictive Control (MPC) built on ZMP constraints for dynamic parkour.
- **SoftBank NAO/PEPPER** use simplified cart-table ZMP for stable gait on flat surfaces.
- **Rehabilitation exoskeletons** (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT) sense GRF via insole sensors to assist paraplegic gait.
- **Prosthetic legs** (e.g., Össur Power Knee) compute desired GRF profiles to mimic natural human walking.

The framework is also the **mathematical foundation of gait analysis** in sports medicine and clinical biomechanics, where force platforms directly measure the GRF vector.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: 2D ZMP for the Cart-Table Model

We consider the **Cart-Table Model** in the **sagittal plane (x-z plane)**, with the mass $m$ moving on top of a massless table that rests on the ground.

### Given:

- Cart position: $(x, z)$ — the CoM of the system.
- Cart acceleration: $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{z})$.
- Ground reaction force applied at the ZMP $(p_x, 0)$ on the ground.
- Gravity: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ acting downward (negative z-direction).

### Find:

The position $p_x$ of the Zero Moment Point on the ground.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the cart in the horizontal direction:**

The only horizontal force on the cart is the horizontal component of the ground reaction force, $F_x$. So:

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot \ddot{x}$$
$$F_x = m \cdot \ddot{x}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to the cart in the vertical direction:**

The vertical forces on the cart are the vertical GRF $F_z$ (upward) and gravity $mg$ (downward):

$$\sum F_z = m \cdot \ddot{z}$$
$$F_z - mg = m \cdot \ddot{z}$$
$$F_z = m(g + \ddot{z})$$

**Step 3 — Define the moment balance condition at the ZMP:**

By definition, the ZMP is the point on the ground where the **net moment of the ground reaction force is zero**. Equivalently, the moments of all other forces (gravity and inertia) about this point must sum to zero:

$$\sum M_{ZMP} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Express moments of individual forces about the ZMP $(p_x, 0)$:**

**Moment of gravity** ($mg$ acting downward at the cart's CoM $(x, z)$):
The moment arm is the horizontal distance $(x - p_x)$, and the sense (using right-hand rule) is such that gravity creates a moment that tends to tip the cart. Taking counter-clockwise as positive:

$$M_{gravity} = -mg(x - p_x)$$

The negative sign indicates that if the CoM is to the right of the ZMP ($x > p_x$), gravity creates a clockwise (negative) tipping moment.

**Moment of the inertial force** (d'Alembert's force $-m\ddot{x}$ acting horizontally at the CoM $(x, z)$):
The moment arm is the vertical height $z$ above the ground. If the cart accelerates in the positive x-direction ($\ddot{x} > 0$), the inertial force $-m\ddot{x}$ acts in the negative x-direction at height $z$, creating a counter-clockwise moment about the ZMP:

$$M_{inertial} = -m \cdot \ddot{x} \cdot z$$

**Step 5 — Sum the moments and set equal to zero:**

$$M_{gravity} + M_{inertial} = 0$$
$$-mg(x - p_x) - m\ddot{x} \cdot z = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for $p_x$:**

Expand the first term:
$$-mgx + mgp_x - m\ddot{x}z = 0$$

Rearrange to isolate $p_x$:
$$mgp_x = mgx + m\ddot{x}z$$

Divide both sides by $mg$ (assuming $m \neq 0$ and $g \neq 0$):
$$p_x = x + \frac{\ddot{x} \cdot z}{g}$$

Wait — let me re-derive this carefully. The correct sign depends on the sign convention of moments.

Let me re-do with a clearer sign convention. Take counter-clockwise (CCW) as positive moments. The cart at $(x, z)$:

- Gravity $mg$ pointing in the $-z$ direction at $(x, z)$.
- Inertial force $m\ddot{x}$ pointing in the $-x$ direction at $(x, z)$ (d'Alembert's principle — it's the pseudo-force needed to balance the real force $m\ddot{x}$ that the table exerts on the cart).

Hmm, let me restart with a cleaner approach using direct moment about the ZMP.

**Cleaner Derivation:**

The resultant ground reaction force $\vec{R} = (F_x, F_z)$ acts at the ZMP $(p_x, 0)$. By definition of the ZMP, the **moment of $\vec{R}$ about the ZMP is zero** (this is automatic since the moment arm is zero).

Now consider the **moment of all other forces** (gravity, $m\ddot{x}$ inertia) about the ZMP. By equilibrium of the whole system, the sum of all moments (including the GRF's, which is zero) about the ZMP must vanish:

$$M_{gravity, \, about \, ZMP} + M_{inertial, \, about \, ZMP} = 0$$

**Compute moment of gravity about ZMP $(p_x, 0)$:**
The force $\vec{F}_g = (0, -mg)$ acts at $(x, z)$. The position vector from the ZMP to the point of application is $\vec{r} = (x - p_x, z)$. The moment is:

$$\vec{M}_g = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}_g = (x - p_x, z, 0) \times (0, -mg, 0)$$

Using the cross product, only the z-component survives:
$$M_{g,z} = (x - p_x)(-mg) - z(0) = -mg(x - p_x)$$

**Compute moment of inertial (d'Alembert) force about ZMP:**
The inertial pseudo-force is $\vec{F}_{in} = (-m\ddot{x}, 0)$ (opposing the actual acceleration) acting at $(x, z)$:

$$\vec{M}_{in} = (x - p_x, z, 0) \times (-m\ddot{x}, 0, 0)$$

$$M_{in,z} = (x - p_x)(0) - z(-m\ddot{x}) = m\ddot{x} \cdot z$$

**Setting the total moment to zero:**

$$M_{g,z} + M_{in,z} = 0$$
$$-mg(x - p_x) + m\ddot{x} \cdot z = 0$$
$$mg(x - p_x) = m\ddot{x} \cdot z$$
$$x - p_x = \frac{\ddot{x} \cdot z}{g}$$
$$p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g}$$

**Step 7 — Generalize for variable CoM height (with vertical acceleration $\ddot{z}$):**

When the CoM has vertical acceleration $\ddot{z}$, the effective gravity term becomes $(g + \ddot{z})$ because the apparent weight changes. The derivation follows the same logic, but the vertical force balance gives $F_z = m(g + \ddot{z})$, and the final result is:

$$p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

This is the **general 2D ZMP equation**. The simplification $\ddot{z} = 0$, $z = h$ (constant) gives the famous **Linear Inverted Pendulum Mode (LIPM)** equation.

**Final Result — The 2D ZMP Equation:**

$$\boxed{p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention Quick Check:** If the cart accelerates forward ($\ddot{x} > 0$) at constant height, then $p_x < x$ — meaning the ZMP lies **behind** the CoM. This is intuitive: a forward-accelerating body must push back on the ground, and the ground pushes forward, with the application point of that force lying behind the CoM.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Multi-Body ZMP (Summation Form)

For a humanoid robot with $n$ rigid links, each link $i$ has:
- Mass: $m_i$
- Center of mass position: $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$
- Acceleration: $(\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{y}_i, \ddot{z}_i)$

**Step 1 — Total moment about the ZMP equals zero (sagittal plane):**

For the entire robot, taking moments about the ZMP $(p_x, p_y, 0)$ on the ground:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ M_{gravity,i} + M_{inertial,i} \right] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Sum the moments in the y-axis direction (roll axis), to find $p_x$:**

Each link contributes:
- Moment of gravity: $m_i \cdot g \cdot (x_i - p_x)$ about the y-axis
- Moment of inertia: $m_i \cdot \ddot{x}_i \cdot z_i - m_i \cdot \ddot{z}_i \cdot (x_i - p_x)$ about the y-axis

Wait, let me be more systematic. The moment about the y-axis of a force at $(x, y, z)$ is given by:

$$M_y = z F_x - x F_z$$

where $F_x$ and $F_z$ are the force components.

**For gravity on link $i$:** $F_x = 0$, $F_z = -m_i g$, applied at $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$:
$$M_{y, gravity, i} = z_i \cdot 0 - x_i \cdot (-m_i g) = m_i g x_i$$

**For inertia on link $i$:** $F_x = -m_i \ddot{x}_i$, $F_z = -m_i \ddot{z}_i$ (d'Alembert), applied at $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$:
$$M_{y, inertia, i} = z_i(-m_i \ddot{x}_i) - x_i(-m_i \ddot{z}_i) = -m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i + m_i x_i \ddot{z}_i$$

**Total moment about y-axis from all links:**
$$M_y^{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i g x_i - m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i + m_i x_i \ddot{z}_i \right]$$

**The moment of the ground reaction force about the ZMP is zero by definition.** But we also need to include the moment contribution of the GRF when computing the total. The GRF is applied at $(p_x, p_y, 0)$:

$$M_{y, GRF} = 0 \cdot F_{x,GRF} - p_x \cdot F_{z,GRF} = -p_x \cdot F_{z,GRF}$$

**The vertical GRF equals the total apparent weight:**

$$F_{z,GRF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Total moment equilibrium: $M_y^{total} + M_{y, GRF} = 0$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i g x_i - m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i + m_i x_i \ddot{z}_i \right] - p_x \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p_x$:**

Rearranging:
$$p_x \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ m_i g x_i - m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i + m_i x_i \ddot{z}_i \right]$$

Factoring $m_i$ and combining $g x_i + x_i \ddot{z}_i = x_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)$:

$$p_x \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left[ x_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) - z_i \ddot{x}_i \right]$$

Dividing both sides:

$$\boxed{p_x = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left[ x_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) - z_i \ddot{x}_i \right]}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}}$$

This is the **general multi-body ZMP equation in the x-direction**. By symmetry, swapping x with y and y-axis with x-axis:

$$p_y = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left[ y_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) - z_i \ddot{y}_i \right]}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification with Single Mass:**
> For $n = 1$ (single mass $m$ at $(x, z)$ with acceleration $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{z})$):
> $$p_x = \frac{m [x(g + \ddot{z}) - z\ddot{x}]}{m(g + \ddot{z})} = x - \frac{z\ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$
> This matches the cart-table result. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ground Reaction Force Components

The GRF is the **resultant force** the ground exerts on the robot. By Newton's Third Law, it is equal and opposite to the force the robot exerts on the ground.

**Step 1 — Sum of all external forces equals the rate of change of total momentum:**

For a robot with total mass $M$ and CoM acceleration $\vec{a}_{CoM} = (\ddot{x}_{CoM}, \ddot{y}_{CoM}, \ddot{z}_{CoM})$:

$$\vec{F}_{GRF} + M\vec{g} = M \vec{a}_{CoM}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for GRF components:**

- **Vertical (z):** $F_{z, GRF} = M(g + \ddot{z}_{CoM})$
- **Horizontal x:** $F_{x, GRF} = M \ddot{x}_{CoM}$
- **Horizontal y:** $F_{y, GRF} = M \ddot{y}_{CoM}$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the total GRF:**

$$|\vec{F}_{GRF}| = \sqrt{F_{x,GRF}^2 + F_{y,GRF}^2 + F_{z,GRF}^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut:** For a humanoid standing still, $\vec{a}_{CoM} = 0$, so the only GRF is vertical: $F_{z, GRF} = Mg$. This is just the robot's weight! For a robot walking, $F_x$ and $F_y$ become nonzero, providing the propulsive and lateral forces needed for locomotion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | CoM State | ZMP Location | Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standing still on flat ground | $\ddot{x} = \ddot{y} = \ddot{z} = 0$ | Directly below CoM, inside foot | ✓ Stable |
| Walking forward (constant height) | $\ddot{x} > 0$, $\ddot{z} = 0$ | $p_x = x - (h/g)\ddot{x}$, behind CoM | Stable if $p_x$ in support polygon |
| Squatting down | $\ddot{z} < 0$ | $p_x = x - (h\ddot{x})/(g + \ddot{z})$ | Slightly different from above |
| Pushed backward (recovering) | $\ddot{x} < 0$ | $p_x = x - (h/g)\ddot{x}$, ahead of CoM | Stable if $p_x$ in support polygon |
| Tipping over (unstable) | Large $\ddot{x}$ | $p_x$ exits support polygon | ✗ Falls |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Cart-Table Model (2D Sagittal Plane)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Cart-Table Model** in the sagittal (x-z) plane, showing the forces and the ZMP location.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Cart (CoM)<br/>Mass m<br/>Position x, z"] -->|Inertial Force<br/>m * x_ddot| B["Free Body<br/>Force Diagram"]
    A -->|Gravity<br/>m * g downward| B
    B -->|Ground Reaction<br/>F_x horizontal| C["ZMP Point<br/>p_x on ground"]
    B -->|Ground Reaction<br/>F_z vertical| C
    C -->|Moment about ZMP<br/>equals ZERO| D["Stability<br/>Condition"]
    D -->|"p_x in support polygon"| E["ROBOT STABLE"]
    D -->|"p_x outside support polygon"| F["ROBOT FALLS"]
    G["Support Polygon<br/>Convex hull of feet"] -->|Defines stability region| D
    H["Walking Pattern<br/>Generator"] -->|"Plans CoM trajectory x_t"| A
    I["ZMP Reference<br/>Trajectory p_x_ref"] -->|"Target position"| C
    H -->|"Feeds to controller"| I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The cart (representing the CoM) is subjected to **gravity** (downward) and an **inertial force** (horizontal, due to acceleration).
- The ground exerts a **reaction force** $(F_x, F_z)$ at the ZMP.
- The **moment of the GRF about the ZMP is zero by definition**.
- If the computed ZMP $p_x$ lies **inside the support polygon** (formed by the feet), the robot remains **stable**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ZMP-Based Walking Controller

This diagram shows the **closed-loop control architecture** used in modern humanoid robots to maintain ZMP-based balance.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Planner["PLANNER LAYER"]
        A1["Desired Footstep<br/>Locations"] --> A2["ZMP Reference<br/>Trajectory"]
        A2 --> A3["CoM Trajectory<br/>Generator (MPC<br/>or Preview Control)"]
    end
    subgraph Controller["CONTROLLER LAYER"]
        A3 --> B1["Inverse Dynamics<br/>Compute Joint<br/>Torques"]
        B1 --> B2["Joint Servos<br/>and Motors"]
    end
    subgraph Robot["PHYSICAL ROBOT"]
        B2 --> C1["Humanoid Body<br/>Links and Joints"]
        C1 --> C2["Force/Torque<br/>Sensors on Feet"]
        C1 --> C3["IMU on Pelvis"]
    end
    subgraph Feedback["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        C2 --> D1["GRF and CoP<br/>Estimator"]
        C3 --> D2["Orientation and<br/>Acceleration"]
        D1 --> D3["Measured ZMP<br/>Comparison"]
        D2 --> D3
        A2 --> D3
        D3 --> E1["Error Signal<br/>ZMP_ref minus ZMP_meas"]
        E1 --> A3
    end
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

1. **Planner Layer:** Generates desired footstep locations and a ZMP reference trajectory.
2. **CoM Trajectory Generator:** Uses **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** or **Preview Control** to compute CoM motion that keeps the ZMP on its reference.
3. **Controller Layer:** Computes joint torques via inverse dynamics and sends commands to motor drivers.
4. **Physical Robot:** Executes the motion, with sensors measuring GRF (at the feet) and orientation (at the pelvis/IMU).
5. **Feedback Layer:** Computes the *measured* ZMP from the GRF distribution and compares it to the reference. The error is fed back to the planner for correction.

---

## 4.3 ZMP Stability Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start(["Robot in Motion"]) --> Step1["Compute CoM Position x, y, z and Acceleration x_ddot, y_ddot, z_ddot"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Apply ZMP Equation<br/>p_x = x minus z times x_ddot divided by g plus z_ddot"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Compute p_y similarly"]
    Step3 --> Step4{"Is p_x, p_y inside<br/>support polygon?"}
    Step4 -->|"Yes"| Stable["ROBOT IS STABLE<br/>Continue motion"]
    Step4 -->|"No"| Unstable["ROBOT WILL TIP<br/>Trigger recovery"]
    Unstable --> Rec1["Option 1: Adjust CoM<br/>trajectory"]
    Unstable --> Rec2["Option 2: Change<br/>support foot"]
    Unstable --> Rec3["Option 3: Take a<br/>recovery step"]
    Rec1 --> Step1
    Rec2 --> Step1
    Rec3 --> Step1
    Stable --> Continue(["Continue walking cycle"])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Note:** In KTU problems, "support polygon" typically refers to a single rectangle (one foot) or a wider rectangle (both feet together). Always compute the polygon vertices first, then check if the ZMP coordinates lie within the convex hull.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) as used in humanoid robotics. Mention its significance in bipedal walking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**, introduced by Vukobratovic in 1972, is the point on the ground (support surface) where the net moment of the inertial forces and the gravitational forces acting on a robot's links has zero horizontal components, i.e., $M_x^{ZMP} = M_y^{ZMP} = 0$. (2 marks)

**Significance:** For stable bipedal walking, the ZMP must remain strictly within the support polygon (the convex hull of the foot/feet contact areas). The ZMP criterion is the most widely used real-time stability test for humanoid robots, as it provides a computationally simple check for whether the robot will tip over. (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the Ground Reaction Force (GRF)? Write the expressions for its three orthogonal components for a robot of total mass $M$ with CoM acceleration $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{y}, \ddot{z})$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ground Reaction Force (GRF)** is the force exerted by the ground on the robot's foot, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the force the robot exerts on the ground, as per **Newton's Third Law**. (1 mark)

The three components are:
- **Vertical:** $F_z = M(g + \ddot{z})$ (2 marks)
- **Sagittal (x):** $F_x = M\ddot{x}$
- **Lateral (y):** $F_y = M\ddot{y}$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) in 2D for a cart-table model with a single mass $m$ moving at position $(x, z)$ with acceleration $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{z})$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A humanoid robot of total mass $M = 50$ kg walks on a flat floor at a constant CoM height of $h = 0.9$ m. During a particular step, the CoM accelerates forward at $\ddot{x} = 0.5$ m/s². The foot is a rectangle of length $0.25$ m and the heel of the foot is at $x = 0$. Compute the ZMP position and check whether the robot will tip. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Assumptions of the cart-table model:**
1. The robot is represented as a single point mass (CoM) on top of a massless table.
2. The mass moves at a height $z$ above the ground (not necessarily constant).
3. The ground is rigid, flat, and horizontal.
4. The contact between the foot and ground produces a single resultant force — the GRF.
5. The ZMP lies within the support polygon (stable condition).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Force balance on the mass:**
- Horizontal: $F_x = m\ddot{x}$
- Vertical: $F_z = m(g + \ddot{z})$

**Step 2 — Moment balance about the ZMP $(p_x, 0)$:**

The moment of the gravity force ($mg$ at $(x,z)$) and the inertial force ($m\ddot{x}$ at $(x,z)$) about the ZMP must sum to zero.

$$M_{gravity} + M_{inertial} = 0$$
$$-mg(x - p_x) - m\ddot{x} \cdot z = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $p_x$:**

$$p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

This is the desired 2D ZMP equation. ✓

**Model Answer Rubric (7 marks):**
- Assumptions listed correctly: 2 marks
- Force balance (horizontal + vertical): 2 marks
- Moment balance equation: 2 marks
- Final solved ZMP expression: 1 mark

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $M = 50$ kg
- $h = z = 0.9$ m (constant height, so $\ddot{z} = 0$)
- $\ddot{x} = 0.5$ m/s²
- Foot length = $0.25$ m
- Heel at $x = 0$ (so foot occupies $x \in [0, 0.25]$)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Apply the 2D ZMP equation (LIPM form):**

$$p_x = x - \frac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

Since $\ddot{z} = 0$ and $z = h = 0.9$ m:

$$p_x = x - \frac{0.9 \times 0.5}{9.81 + 0}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ZMP shift from the CoM:**

$$\Delta p = \frac{0.9 \times 0.5}{9.81} = \frac{0.45}{9.81} \approx 0.0459 \text{ m} \approx 4.59 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the ZMP position:**

$$p_x = x - 0.0459 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Stability check:**

The foot is from $x = 0$ to $x = 0.25$ m. The ZMP must satisfy $0 \leq p_x \leq 0.25$ m.

For the ZMP to be on the foot: $x - 0.0459 \in [0, 0.25]$, i.e., $x \in [0.0459, 0.2959]$ m.

If the CoM is at any $x$ in this range, **the robot will NOT tip**.

**Example numerical check:** Suppose $x = 0.15$ m (middle of the foot):
$$p_x = 0.15 - 0.0459 = 0.1041 \text{ m} \approx 10.41 \text{ cm}$$

This is **inside the foot** ($0 \leq 0.1041 \leq 0.25$), so the robot is **stable**. ✓

**Model Answer Rubric (7 marks):**
- Correct identification of LIPM form: 1 mark
- Substitution of values: 2 marks
- Calculation of $\Delta p$: 2 marks
- Stability check with conclusion: 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the support polygon and the CoP (Center of Pressure). State the relationship between ZMP, CoP, and the CoM projection on the ground. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bipedal robot has two links: the torso (link 1) and the right leg (link 2). The data is given below:

| Link | Mass (kg) | CoM position $(x, z)$ in m | CoM acceleration $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{z})$ in m/s² |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Torso | 30 | $(0.0, 0.8)$ | $(0.2, 0.0)$ |
| Right Leg | 10 | $(0.0, 0.3)$ | $(0.2, 0.0)$ |

Compute the ZMP position using the multi-body formula. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Support Polygon:**
The **support polygon** is the **convex hull** of all contact points between the robot's feet (or other body parts in contact with the ground) and the ground surface. (1 mark)
- For single support (one foot on ground): The polygon is the convex hull of the foot's contact area — typically a rectangle.
- For double support (both feet on ground): The polygon is the convex hull of both feet, forming a larger region (often a hexagon or octagon).
- For point contact (e.g., toe-only): The polygon degenerates to a line or a point. (1 mark)

**Center of Pressure (CoP):**
The **Center of Pressure (CoP)** is the point on the contact surface where the **resultant ground reaction force** is applied. It is computed as the centroid of the pressure distribution under the foot. (2 marks)

**Relationship between ZMP, CoP, and CoM projection:**

- **ZMP** = the point on the ground where the net horizontal moment of the inertial + gravitational forces is zero. (1 mark)
- **CoP** = the point on the ground where the resultant GRF is applied.
- **ZMP = CoP** when the foot is flat and in dynamic equilibrium (no tipping). (1 mark)
- The **projection of the CoM on the ground** (sometimes called the *xCoM* or *eCMP* in advanced models) is **different** from the ZMP. The ZMP lies **closer to the support foot** than the CoM projection when the robot is accelerating. (1 mark)

**Model Answer Rubric (7 marks):**
- Support polygon definition with examples: 2 marks
- CoP definition: 2 marks
- ZMP = CoP condition: 2 marks
- CoM projection distinction: 1 mark

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**

| Link | $m_i$ (kg) | $x_i$ (m) | $z_i$ (m) | $\ddot{x}_i$ (m/s²) | $\ddot{z}_i$ (m/s²) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (Torso) | 30 | 0.0 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 0.0 |
| 2 (Right Leg) | 10 | 0.0 | 0.3 | 0.2 | 0.0 |

**Step 1 — Recall the multi-body ZMP equation:**

$$p_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left[ x_i (g + \ddot{z}_i) - z_i \ddot{x}_i \right]}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (link by link):**

For link 1 (Torso):
- $x_1 (g + \ddot{z}_1) = 0.0 \times (9.81 + 0.0) = 0$
- $z_1 \ddot{x}_1 = 0.8 \times 0.2 = 0.16$
- Numerator contribution: $30 \times [0 - 0.16] = -4.8$

For link 2 (Right Leg):
- $x_2 (g + \ddot{z}_2) = 0.0 \times (9.81 + 0.0) = 0$
- $z_2 \ddot{x}_2 = 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.06$
- Numerator contribution: $10 \times [0 - 0.06] = -0.6$

**Total numerator:**
$$\sum = -4.8 + (-0.6) = -5.4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

For link 1: $30 \times (9.81 + 0.0) = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3$

For link 2: $10 \times (9.81 + 0.0) = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1$

**Total denominator:**
$$\sum = 294.3 + 98.1 = 392.4$$

**Step 4 — Compute $p_x$:**

$$p_x = \frac{-5.4}{392.4} \approx -0.01376 \text{ m} \approx -1.376 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The ZMP is at $p_x \approx -1.376$ cm, which is **behind the CoM** (which is at $x = 0$). The negative sign indicates the ZMP is in the negative x-direction from the CoM's vertical projection, consistent with a forward acceleration. The robot is stable as long as the foot extends at least 1.376 cm in the negative x-direction from the CoM.

**Model Answer Rubric (7 marks):**
- Correct formula statement: 1 mark
- Numerator calculation (2 links × 2 marks): 2 marks
- Denominator calculation: 2 marks
- Final $p_x$ value and physical interpretation: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ZMP/GRF Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** The ZMP equation $p_x = x - (z\ddot{x})/(g + \ddot{z})$ assumes CCW positive moments. Mixing conventions leads to sign errors in $p_x$.
> 
> 2. **Using $g = 9.8$ vs $g = 9.81$:** KTU problems often specify $g$ — always use the value given in the problem. If unspecified, use **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** (the more accurate value).
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to check the support polygon:** Computing $p_x$ alone is **NOT** enough. You must verify that $(p_x, p_y)$ lies **inside** the support polygon to conclude stability.
> 
> 4. **Confusing CoP with ZMP in unstable cases:** When the robot is tipping, the ZMP (mathematically) exits the support polygon, but the CoP cannot (it stays on the foot). In such cases, the computed ZMP is called the **Fictitious ZMP (FZMP)**, and the relationship ZMP = CoP breaks down.
> 
> 5. **Missing the vertical acceleration term:** If the problem gives $\ddot{z} \neq 0$, do **NOT** drop the $(g + \ddot{z})$ term. The simplified LIPM form $p_x = x - (h/g)\ddot{x}$ is **only valid for constant CoM height**.
> 
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always convert cm to m (or vice versa) before substitution. Mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ZMP, 2D Analysis & GRF**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- ☐ ZMP = point on ground where horizontal moments of gravity + inertia = zero
- ☐ CoP = point on foot where resultant GRF is applied
- ☐ ZMP = CoP **only** when foot is flat and in equilibrium
- ☐ Support Polygon = convex hull of foot contact area(s)
- ☐ Stability condition: ZMP must lie inside the support polygon

### **Must-Know Formulas:**
- ☐ **2D ZMP (general):** $p_x = x - \dfrac{z \cdot \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$
- ☐ **2D ZMP (LIPM):** $p_x = x - \dfrac{h}{g} \cdot \ddot{x}$  *(when $z = h$ constant)*
- ☐ **Multi-body ZMP (sagittal):** $p_x = \dfrac{\sum m_i [x_i(g + \ddot{z}_i) - z_i \ddot{x}_i]}{\sum m_i (g + \ddot{z}_i)}$
- ☐ **Vertical GRF:** $F_z = M(g + \ddot{z})$
- ☐ **Horizontal GRF:** $F_x = M\ddot{x}$, $F_y = M\ddot{y}$
- ☐ **LIPM Dynamics:** $\ddot{x} = \dfrac{g}{h}(x - p_x)$

### **Critical Physical Intuition:**
- ☐ Forward acceleration → ZMP shifts **behind** CoM
- ☐ Backward acceleration → ZMP shifts **ahead** of CoM
- ☐ A robot's "balance credit" is the ZMP — it must stay on the "table" of the support polygon
- ☐ GRF vertical component equals the apparent weight (true weight + inertial vertical force)

### **Common KTU Problem Patterns:**
- ☐ Given CoM trajectory, compute ZMP, check stability against a rectangular support polygon
- ☐ Given a multi-link robot with mass distribution, compute ZMP using the summation formula
- ☐ Compute GRF components during walking/running/jumping phases
- ☐ Apply the cart-table model for simplified LIPM analysis

### **Numerical Values to Remember:**
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s² (or 9.8 m/s², depending on problem)
- ☐ Typical humanoid CoM height: $0.7 - 1.0$ m
- ☐ Typical humanoid foot length: $0.20 - 0.30$ m
- ☐ Typical humanoid foot width: $0.08 - 0.12$ m

### **Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Confused):**
- ☐ **ZMP ≠ CoM projection** — ZMP is on the ground, CoM is in 3D space
- ☐ **ZMP ≠ FZMP** — FZMP is computed when robot is tipping (ZMP outside support)
- ☐ **ZMP criterion ≠ Zero Moment Point theorem** — criterion is the practical application
- ☐ **GRF ≠ Friction force** — GRF is the total contact force, friction is one component
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics: Humanoid Robot Motion and Ground Reaction Forces, Calculation of Centre of Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamics: Humanoid Robot Motion and Ground Reaction Forces, Calculation of Centre of Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Humanoid Robot Dynamics — Formal Definition

**Humanoid robot dynamics** is the branch of multi-body mechanics that studies the forces, torques, and resulting motions of a bipedal, anthropomorphic robot interacting with the ground. Because a humanoid is an **under-actuated, floating-base, multi-rigid-body system**, its dynamics cannot be described by standard fixed-base manipulator equations. The dynamics is governed by:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{S}^T\boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{J}_c^T \mathbf{F}_{GRF}$$

where $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is the mass/inertia matrix, $\mathbf{C}$ captures Coriolis/centrifugal effects, $\mathbf{G}$ is gravity, $\mathbf{S}^T\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the actuator input (mapped from joint torques), and $\mathbf{J}_c^T \mathbf{F}_{GRF}$ represents the **Ground Reaction Forces** transmitted through the foot contacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A humanoid has typically **23–35 degrees of freedom** but only **6 independent base DOF**. Hence **floating-base dynamics** and contact constraints (GRF) are mandatory topics in Module 4.

### 1.2 Ground Reaction Forces (GRF) — Formal Definition

**Ground Reaction Force (GRF)** is the equal and opposite force exerted by the ground on the robot's foot (Newton's Third Law) during locomotion. It has three components:
- Vertical: $\mathbf{F}_z$ (supports body weight)
- Horizontal: $\mathbf{F}_x$, $\mathbf{F}_y$ (propulsion / braking)
- Associated moment: $\mathbf{M}_x$, $\mathbf{M}_y$, $\mathbf{M}_z$ (used to compute the **Zero Moment Point / Center of Pressure**)

### 1.3 Centre of Mass (CoM) — Formal Definition

The **Centre of Mass (CoM)** of a humanoid robot is the unique point in 3-D space where the weighted average position of all distributed mass is concentrated. Mathematically, for $N$ rigid links:

$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \, \mathbf{r}_{Ci}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i}$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of link $i$ and $\mathbf{r}_{Ci}$ is the position of the CoM of link $i$.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — GRF:** Imagine you standing on a bathroom weighing scale. The scale reads your weight because the floor pushes UP on your feet with a force equal to $mg$. That upward push *is* the Ground Reaction Force. If you lean forward, the GRF shifts to the front of your foot. If you push off the back foot to walk, the GRF has a backward horizontal component — that is the **propulsive** force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — CoM:** Think of a **hammer throwing athlete**. The hammer has mass at the head and a long handle. The "sweet spot" where the athlete balances the hammer on one finger is the **Centre of Mass**. For a humanoid, the CoM is roughly near the **pelvis (sacrum)** when standing — slightly above the navel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — ZMP (linked to GRF):** When you walk, there is one magical point on the sole of your foot where the net moment of all ground forces equals **zero**. That point is the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**. If the ZMP stays *inside* the footprint, you do not fall; if it leaves the foot, you tip over.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Typical Humanoid Value |
|---|---|
| Total Mass ($M$) | **30 – 60 kg** (e.g., ASIMO: 48 kg, NAO: 5.2 kg) |
| Total Height | **0.5 – 1.3 m** |
| Standing CoM height ($h$) | **~55 % of body height** |
| Gravitational acceleration ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Coefficient of friction ($\mu$) | **0.5 – 1.0** (rubber sole on tile) |
| Support Polygon (SS) | Single foot convex hull |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Humanoid Dynamics Difficult?

A humanoid robot is special because:

1. **Floating Base**: There is no fixed base bolted to the world — the pelvis (or trunk) moves freely in 6 DOF. This means 6 of the generalized coordinates are *not* actuated; they are driven indirectly through foot contacts.
2. **Under-actuation**: A typical humanoid has ~25 actuators but needs to control 31 DOF → **6 passive DOF**.
3. **Contact Constraints**: Feet alternate between *single support*, *double support*, and *flight* phases. Each phase imposes a different set of holonomic constraints.
4. **GRF is the only "external" propulsion source** — there is no wheel, no propeller. Walking is essentially *controlled falling*.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Computing Dynamics

The dynamics problem is solved in three nested levels:

**Level 1 — Whole-Body Newton-Euler (Linear Momentum):**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \, \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci} = M \, \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_c} \mathbf{F}_j + M\mathbf{g}$$

where $\mathbf{F}_j$ are contact forces at $n_c$ support points.

**Level 2 — Whole-Body Angular Momentum about CoM:**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\sum_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci/CoM} \times m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci}\right) = \dot{\mathbf{H}}_{CoM} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_c} (\mathbf{p}_j - \mathbf{r}_{CoM}) \times \mathbf{F}_j$$

**Level 3 — Joint-Space Dynamics (Lagrangian form):**
$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{S}^T\boldsymbol{\tau} + \sum_{j=1}^{n_c} \mathbf{J}_{cj}^T \mathbf{F}_j$$

### 2.3 The Zero Moment Point (ZMP) — Core Derivation Idea

The **ZMP** is the point $(p_x, p_y, 0)$ on the ground where the moment of the ground reaction force about the horizontal axes equals zero:

$$M_x^{ZMP} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad M_y^{ZMP} = 0$$

Using the linear momentum balance $M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{F}_{GRF} + M\mathbf{g}$ and the angular momentum balance about the ZMP, the classic Kajita et al. (1991) formula emerges:

$$p_x = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{x}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

$$p_y = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{y}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (Kajita):** The robot does NOT fall as long as the computed ZMP lies inside the **support polygon** (the convex hull of all contact points). If $p_x, p_y$ leave this polygon, the foot will rotate about an edge — the robot topples.

### 2.4 Ground Reaction Force — Friction Cone Constraint

For the foot not to slip, the horizontal GRF components must lie inside the **friction cone**:

$$\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \;\leq\; \mu \, F_z$$

where $\mu$ is the static coefficient of friction. This can be linearised as a 4-inequality pyramid:

$$\vert F_x \vert \;\leq\; \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2}} F_z, \quad \vert F_y \vert \;\leq\; \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2}} F_z$$

### 2.5 Centre of Mass Calculation — Two Approaches

**Approach A — Direct (Discrete) Summation:**
$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \frac{\sum_{i} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i} m_i}, \quad \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{\sum_i m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_i}{\sum_i m_i}, \quad \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{\sum_i m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i}{\sum_i m_i}$$

**Approach B — Jacobian (Forward Kinematics based):**

For a humanoid with generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q}$, the CoM of link $i$ is:
$$\mathbf{r}_{Ci}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{r}_{base}(\mathbf{q}) + \sum_{k=1}^{i} {}^{base}\mathbf{T}_k(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{r}_{Ci}^{\text{local}}$$

Its Jacobian: $\mathbf{J}_{Ci} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{r}_{Ci}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$

Total CoM Jacobian:
$$\mathbf{J}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{J}_{Ci}$$

So:
$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{J}_{CoM} \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{J}_{CoM} \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}_{CoM}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why it Matters |
|---|---|
| **ASIMO / Atlas / HRP series** | Stable walking requires ZMP inside the support polygon at every instant. |
| **Lower-limb exoskeletons** | GRF sensors in the shoe measure real-time $\mathbf{F}_z$ to estimate user intent. |
| **Prosthetic legs** | CoM trajectory planning (preview control) ensures smooth, stable gait. |
| **Fall detection & prevention** | If computed ZMP nears polygon boundary, ankle/hip strategy triggers. |
| **Sports biomechanics** | GRF plates analyse human athletes (running, jumping). |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Compact)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{M}$ | Position of whole-body CoM | m |
| 2 | $M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \sum \mathbf{F}_j + M\mathbf{g}$ | Linear momentum balance | N |
| 3 | $\dot{\mathbf{H}}_{CoM} = \sum (\mathbf{p}_j - \mathbf{r}_{CoM})\times\mathbf{F}_j$ | Angular momentum balance | N·m |
| 4 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{G} = \mathbf{S}^T\boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{J}_c^T\mathbf{F}_{GRF}$ | Joint-space dynamics | N·m |
| 5 | $p_x = \dfrac{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)x_i - \sum m_i\ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)}$ | ZMP x-coordinate | m |
| 6 | $p_y = \dfrac{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)y_i - \sum m_i\ddot{y}_i z_i}{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)}$ | ZMP y-coordinate | m |
| 7 | $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} \le \mu F_z$ | Friction cone (no-slip) | N |
| 8 | $\mathbf{J}_{CoM} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum m_i \mathbf{J}_{Ci}$ | CoM Jacobian | — |
| 9 | $\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{J}_{CoM}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}_{CoM}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | CoM acceleration | m/s² |
| 10 | $\text{Stable} \iff (p_x, p_y) \in \text{Support Polygon}$ | Kajita stability criterion | — |
