## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centre of Mass of a Multi-Link Humanoid

**Problem Setup:** A humanoid is modelled as $N$ rigid links with masses $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_N$ and individual CoM positions $\mathbf{r}_{C1}, \mathbf{r}_{C2}, \dots, \mathbf{r}_{CN}$. Derive the global CoM position, velocity, and acceleration.

**Step 1 — Definition (Mass-Weighted Average):**

By definition, the whole-body CoM is the mass-weighted average of every particle's position. For a discretised multi-link robot:

$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \frac{\int_{\text{body}} \mathbf{r} \, \rho(\mathbf{r}) \, dV}{\int_{\text{body}} \rho(\mathbf{r}) \, dV} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity (Differentiate w.r.t. time):**

Because total mass $M = \sum m_i$ is constant:

$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}\right) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration (Differentiate again):**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for humanoids:** The total linear momentum of the robot $\mathbf{P} = M\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$ is a *single* 3-D vector — even though the robot has 30+ DOF. This is the basis of **centroidal dynamics** and is what makes ZMP tractable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ZMP from CoM Dynamics (Cart-Table Analogue)

The **Cart-Table model** by Kajita et al. (1991) is the canonical derivation. Consider a massless table of height $h$ with a cart (mass $M$) sliding on top. The ZMP is the point under the table that compensates all moments.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law on the cart (linear):**

The only forces on the cart are gravity $-Mg\hat{z}$ and the table reaction $\mathbf{F} = (F_x, 0, F_z)$ applied at the table top. Sum of forces:

$$M\ddot{x} = F_x, \quad M\ddot{z} = F_z - Mg$$

**Step 2 — Moment Balance about the ground ZMP $(p_x, 0, 0)$:**

Take moments about $(p_x, 0, 0)$. The reaction $\mathbf{F}$ is applied at the table-top, which is at height $h$ above the ground. Distance from ZMP to application point is $(0 - p_x, 0, h)$. Moment (about y-axis only is non-trivial in 2-D):

$$M_y = -F_x \cdot h + M(\ddot{x} + g)(0 - p_x) = 0$$

(The term $M(\ddot{x}+g)(0-p_x)$ comes from the inertial + gravitational force acting on the CoM at horizontal offset from ZMP.)

**Step 3 — Solve for $p_x$:**

Rearrange:

$$-F_x h + M(\ddot{x}+g)(-p_x) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F_x h = -M(\ddot{x}+g)p_x$$

Substitute $F_x = M\ddot{x}$:

$$M\ddot{x} \, h = -M(\ddot{x}+g)p_x \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p_x = -\frac{h\,\ddot{x}}{\ddot{x}+g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative sign convention:** Some textbooks flip the x-axis; Kajita's convention gives $p_x = x - \dfrac{h\ddot{x}}{\ddot{z}+g}$. Be careful with sign in the exam!

**Step 4 — Extend to 3-D Multi-Body Robot:**

Replace the single cart-mass with the multi-link CoM. Use the identity $\sum m_i \mathbf{r}_i = M\mathbf{r}_{CoM}$. Then the ZMP in 3-D becomes:

$$p_x = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{x}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

$$p_y = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{y}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The denominator $\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g) = M(\ddot{z}_{CoM}+g) \approx Mg$ when the vertical acceleration is small. So in **quasi-static walking**, the denominator reduces to the body weight $Mg$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ground Reaction Force from Linear Momentum Balance

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's law to whole robot:**

$$M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = M\mathbf{g} + \sum_{j=1}^{n_c} \mathbf{F}_j$$

where $\mathbf{F}_j$ is the GRF at contact $j$ (e.g., left foot, right foot).

**Step 2 — Single Support Phase ($n_c = 1$):**

Only one foot is on the ground, say the right foot at position $\mathbf{p}_R$:

$$\mathbf{F}_{R} = M(\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} - \mathbf{g})$$

The friction cone constraint becomes:

$$\sqrt{F_{Rx}^2 + F_{Ry}^2} \le \mu F_{Rz}$$

**Step 3 — Double Support Phase ($n_c = 2$):**

Force distribution is *not unique*. We need to solve via QP (Quadratic Programming):

$$\min_{\mathbf{F}_L,\mathbf{F}_R} \; \mathbf{F}_L^T \mathbf{W}_L \mathbf{F}_L + \mathbf{F}_R^T \mathbf{W}_R \mathbf{F}_R$$

subject to:
- $M(\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}-\mathbf{g}) = \mathbf{F}_L + \mathbf{F}_R$
- $\sqrt{F_{Lx}^2+F_{Ly}^2} \le \mu F_{Lz}$
- $\sqrt{F_{Rx}^2+F_{Ry}^2} \le \mu F_{Rz}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** KTU exam problems usually ask for the **single-support ZMP equation** — not the full QP. Memorise the Kajita formula in §3.2 Step 4.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 3-link planar humanoid model has links with $m_1 = 5$ kg (foot), $m_2 = 12$ kg (shank), $m_3 = 8$ kg (thigh), positioned vertically at $r_1 = 0.1$ m, $r_2 = 0.5$ m, $r_3 = 0.9$ m above the ground. Compute the CoM height.

**Solution:**

$$M = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 5 + 12 + 8 = 25 \text{ kg}$$

$$h_{CoM} = \frac{m_1 r_1 + m_2 r_2 + m_3 r_3}{M}$$

$$h_{CoM} = \frac{(5)(0.1) + (12)(0.5) + (8)(0.9)}{25}$$

$$h_{CoM} = \frac{0.5 + 6.0 + 7.2}{25} = \frac{13.7}{25} = 0.548 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{CoM} = 0.548 \text{ m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matches the empirical biomechanics rule: **human CoM is at ~55% of standing height from the ground**.

---

### 3.5 CoM Acceleration via Jacobian

**Setup:** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$. The position of link $i$'s CoM in world frame is $\mathbf{r}_{Ci}(\mathbf{q})$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate to get velocity:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_{Ci}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}_{Ci}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate again:**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci} = \mathbf{J}_{Ci}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}_{Ci}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 3 — Total CoM acceleration:**

$$\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci} = \left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_i m_i \mathbf{J}_{Ci}\right)\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_i m_i \dot{\mathbf{J}}_{Ci}\right)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

$$\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{J}_{CoM}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \dot{\mathbf{J}}_{CoM}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **the** formula used inside model-predictive controllers (MPC) for humanoid walking — e.g., the open-source **OCS2** framework used by ETH Zürich's ANYmal.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Ground Reaction Force (GRF) in the context of humanoid locomotion. List its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Ground Reaction Force is the force exerted by the ground on the robot's foot in accordance with Newton's third law when the foot is in contact with the ground.
- **Components (2 marks):**
  - Vertical component $F_z$ — supports the body weight.
  - Horizontal components $F_x$ and $F_y$ — provide propulsion / braking / lateral stabilisation.
  - (Plus associated moments $M_x, M_y, M_z$ about the contact point.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State and explain Kajita's stability criterion for bipedal walking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Criterion (2 marks):** A biped robot is dynamically stable during locomotion **if and only if the computed Zero Moment Point (ZMP) lies inside the support polygon** (the convex hull of all current contact points between the feet and the ground).
- **Explanation (1 mark):** If the ZMP exits the polygon, the moment from gravity and inertia can no longer be balanced by the foot's reaction, causing the foot to rotate about an edge — the robot falls.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Centre of Mass (CoM) position, velocity, and acceleration of a multi-link humanoid robot having $N$ rigid links. Show clearly that the total linear momentum equals $M\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the Newton-Euler linear momentum balance for a humanoid robot in single-support phase, derive the Ground Reaction Force (GRF) at the supporting foot. State and apply the friction cone constraint for the same. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CoM Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the humanoid have $N$ rigid links with masses $m_i$ and individual CoM positions $\mathbf{r}_{Ci}$ for $i=1,\dots,N$. Total mass $M = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i$.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Position:**
$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Velocity:**
$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_{Ci}\right)$$

Since $M$ is constant:

$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Linear momentum:**
The total linear momentum of the system is:

$$\mathbf{P} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci} = M\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$$

This proves $\mathbf{P} = M\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$ — a single 3-D vector captures the entire translational state of the robot.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Acceleration:**
Differentiate once more:

$$\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{Ci}$$

---

**(b) GRF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Newton's Law on whole robot:**
Apply linear momentum balance:

$$M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_c}\mathbf{F}_j + M\mathbf{g}$$

For single support, $n_c = 1$ and the only contact is the supporting foot at $\mathbf{p}_R$:

$$M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \mathbf{F}_R + M\mathbf{g}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Solve for GRF:**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{F}_R = M\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} - M\mathbf{g} = M(\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} - \mathbf{g})}$$

In components:

$$F_{Rx} = M\ddot{x}_{CoM}, \quad F_{Ry} = M\ddot{y}_{CoM}, \quad F_{Rz} = M(\ddot{z}_{CoM} + g)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Friction Cone Constraint:**
For the foot not to slip, the horizontal GRF magnitude must satisfy:

$$\sqrt{F_{Rx}^2 + F_{Ry}^2} \le \mu F_{Rz}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction at the foot-ground interface. Equivalently:

$$\sqrt{\ddot{x}_{CoM}^2 + \ddot{y}_{CoM}^2} \le \mu(\ddot{z}_{CoM} + g)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation:**
If the horizontal acceleration of the CoM is too large (e.g., during fast running), the inequality is violated → the foot slips. Walking controllers must therefore keep $\ddot{x}_{CoM}$ and $\ddot{y}_{CoM}$ small enough.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, define the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**. Derive the Kajita ZMP formula for a planar biped using the cart-table model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link planar biped has the following data:
- Link 1 (lower): $m_1 = 10$ kg, CoM at $r_1 = (0.2, 0.4)$ m
- Link 2 (upper): $m_2 = 15$ kg, CoM at $r_2 = (0.1, 0.8)$ m
- Acceleration of each CoM: $\ddot{x}_1 = 0.5$ m/s², $\ddot{z}_1 = 0$; $\ddot{x}_2 = 0.3$ m/s², $\ddot{z}_2 = 0$.

Compute the ZMP x-coordinate. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ZMP Definition and Cart-Table Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:**
The ZMP is the point on the ground $(p_x, p_y, 0)$ at which the net moment of the ground reaction force about the horizontal axes equals zero.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Cart-table model:**
A cart of mass $M$ slides on a massless table of height $h$. Forces: gravity $Mg$ downward, table reaction $\mathbf{F} = (F_x, 0, F_z)$ applied at the table-top.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Newton's law:**
$$M\ddot{x} = F_x, \quad M\ddot{z} = F_z - Mg$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Moment balance about $(p_x, 0, 0)$:**
$$M_y = -F_x h + M(\ddot{x} + g)(0 - p_x) = 0$$

Solve using $F_x = M\ddot{x}$:

$$-M\ddot{x} h - M(\ddot{x}+g)p_x = 0$$

$$p_x = -\frac{h\ddot{x}}{\ddot{x}+g}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Generalisation to multi-link:**
Replace single mass with $\sum m_i$ and you obtain the full Kajita formula quoted in §2.3.

---

**(b) Numerical ZMP Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m_1 = 10$ kg, $r_1 = (0.2, 0.4)$
- $m_2 = 15$ kg, $r_2 = (0.1, 0.8)$
- $\ddot{x}_1 = 0.5$, $\ddot{z}_1 = 0$
- $\ddot{x}_2 = 0.3$, $\ddot{z}_2 = 0$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Numerator $\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)x_i$:**

$$N_1 = m_1(\ddot{z}_1 + g)x_1 + m_2(\ddot{z}_2 + g)x_2$$
$$N_1 = 10(0 + 9.81)(0.2) + 15(0 + 9.81)(0.1)$$
$$N_1 = 10(9.81)(0.2) + 15(9.81)(0.1)$$
$$N_1 = 19.62 + 14.715 = 34.335$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Numerator $\sum m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i$:**

$$N_2 = m_1\ddot{x}_1 z_1 + m_2\ddot{x}_2 z_2$$
$$N_2 = 10(0.5)(0.4) + 15(0.3)(0.8)$$
$$N_2 = 2.0 + 3.6 = 5.6$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Denominator $\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)$:**

$$D = m_1(\ddot{z}_1+g) + m_2(\ddot{z}_2+g) = 10(9.81) + 15(9.81) = 25(9.81) = 245.25$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Final ZMP:**

$$p_x = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{D} = \frac{34.335 - 5.6}{245.25} = \frac{28.735}{245.25} \approx 0.1172 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Interpretation:**
$$p_x \approx 0.117 \text{ m} = 11.7 \text{ cm from the heel towards the toe}$$

If the foot length is e.g. 25 cm (heel at 0, toe at 0.25), the ZMP lies inside the foot → **robot is stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in ZMP formula** — Many students forget the minus sign between the two numerator terms; this gives a wildly wrong $p_x$. Write out the formula fully.
> 2. **Mixing up $\ddot{z}_i + g$ vs $g - \ddot{z}_i$** — Always substitute carefully. The vertical acceleration in this problem is zero, so it's just $g$, but in jumping motion $\ddot{z}$ is large and the sign matters.
> 3. **Forgetting units in friction cone** — friction coefficient $\mu$ is dimensionless; force components are in N.
> 4. **Confusing CoM with CoP (Center of Pressure)** — CoP = ZMP when foot is flat; CoM and CoP are *not* the same in general.
> 5. **Miscounting link masses** — double-check the summation; off-by-one error in mass index is a common deduction.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CoM** = mass-weighted average of all link positions; one 3-D point that captures translational state.
- **GRF** = Newton's 3rd-law reaction at foot; 3 force + 3 moment components.
- **ZMP** = point on ground where net horizontal moment of GRF = 0.
- **Kajita ZMP formula** (memorise!):
  $$p_x = \frac{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)x_i - \sum m_i\ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)}$$
- **Stability Criterion (Kajita):** ZMP inside **support polygon** ⟹ stable; outside ⟹ fall.
- **Friction cone:** $\sqrt{F_x^2+F_y^2} \le \mu F_z$ — foot does not slip.
- **Total linear momentum** = $M\dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$ — single 3-vector for whole robot.
- **Total angular momentum** about CoM = $\mathbf{H}_{CoM}$; its derivative is balanced by contact moments.
- **Joint-space dynamics equation:** $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \mathbf{S}^T\boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{J}_c^T\mathbf{F}_{GRF}$.
- **Floating-base issue:** 6 DOF of the trunk are passive; actuated through foot contacts only.
- **Quasi-static approximation:** Denominator $\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g) \approx Mg$ when vertical acceleration is small.
- **CoM Jacobian:** $\mathbf{J}_{CoM} = \frac{1}{M}\sum m_i \mathbf{J}_{Ci}$ — used in MPC-based walking controllers.
- **Practical humanoid masses:** 5 kg (NAO) to 80+ kg (Atlas); typical CoM at ~55% of height.
- **Use SI units throughout** (kg, m, s, N, N·m) — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit inconsistency.
- **Diagram clearly in exam** — draw free-body diagram, label GRF vectors at the foot, indicate ZMP on the ground.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anthropomorphic Characters Case Studies: Technical Details of the Following Humanoid Robots as Samples: Honda Robots (ASIMO), Sony Dog and Biped (mpg1, mpg2), Hiroshi Face Robot, Mitra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT744 - FUNDAMENTALS OF HUMANOID ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Anthropomorphic Characters Case Studies: Technical Details of the Following Humanoid Robots as Samples: Honda Robots (ASIMO), Sony Dog and Biped (mpg1, mpg2), Hiroshi Face Robot, Mitra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Anthropomorphic Character Case Studies?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Anthropomorphic characters* are physical robotic systems that exhibit human-like or animal-like form factors, behaviors, and locomotion patterns. *Case studies* in this context refer to the systematic technical documentation and engineering analysis of landmark historical and contemporary platforms (such as ASIMO, AIBO, Geminoid, and Mitra) to extract design principles, control philosophies, and architectural lessons applicable to next-generation humanoid research.

In simple terms, we are looking at the **"famous robots of the world"** — pulling them apart like a car mechanic strips down a classic engine — to understand how engineers actually built walking, talking, thinking machines. Just as medical students learn anatomy by studying real cadavers, robotics engineers learn *biomechatronics* by reverse-engineering these flagship platforms.

### 1.2 The Five Reference Platforms (At a Glance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five platforms collectively span the entire design space of anthropomorphic robotics: **full-body bipedal locomotion** (ASIMO), **quadruped emotional companionship** (AIBO), **small-scale bipedal entertainment** (Sony Dream Robot), **hyper-realistic facial mechatronics** (Ishiguro's Geminoid/Repliee), and **service-sector human interaction** (Mitra).

| # | Platform | Category | Year | Primary Purpose |
|---|----------|----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Honda **ASIMO** | Bipedal Humanoid | 2000 (public) | Assistant / Research |
| 2 | Sony **AIBO** (ERS series) | Quadruped Dog | 1999 | Companionship |
| 3 | Sony **SDR** (mpg1, mpg2) | Small Biped | 2000 / 2002 | Entertainment / R&D |
| 4 | **Hiroshi Face Robot** (Geminoid/Repliee) | Android Face | 2006+ | Telepresence / Human-Robot Interaction |
| 5 | **Mitra** | Service Humanoid | 2017 | Banking / Customer Service |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of these robots like the *evolution of the automobile*. ASIMO is the **Rolls-Royce** of humanoids — refined, powerful, and engineered to perfection. AIBO is the **family pet** (a robotic Labrador). The Sony SDR is the **concept car** — experimental, small, and stylish. The Ishiguro face robot is the **movie animatronic** on a university research budget. And Mitra is the **auto-rickshaw** — affordable, practical, and built for Indian roads (and Indian banks).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

### 2.1 Honda ASIMO — Detailed Technical Profile

ASIMO stands for **Advanced Step in Innovative Mobility**. The project began in **1986** under Honda's Wako Research Center and went through a series of prototypes (E0, E1, E2, ... E6, P1, P2, P3) before ASIMO was unveiled in **2000**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Innovation — The ZMP (Zero Moment Point) Controller:**
> ASIMO is famous for applying *ZMP-based stable walking*, originally theorized by Miomir Vukobratović in 1969. The ZMP is the point on the ground where the net moment of the inertial forces and the gravity forces has **zero** components along the horizontal axes.

#### Why this matters in plain English:
If you stand on one leg and tilt slightly, you will fall over. ASIMO solves this problem by constantly measuring where the *combined forces* of gravity and motion land on its feet, and *shifting* its body so that this landing point stays **inside the polygon of foot support** at all times. This is the *active balancing* trick that makes ASIMO look like it is gliding.

#### Hardware Specifications (2011 model — most cited in KTU papers):

- **Height:** 130 cm
- **Weight:** 48 kg
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** **57 DOF total** (head 3, arms 7×2, hands 13, legs 6×2, hip 2)
- **Walking speed:** up to **2.7 km/h** walking, **9 km/h** running
- **Actuators:** Servo motors with **Harmonic Drive** reducers
- **Sensors:**
  - 2× head-mounted cameras
  - 6-axis force/torque sensors at wrists and feet
  - Gyroscope + Accelerometer (Inertial Measurement Unit)
  - Ultrasonic and infrared floor-surface sensors
- **Power source:** Lithium-ion battery, ~1 hour operation
- **Intelligence:** Onboard PC + 3 back-mounted processors

#### Walking Control Hierarchy:
1. **High-level planner** — decides footstep location.
2. **ZMP reference generator** — sets the *desired* ZMP trajectory.
3. **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** — calculates joint trajectories.
4. **Low-level servo control** — drives each joint to track trajectory.

---

### 2.2 Sony AIBO — The Robotic Dog

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AIBO (Artificial Intelligence roBOt, also meaning "pal" in Japanese) was the **world's first mass-market consumer entertainment robot**, released in 1999.

#### Hardware Generations:
- **ERS-110 (1999)** — 18 DOF, 1 MIPS CPU
- **ERS-210 (2001)** — 20 DOF, embedded emotion engine
- **ERS-7 (2003)** — 20 DOF, Wi-Fi enabled
- **ERS-1000 / ERS-1000III (2018 revival)** — 22 DOF, OLED eyes, deep learning

#### OPEN-R Architecture:
Sony's AIBO uses a modular **OPEN-R** bus architecture where each actuator, sensor, and processor is a *physical and software object* with a standardized interface. This allowed third-party developers to add new behaviors.

#### Sensors on AIBO ERS-7:
- CCD color camera (in nose)
- Stereo microphones
- Touch sensors (head, chin, back, paws)
- Infrared distance sensor
- Acceleration sensor
- Temperature sensor

#### Why AIBO is historically important:
- Pioneered **affective computing** in a consumer product.
- Used a probabilistic **Markov Decision Process** for behavior selection.
- Re-introduced Sony to AI after the AIBO project was discontinued in 2006 and revived in 2018 with deep-learning-based models.

---

### 2.3 Sony Dream Robot (SDR) — Biped "mpg1" and "mpg2"

> [!NOTE]
> The Sony Dream Robot (SDR) was a research biped series, distinct from AIBO. "mpg" is a label used in some KTU references to denote the *motion-planning generation* prototypes.

#### SDR-3X (also loosely called "mpg1" in academic literature):
- Released in 2000
- **Height:** 50 cm
- **Weight:** 5 kg
- **DOF:** 24
- **Locomotion:** Real-time adaptive walking on flat surfaces
- **Sensors:** CCD cameras, microphones, balance gyro
- **Communication:** Speech recognition + speech synthesis

#### SDR-4X (loosely "mpg2"):
- Released 2002
- Height: 58 cm
- Weight: 6.5 kg
- DOF: 28
- **Key advances:**
  - **Real-Time Integrated Motion Control (RT-MIO)** — blends stable walking, body balancing, and arm gestures.
  - Memory-stick-based software for personality upgrade.
  - Improved **USLOT** (Universal Socket for Linking Object and Tactic) — modular hardware/software framework similar to AIBO's OPEN-R.
  - Could walk on uneven surfaces and recover from pushes.

#### The Engineering Lesson from SDR:
SDR was the *bridge* between AIBO's small-scale mechatronics and full humanoids. It proved that **bipedal control algorithms could be shrunk** to fit in a 5 kg chassis.

---

### 2.4 Hiroshi Ishiguro's Face Robot (Geminoid & Repliee Series)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Hiroshi Ishiguro* (石黒浩), formerly of ATR and now Osaka University, is famous for building **androids** — robots that look like specific human beings, including a copy of *himself* called **Geminoid HI-1** (2006).

#### Two Major Lines:
- **Repliee series** (Repliee R1, Q1, Q1Expo, Q2) — modeled after Japanese women.
- **Geminoid series** (HI-1, HI-2, HI-4) — modeled after Hiroshi Ishiguro himself.

#### Face Robot Technical Details:
- **Skin:** Silicone rubber with embedded tactile sensors.
- **Actuation:** Pneumatic (compressed air) McKibben-style artificial muscles and small servo motors.
- **DOF in the face:** approximately **12-14** (eyes, eyelids, eyebrows, lips, jaw, neck).
- **Air supply:** External compressor (a major limitation in earlier versions).
- **Control:** Servo-pneumatic closed-loop with position feedback.
- **Materials behind the skin:** A metal / plastic skull-shaped frame.

#### The "Uncanny Valley" Connection:
Mori's 1970 *Uncanny Valley* hypothesis was tested empirically using Repliee Q2. Ishiguro's research showed that **as appearance becomes more human, micro-movements of the face become the determining factor** of perceived realism. A perfectly still human-looking face is *more* disturbing than a clearly mechanical one.

#### Telepresence Application:
The Geminoid HI series is used for **remote teaching** — Ishiguro can deliver a lecture from Tokyo while his android body stands in a remote university in another country, and an operator (or Ishiguro himself via network) drives its movements.

---

### 2.5 Mitra — India's Service Humanoid

> [!NOTE]
> *Mitra* (meaning "friend" in Sanskrit/Hindi) is a humanoid robot developed by **Invento Robotics**, a Bangalore-based startup founded by Balaji Viswanathan. It was unveiled in 2017 at the Global Entrepreneurship Summit in Hyderabad.

#### Technical Profile:
- **Height:** approximately **5 feet (≈ 152 cm)**
- **Weight:** ~ 30 kg
- **Mobility:** Wheeled base (not bipedal)
- **Body:** 3D-printed white plastic shell
- **Chest display:** Built-in **touchscreen** for UI interaction
- **Head:** Large rounded display showing animated eyes/face

#### Sensors and I/O:
- 2× RGB cameras for face detection
- Microphone array for voice input
- Speakers for speech output (TTS)
- Proximity sensors on the base
- Touchscreen (capacitive) on chest

#### Software Stack:
- Speech recognition (uses cloud-based ASR like Google Speech)
- Natural Language Processing for intent detection
- Face recognition via deep CNNs
- Pre-scripted conversational flows for banking
- Real-time operating system (RTOS) for motion + dialogue

#### Real-World Deployment:
- Used in **ICICI Bank** branches (2017) to greet customers, answer FAQs, and handle basic navigation in the lobby.
- Deployed at the **2017 GES Summit**, the **2018 Indian Science Congress**, and various conferences.
- Featured as one of the *TIME* magazine's "Best Inventions of 2017."

#### Limitations (from a KTU-exam-relevant viewpoint):
- No manipulator arm in early versions (later versions added a 5-DOF arm).
- Wheeled, so cannot navigate stairs.
- Dependent on cloud connectivity for most of its intelligence.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Parameter | ASIMO | AIBO | SDR (SDR-4X) | Ishiguro Face | Mitra |
|-----------|-------|------|--------------|---------------|-------|
| **Form factor** | Bipedal humanoid | Quadruped dog | Small biped | Head + torso | Wheeled humanoid |
| **DOF** | 57 | 18-22 | 28 | 12-14 face | 5-6 |
| **Height** | 130 cm | ~ 28 cm | 58 cm | Head only | 152 cm |
| **Year** | 2000+ | 1999+ | 2000-2002 | 2006+ | 2017 |
| **Primary actuator** | Servo + harmonic drive | Servo motors | Servo motors | Pneumatic + servo | Servo |
| **Control approach** | ZMP + MPC | Behavior-based | RT-MIO | Telepresence / scripted | Cloud + script |
| **Domain** | Service / research | Companion | R&D | HRI / uncanny valley | Banking service |
| **Country** | Japan | Japan | Japan | Japan | India |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — ASIMO Walking Stability (KTU Numericals)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the ZMP concept using a simple 2-link inverted pendulum model of humanoid walking. The following derivation is the *standard answer* pattern expected by KTU examiners.

### 3.1 The Zero Moment Point (ZMP) Derivation

#### Step 1: Define the system
Consider a single foot of a humanoid robot in contact with the ground. The foot is treated as a rigid body. Let:
- $m$ = total mass of the robot (kg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $F$ = ground reaction force
- $P_{ZMP}$ = the ZMP coordinates $(p_x, p_y)$ on the ground plane
- $\tau_x, \tau_y$ = moments about the X and Y axes

#### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
The total force equilibrium (vertical) is:

$$F_z = m \cdot g$$

#### Step 3: Apply moment equilibrium about the ZMP
By definition, the moment about the ZMP is **zero**:

$$\tau_x^{ZMP} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_y^{ZMP} = 0$$

#### Step 4: Express the moments
Taking the center of mass (CoM) at position $(x_{com}, y_{com}, z_{com})$ and the ZMP at $(p_x, p_y, 0)$, the moment about the ZMP is:

$$\tau_x^{ZMP} = m \cdot g \cdot (x_{com} - p_x) - m \cdot \ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com} = 0$$

#### Step 5: Solve for the ZMP coordinate $p_x$
Rearranging:

$$p_x = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g}$$

#### Step 6: Similarly for the y-axis:

$$p_y = y_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{y}_{com}}{g}$$

#### Step 7: Interpretation
- The ZMP is **inside** the support polygon → robot is stable.
- The ZMP **leaves** the support polygon → robot tips over.
- For ASIMO, a real-time **preview control** algorithm adjusts the CoM trajectory so the predicted ZMP tracks a reference ZMP that lies inside the foot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** When asked "Why does ASIMO not fall while walking?", the model answer is:
> *"ASIMO continuously measures the ground reaction force through 6-axis force sensors at its feet, computes the actual ZMP, and uses a model-predictive controller to shift its center of mass so the ZMP remains inside the convex hull of its support foot. This active control compensates for the inherently unstable dynamics of a bipedal inverted pendulum."*

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Degrees of Freedom by Robot

| Robot | Head | Arms (each) | Hands (each) | Legs (each) | Torso / Pelvis | Total |
|-------|------|-------------|--------------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| ASIMO (2011) | 3 | 7 | 13 | 6 | 2 | 57 |
| AIBO ERS-7 | 3 | 4 | 0 | 4 (per leg) | 1 | 20 |
| SDR-4X | 2 | 5 | 3 | 6 | 1 | 28 |
| Ishiguro Geminoid (face) | 12-14 | — | — | — | 2 (neck) | ~16 |
| Mitra (v1) | 2 | 2 (small arm) | 0 | 0 (wheeled) | 1 | 5 |

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark KTU questions, the most tested *single number* is **ASIMO's 57 DOF** and **AIBO ERS-110's release year 1999**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ASIMO System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Task Planner] --> nodeB[Footstep Planner]
    nodeB --> nodeC[ZMP Reference Generator]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Model Predictive Controller]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Joint Trajectory Generator]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Servo Drivers]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Harmonic Drive Actuators]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Robot Joints]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Force Torque Sensors at Feet]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[State Estimator]
    nodeJ --> nodeD
    nodeK[Cameras and IMU] --> nodeJ
    nodeL[Onboard PC] --> nodeA
    nodeL --> nodeB
    nodeL --> nodeC
```

*Caption:* Closed-loop architecture of ASIMO's walking controller. The **ZMP feedback loop** is the defining engineering choice that makes ASIMO stable.

---

### 4.2 AIBO OPEN-R Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[OPEN-R Bus] --> nodeB[CPU Main MIPS or ARM]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Actuator Modules]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Sensor Modules]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Behavior Engine]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Servo Joints 20 DOF]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Camera CCD]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Touch Sensors]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Microphone Array]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Infrared Sensor]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Emotion Engine]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Instinct Module]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Learning Module]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Action Selection MDP]
```

*Caption:* AIBO's modular hardware/software architecture using the OPEN-R bus. The Emotion Engine is the iconic *affective* component.

---

### 4.3 Ishiguro Geminoid Pneumatic Face — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Operator Input or Telepresence Stream] --> nodeB[Controller PC]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pneumatic Valve Manifold]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Servo Controller for Eyes]
    nodeC --> nodeE[McKibben Air Muscles in Face]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Eye Mechanism]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Silicone Skin Deformation]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Visual and Tactile Feedback]
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeI[External Air Compressor] --> nodeC
```

*Caption:* The Geminoid face robot uses a *hybrid* actuation strategy: pneumatics for large skin deformation, servos for precise eye movement.

---

### 4.4 Mitra Service Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[User Speech Input] --> nodeB[Microphone Array]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Cloud ASR]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Intent Classifier NLP]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Dialogue Manager]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Response Generator]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Text to Speech TTS]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Speaker Output]
    nodeI[User Face] --> nodeJ[RGB Camera]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Face Recognition CNN]
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeL[Chest Touchscreen UI]
    nodeM[Wheeled Base Controller] --> nodeN[Drive Motors]
    nodeE --> nodeM
```

*Caption:* Mitra's service architecture is *cloud-dependent*, which is both its strength (rapid upgrade) and its weakness (no internet = limited function).

---

### 4.5 Comparison of Architectural Philosophies

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[ASIMO ZMP MPC Closed Loop] --> nodeX[Stability First]
    nodeB[AIBO Behavior Based MDP] --> nodeY[Adaptation First]
    nodeC[SDR Integrated Motion RTMIO] --> nodeZ[Compactness First]
    nodeD[Ishiguro Pneumatic Face] --> nodeW[Realism First]
    nodeE[Mitra Cloud Scripted] --> nodeV[Service Utility First]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the five anthropomorphic robot case studies mentioned in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Categorize each by its locomotion type (bipedal, quadruped, wheeled, etc.).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Robot | Locomotion Type |
|-------|-----------------|
| Honda ASIMO | Bipedal walking / running |
| Sony AIBO | Quadruped walking |
| Sony SDR (mpg1, mpg2) | Bipedal small-scale walking |
| Hiroshi Ishiguro Face Robot | Stationary (head + neck) |
| Mitra | Wheeled base + articulated arm |

(1 mark: names, 1 mark: locomotion, 1 mark: correct mapping)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the meaning of the acronym "ASIMO"? State the number of degrees of freedom of the latest ASIMO model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- ASIMO = **Advanced Step in Innovative Mobility** (2 marks)
- Latest ASIMO has **57 degrees of freedom** (1 mark)

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 marks)**

**Q A(a). [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain the ZMP-based stable walking control of Honda ASIMO. Include the derivation of the ZMP coordinate and its physical significance.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction to ZMP** (1 mark)
The Zero Moment Point (ZMP), proposed by Vukobratović (1969), is the point on the ground at which the net moment of the inertial forces and gravity forces acting on the robot has no horizontal component. It is the **gold-standard metric** for bipedal walking stability.

2. **Derivation** (3 marks)
Considering a single-support phase, with the CoM at $(x_{com}, y_{com}, z_{com})$:

$$p_x = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g}$$

$$p_y = y_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{y}_{com}}{g}$$

Derivation follows from moment equilibrium about the ZMP being zero (full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes).

3. **Application in ASIMO** (2 marks)
ASIMO uses **6-axis force/torque sensors at the feet** to compute the *actual* ZMP, then a **model-predictive controller** adjusts the CoM trajectory such that the *predicted* ZMP tracks a reference ZMP inside the foot's support polygon. ASIMO also uses a *preview control* (Kajita's method) using future footstep information 1-2 seconds ahead.

4. **Stability Criterion** (1 mark)
If the computed ZMP lies inside the convex hull of the support foot (or feet, in double support), the robot is **dynamically stable**. If the ZMP reaches the foot edge, the robot must take a recovery step or it will fall.

---

**Q A(b). [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Compare the Sony AIBO ERS-7 and the Sony Dream Robot SDR-4X in terms of: (i) form factor, (ii) degrees of freedom, (iii) sensors, (iv) software architecture, and (v) intended application.*

**Model Solution (tabular, 7 marks — 1.4 marks per point):**

| Parameter | AIBO ERS-7 | SDR-4X |
|-----------|------------|--------|
| **Form factor** | Quadruped dog-like | Small bipedal humanoid |
| **DOF** | 20 | 28 |
| **Sensors** | CCD camera, stereo mics, touch, IR, accelerometer, temperature | CCD cameras, mics, balance gyro, force sensors |
| **Architecture** | OPEN-R modular bus | USLOT (Universal Socket for Linking Object and Tactic) |
| **Application** | Emotional companionship, pet simulation | Bipedal walking research, entertainment |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** AIBO focused on *affective behavior* in a 4-legged form, while SDR pushed *bipedal control algorithms* into a compact humanoid form factor.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 marks)**

**Q B(a). [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Describe the Hiroshi Ishiguro Geminoid/Repliee series of face robots. Discuss: (i) appearance and skin material, (ii) actuation method, (iii) degrees of freedom, (iv) the Uncanny Valley contribution, and (v) telepresence application.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Appearance and Skin (1.5 marks):** Silicone-rubber skin molded over a rigid skull-like frame. The skin contains embedded **tactile sensors** to register contact.

2. **Actuation (1.5 marks):** *Hybrid* — pneumatic **McKibben artificial muscles** for large skin deformation (cheeks, lips, brow), and small **DC servo motors** for eye, eyelid, and neck movement. Pneumatic supply comes from an external compressor.

3. **DOF (1 mark):** Approximately **12-14 in the face** (eyes 2, eyelids 2, eyebrows 2, lips 4, jaw 1, neck 2-3), making about **16 DOF total** including the neck.

4. **Uncanny Valley Contribution (1.5 marks):** Ishiguro's empirical studies with Repliee Q2 showed that *micro-movements* (subtle blinks, breathing, gaze shift) are the dominant factor in perceiving a humanoid as "alive." Static human-like faces are perceived as eerie; **animated** human-like faces cross into acceptance.

5. **Telepresence Application (1.5 marks):** Geminoid HI-1 (a copy of Ishiguro himself) is used for remote lecturing. An operator (or Ishiguro via network) drives the android in real time, allowing the android to give a lecture in a distant location while Ishiguro's presence is "tele-transported."

---

**Q B(b). [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Discuss the Indian humanoid "Mitra" — its hardware, sensors, software stack, and real-world deployments. Mention at least two limitations of the design.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Hardware (2 marks):**
- Height ~5 ft, weight ~30 kg
- Wheeled base (omni-directional)
- 3D-printed white plastic shell
- Chest-mounted **capacitive touchscreen**
- Head display for animated facial expressions

2. **Sensors (1.5 marks):**
- Two RGB cameras for face detection
- Microphone array for voice input
- Proximity sensors for obstacle avoidance
- Touchscreen for direct user input

3. **Software Stack (2 marks):**
- Cloud-based **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** (Google Speech API)
- NLP-based **intent classification**
- **Face recognition** using deep CNN
- **Dialogue manager** with pre-scripted flows for banking
- **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** for responses

4. **Real-World Deployments (1 mark):**
- **ICICI Bank** branches (2017) — customer greeter, FAQ
- **Global Entrepreneurship Summit 2017**, Hyderabad
- **Indian Science Congress 2018**
- Listed in *TIME*'s Best Inventions of 2017

5. **Limitations (0.5 mark each, any two):**
- **Cloud dependency** — loses intelligence offline.
- **Wheeled base** — cannot climb stairs.
- **Limited manipulation** — early version had no arm; later added 5-DOF arm.
- **No real-time bipedal walking** — design choice for cost and stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Spelling pitfall:** Many students write *"ASIMO"* as *"ASIMA"* or *"ASEMO"* — both are *zero-mark* answers. The correct spelling is **A-I-S-I-M-O**.
> 2. **Number pitfall:** ASIMO's DOF is **57** (not 50, not 47, not 60). Mixing this up loses full marks on DOF comparison questions.
> 3. **Concept pitfall:** In ZMP questions, students often write "ZMP is the center of mass" — this is **wrong**. The ZMP is on the *ground*, the CoM is on the *body*. The relationship between them is given by the equation in Section 3.1.
> 4. **AIBO confusion:** Students sometimes confuse AIBO (dog) with Sony Dream Robot (biped). They are **two different platforms**.
> 5. **Mitra origin:** Many students wrongly attribute Mitra to IIT Madras or a government lab. Mitra is by **Invento Robotics**, a Bangalore startup founded by **Balaji Viswanathan**.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **ASIMO** = Advanced Step in Innovative Mobility; 57 DOF; ZMP-based stable walking; 130 cm tall; uses harmonic-drive servo actuators.
- ✅ **ASIMO walking control** is a 4-level hierarchy: Task Planner → Footstep Planner → ZMP Generator → MPC → Servos. The **ZMP must stay inside the foot support polygon**.
- ✅ **AIBO** = Sony's robotic dog; ERS-110 launched 1999; uses **OPEN-R** bus; **ERS-7 has 20 DOF**; pioneered consumer affective computing.
- ✅ **Sony Dream Robot (SDR)** = biped research; SDR-3X (2000) 24 DOF; **SDR-4X (2002) 28 DOF**; uses **USLOT** architecture; introduced **Real-Time Integrated Motion Control (RT-MIO)**.
- ✅ **Hiroshi Ishiguro Face Robot** = Geminoid HI series + Repliee series; **silicone skin**, **pneumatic McKibben muscles + servo** hybrid actuation; ~12-14 face DOF; famous for **Uncanny Valley** studies and **telepresence**.
- ✅ **Mitra** = Indian humanoid by **Invento Robotics**; ~5 ft tall; **wheeled base**; chest touchscreen; cloud-dependent ASR/NLP; deployed in **ICICI Bank** and **GES 2017**.
- ✅ **ZMP formula** to memorize:
$$p_x = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g}, \quad p_y = y_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{y}_{com}}{g}$$
- ✅ **Five key dates** to remember: **1986** (Honda E0), **1999** (AIBO ERS-110), **2000** (SDR-3X, ASIMO unveil), **2002** (SDR-4X), **2006** (Geminoid HI-1), **2017** (Mitra).
- ✅ **Architectural philosophies:** ASIMO = *stability*, AIBO = *affect*, SDR = *compactness*, Ishiguro = *realism*, Mitra = *service utility*.
- ✅ **One-line exam punchline:** *"ASIMO walks by chasing its own ZMP, AIBO learns by feeling, SDR walks to shrink ASIMO, Ishiguro's face teaches us about the Uncanny Valley, and Mitra brings humanoid service to Indian banking."*
